Flop K♥ 8♣ 6♠ I checked as planned — I have only Ace‑high with runner‑runner club possibilities (Ac3c + 8c on board = backdoor clubs) and an Ace blocker. Claude leads for 600 into a 1500 pot (~40% pot). Calling 600 to win a 2700 pot after my call requires ≈22% equity. Against Claude’s likely c‑bet range (Kx, medium pairs, some draws) my immediate equity is modest but in the ballpark to justify a call given the small size and my blocker/backdoor outs. Raising as a bluff OOP multiway is thin (likely to be called or 3‑bet and will bloat the pot out of position), and folding is too tight given the price. I’ll call the 600, keep the pot controlled, and re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to significant turn aggression unless I improve or get a clear read.

On a board of Kh-8c-6s, I have Queen-high with two overcards to the 8 and 6. Claude (who limped pre-flop) has bet $600, and Minni (the pre-flop raiser) has called that bet. This sequence of action suggests that both players likely have a piece of this board. Claude's lead into two players after the aggressor checked often indicates at least a pair, and Minni's call confirms she is not folding easily. With only Queen-high and no real draws other than weak overcards that might not even be good if they hit (since a King is on board), I do not have the equity to continue. Folding is the most profitable decision here.

Board: 8♣ 6♠ K♥ 4♥. I hold A♣3♣. After calling Claude’s 600 flop C-bet I’m now heads-up and the turn is a blank for me. Critical points: the turn 4♥ kills any realistic backdoor flush — with only one club on the board now I cannot make a five-card club flush by river. My only clean outs are three Aces and three Threes (≈6 outs, ~13% to improve on the river). Pot is 2,100 and both stacks are deep relative to the pot. Betting or bluffing OOP here will bloat the pot when I’m likely behind (Claude’s flop lead is consistent with Kx, middle pairs or strong draws) and will often get called. Raising is too thin and unnecessary. Priority is pot control and keeping options for the river. I’ll check the turn to see the river card for free; plan for river: if Claude checks I’ll reassess (likely check down), if he bets small (roughly ≤ ~350–400) I can call given the price; if he fires a larger sizeable bet I’ll fold this unimproved Ace‑high. If I pair the Ace or the 3 on the river I will switch to value‑bet/call accordingly.

Betting 900 on the turn with top pair (KT on a K-8-6-4 board). Minni has checked twice, indicating weakness. The bet extracts value from draws and weaker holdings while maintaining positional pressure. The sizing (43% of pot) balances value extraction with pot control on a board that now contains two hearts (potential flush draws). This is the natural aggressive play in position against a checking opponent.

Board: 8c 6s Kh 4h; I hold Ac 3c. Claude bets 900 into a 3000 pot (to call 900). Calling 900 would require ~23.1% equity (900 / (3000+900)). I have only 6 clean outs (three Aces + three Threes) to pair on the river — ~13% chance — well below the break-even. Claude’s line (calling preflop, leading the flop for 600, firing again on the turn) is weighted toward value (Kx, middle pairs, possibly sets), so his bluff frequency is likely low and even a river Ace may still lose to Kx. Raising OOP as a bluff is thin: I block some AK combos with the Ace but not enough to make a raise + riversize profitable, and it risks bloating a pot where I’m a big underdog. With deep stacks and many future profitable spots, preserving chips is correct. I fold this unimproved Ace-high.

I have 3♠8♦ on the button in a 3-handed pot. This is a very weak, non-connected offsuit hand that is dominated by virtually any hand the blinds will defend with. To call 100 into a 150 pot requires roughly 40% equity to break even (100/(150+100)=40%), which 3-8o does not have versus typical SB/BB defending ranges. Raising as a light steal is possible in three-handed play, but a min-raise to 200 is small and likely to be 3-bet or called, putting me in difficult postflop spots with a trash hand. With a healthy stack and many better spots to exploit later, the +EV play is to fold and wait for a stronger holding.

With the button folding, I am in the Small Blind with pocket sixes (6h 6d). This is a strong hand heads-up against the Big Blind. Standard strategy dictates a raise here to both build a pot with a range advantage and potentially take down the blinds immediately. I will raise to 300, which is 3x the big blind.

I'm in the BB facing Flash's raise to 300 from the SB. I'm holding J8o, which is a marginal but playable hand. Key factors: (1) I'm getting 3:1 pot odds (600 in pot after I call 200), which is favorable for a hand with reasonable equity; (2) J8o has approximately 40-45% equity against Flash's wide SB raising range; (3) Flash is short-stacked with only 6,050 chips and is essentially committed if action continues; (4) Heads-up play with a short stack means I can extract value postflop with decent hands. Calling is the mathematically sound choice here - I'm making a +EV decision that maximizes my expected value over the course of 150 hands.

I have flopped quads (four of a kind sixes). This is an incredibly strong hand that essentially has 100% equity against any possible range Claude could have. My objective now is to build the pot as much as possible without scaring Claude away. Since the board is pair-heavy (6-10-6), Claude might be skeptical of my holding or could have a Ten. If I check, I allow him to potentially bluff or catch a card on the turn to give him a second-best hand. However, leading for a small amount can also induce calls or raises from a Ten or pocket pairs. Given the pot is 600, I will check to see if Claude takes a stab at it, maintaining a deceptive line.

I'm holding J8o on a paired 6-6-T board. This is a weak hand with minimal equity. Flash just checked from the SB, suggesting either he whiffed or is being cautious. Betting here doesn't make sense—my hand is too weak to bet for value, and if Flash has any pair, I'm in bad shape. Checking keeps the pot manageable and allows me to see the turn card inexpensively, which might improve my hand (hitting a J would give me top pair). This is a prudent approach to minimize losses with marginal equity.

I have quad sixes, which is the absolute nuts on this board. Since I checked the flop to trap, and a third spade has now appeared on the turn, I will check again to induce a bluff from Claude or to let him bet if he has turned a flush or a Ten. Checking remains the most deceptive play here to maximize value, as I want Claude to commit chips with a second-best hand or a pure bluff.

Holding J8o (Jack-high with a gutshot) on a paired 6-6 board with a 10 and deuce, I face Flash's second check. My hand is too weak to bet for value or as a semi-bluff. Flash's two checks suggest weakness, but my hand doesn't have sufficient equity to justify a bet. Checking allows me to see the river for free and potentially improve, while keeping the pot manageable. Flash is short-stacked at 6,050 chips and I have position for the river—checking is the disciplined play that minimizes losses while preserving my option to call a river bet if needed.

I have the absolute nuts with quad sixes (6h 6d 6c 6s). The board run-out now includes three spades (Ts, 2s, 4s). I have checked the flop and turn to trap Claude, and he has also checked back both streets. On the river, there is now a possible flush and a possible straight. I need to get some value from this hand. Since Claude has shown zero interest in betting, I will lead out with a bet. A bet of around half the pot (300) looks like a thin value bet or a blocker bet, which might induce a call from a Ten, a pocket pair, or even an Ace-high that doesn't believe I have the flush or the six.

On the river with the final board 6c-Ts-6s-2s-4s, I'm holding Js-8h, which gives me a Jack-high spade flush (Js-Ts-6s-4s-2s). This is an extremely strong hand. Flash's pre-flop raise, subsequent checks on flop and turn, followed by this river bet of 300 suggests he likely has a medium-strength hand rather than a premium spade flush (which would require A♠ or K♠). Even accounting for a small probability Flash has one of those hands, I'm getting excellent pot odds: 4:1 (risking 300 to win 1,200). My expected value is strongly positive here. A straightforward call wins me this pot in the vast majority of scenarios. With Flash short-stacked at 5,750, there's no reason to overcomplicate this with a raise.

I am on the button with K7o. This is a marginal hand, but standard practice on the button in a three-handed game is to open a relatively wide range to steal the blinds. K7o is strong enough to open for a raise, typically around 3x the big blind, to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take the pot down pre-flop. Claude and Minni have both been active, but a standard 300 chip open is a disciplined play here.

Calling with Ad9c from small blind. The hand is above average and gets 1.8:1 pot odds. Flash's aggressive pattern and my stack depth make this a profitable call. Waiting to see Minni's action before committing more chips. A simple call maximizes information gathering and pot control.

I’m on the big blind with 7♣3♣ facing a raise to 300 (Button) and a call from the SB — a three-way pot. 7-3 suited is a very weak hand: poor showdown value, frequently dominated, and has low playability multiway. Pot odds to call 200 into 700 (~4.5:1) require ~22% equity, but versus a raiser’s opening range and a caller’s calling range my true equity is typically well below that in a three-way spot. A light 3-bet bluff here is thin (would cost significantly more and risks isolation or a 4-bet), and playing postflop out of position against two opponents is unattractive. With deep stacks and plenty of hands to wait for, the correct, risk-averse play is to fold and preserve chips for better spots.

I'm heads-up against Flash on a Qs7s9h board with Ad9c. I have a pair of nines with an ace kicker - a medium-strength hand that's vulnerable. Given that I'm out of position (small blind) and Flash is aggressive, checking is the prudent play here. This allows me to: (1) control the pot size with a moderately strong but not premium hand, (2) see what Flash does - whether they're continuation betting or checking, and (3) avoid overcommitting before seeing additional cards. Flash has limited stack depth (6650), so I can reassess and apply pressure on later streets if my hand remains strong. Checking also keeps Flash's wider range honest and gives me flexibility to call or raise based on their action.

On the flop of Qs 7s 9h, I have middle pair (sevens) with a King kicker. Claude checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise from the small blind. While the board is quite connected with potential straight and flush draws (86, JT, spade draws), my hand has decent showdown value and some equity. I will bet for protection and value, as Claude might call with draws or worse hands like lower pairs. A bet of around half the pot (350) is standard here to define my hand and keep the initiative.

Raising to 900 on the flop with Ad9c (pair of nines with ace kicker) on Qs7s9h. Flash's modest raise to 350 suggests a continuation bet rather than a strong hand. My pair is likely ahead of Flash's range (broadway cards, draws, weaker pairs). Flash is short-stacked at 6,300, so applying pressure while ahead is optimal. Being out of position argues for building the pot now rather than playing passively on later streets where I'll have less control. This raise extracts value when I'm likely ahead and forces Flash to make a tough decision with limited chips. Excellent spot to take down the pot or build it while in favorable shape.

On a board of Qs-7s-9h, I have middle pair (7s) with a King kicker. After I made a continuation bet of 350, Claude raised to 900. This is a relatively small raise (an additional 550 into a pot that will be 2500 after I call). Given my pot odds are roughly 22%, I have enough equity to continue, especially since I have position. Claude's range here could include strong hands like Q9, 97, or 77, but it also contains many draws like JT, 86, or spade flush draws. I'll call to see a turn card and re-evaluate based on the action and board texture.

Betting 2000 into the 2500 pot on the turn with two pair (99 77 A). Flash checked, indicating a weak/marginal hand (likely a draw or weaker pair). The 7h on the turn improves my hand while being less likely to help Flash's likely drawing/marginal range. This bet extracts maximum value while putting Flash to a tough decision at 2:1 pot odds. Flash's short stack (5750) and the aggressive action suggests they're not folding equity, making value extraction optimal. Checking would be too passive here and risks giving Flash a free card with their likely incomplete hand.

On the turn, I have improved to three of a kind (trip sevens) with a King kicker. The board is quite coordinated with potential straights (JT) and full houses (99, Q7, 97), but my hand is extremely strong in a three-handed game. Claude's check-raise on the flop followed by a large lead on the turn indicates a lot of strength, representing either a made hand like a straight or a set, or a strong draw. The seven on the turn makes it less likely he holds a seven himself (Q7, 97, 77). Folding is not an option given the strength of my hand and the pot odds. I will call to keep his bluffs and weaker value hands (like a Queen) in the pot, and to evaluate the river. Raising would likely only commit me against hands that have me beat.

I have two pair (nines and sevens with ace kicker) on a Qs7s9h7h3d river board versus Flash who has 3750 chips remaining.

Flash's action pattern throughout this hand strongly indicates a weaker hand:
1. Pre-flop: Flash raised to 300 (standard raise)
2. Flop: Flash made a small probe bet of 350 after checking, suggesting weakness or a continuation bet
3. Turn: Flash called my 2000 bet without aggression, indicating they don't have a monster hand

Flash is now at 3750 chips remaining after calling my 2000 turn bet. Their calling pattern suggests they have a marginal hand (likely broadway cards like KQ/KJ, or a weaker pair) rather than a strong made hand.

My two pair is a strong, nearly nuts-type hand on this runout. The river card (3d) was a blank that doesn't improve most of Flash's likely holdings.

Betting strategy:
- I have the best hand with high confidence
- Flash is short-stacked and committed to the pot
- Flash will likely call a reasonable value bet with their hand
- Checking would miss value extraction opportunity

I'll bet 2500, which:
1. Gets called by Flash's likely calling range (broadway pairs, weaker pairs)
2. Puts Flash at 1250 after calling, applying maximum pressure
3. Represents solid value while not overplaying
4. Extracts chips while ahead with the strongest hand

On the river, the board is Qs-7s-9h-7h-3d. I have trip sevens with a King kicker (K7). Claude has been aggressive throughout the hand, check-raising the flop and leading large on the turn and river. While his line could represent a full house (99, QQ, 97, Q7) or a straight (JT), the pot odds are very favorable. I need to call 2500 to win a total pot of 11,450, meaning I only need to be right about 22% of the time. In a three-handed game, trip sevens are extremely strong, and Claude could be bluffing with missed draws like spades or an open-ended straight draw (86). Raising is not advisable as it would only be called by hands that beat me. Therefore, calling is the most profitable decision.

With 2c 5d on the button, I'm holding one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While position is valuable, this hand lacks any real value - it's not a pair, has no broadway cards, and has poor connectivity.

Current situation analysis:
- Minni (small blind) has 19825 chips - solid stack, aggressive player
- Flash (big blind) has 12650 chips - reasonable stack
- I have 7375 chips - need to be selective
- To call costs 100 with 150 in the pot (1.5:1 odds)

Even with favorable position and decent pot odds for a blind, 2-5 offsuit is not a profitable play. The hand is too weak to play profitably against two opponents who will likely defend their blinds and punish a weak holding. I need to preserve my stack and wait for premium hands where I have a real edge.

Fold is the mathematically correct decision.

Heads-up from the small blind with 6♦5♠ — a marginal but playable, connected hand. In heads-up play I should be opening extremely wide; raising takes the initiative and leverages my post‑flop position (small blind is button heads‑up). I’ll raise to the min (200 total) to apply pressure and steal the BB while keeping the pot manageable with a speculative holding. If called I can c‑bet many flops and use position to realize equity; if faced with a large 3‑bet I’ll fold and preserve a large stack. This line maximizes long‑term EV against a wide defending BB.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit. Minni, in the Small Blind, has raised to 200, which is a min-raise (making it 100 more for me to call). While A3o is a relatively weak hand, I am getting 3:1 odds on a call and have position on Minni post-flop. Given the stack depths and the price, I will defend my blind and see a flop.

I raised wide from the button preflop and now hold 6♦5♠ on K♥7♣5♣ — I’ve flopped a pair of fives (bottom/mid pair) with position. Heads‑up the BB’s calling range is wide and many hands missed this K‑high, so a continuation bet will fold a lot of air and pick up value from worse pairs and some draws. The two clubs on board introduce some flush‑draw risk, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a large bet and get check‑raised off a marginal hand. I’ll make a half‑pot c‑bet (200 into 400) to maintain initiative, extract value from worse hands/draws, and keep the turn decisions manageable. Plan: if he folds I win the pot; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn (protect vs clubs/K/7 and fold to heavy aggression); if he check‑raises the flop large I’ll concede with this marginal holding.

On this King-high board, Minni's continuation bet of 200 into a 400 pot is a standard play after her pre-flop raise. I have Ace-high with no direct draw. While an Ace could potentially be the best hand if she is bluffing, there are many hands in her range that beat me (any King, any pair, or even some better Ace-high hands). Given that I have no equity improvement draws (like a flush or straight draw) and I'm facing a lead on a board that favors the pre-flop raiser's range, a fold is the most disciplined play to avoid losing more chips with a marginal hand.

I'm on the button with Kc9s — a playable, positionally strong hand. Pot is 150 and it would cost 100 to just limp; I prefer to steal and take the initiative. I will open-raise to 300 (≈3x BB). That sizing gives fold equity vs the blinds, keeps dominated hands and weaker broadways from seeing cheap flops, and leaves plenty of stack to play postflop if called. If I face a large 3-bet I will re-evaluate (likely fold to very large 3-bets out of position, defend or 4-bet selectively vs small-to-medium 3-bets depending on opponent), and if called I'll use position to c-bet favorable boards and control pot size on dangerous textures.

I am in the Small Blind with 9c6h. Minni, who has been quite active from the button, has raised to 300. 96 offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive button raiser and a potentially active big blind. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop, and 3-betting is far too loose with this holding. Folding is the most profitable long-term play here to preserve my stack.

I'm in the big blind with J♠7♦ facing a button raise from Minni to 300. Flash has folded, making this heads-up. J7o is a marginal hand with poor equity against Minni's aggressive raising range from the button. Minni has shown consistent aggression throughout the session and raises frequently from late position. 

Even though I have reasonable pot odds (450 to 200 call = 2.25:1), J7o plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. I have ~7275 chips, so I'm not in immediate danger, but wasting chips on marginal hands is detrimental to my long-term profitability.

This is a clear fold. I should preserve my stack and wait for better spots with stronger holdings.

I am on the button with A7s, which is a premium starting hand in a three-handed game. I have the opportunity to take control of the pot early. Given the current stack sizes and my position, a standard raise is the most effective play to either win the blinds immediately or build a pot with a strong range advantage if called.

I'm in the small blind with J4 suited (both diamonds), which has decent potential with a flush draw possibility. Flash raised to 300 from the button, which is a common aggressive position play with a wide range. The pot odds are favorable at 1.8:1 (450:250), and calling allows me to see a flop in position after the big blind acts. While J4s is not a premium hand, it has enough equity and playability to warrant a call here, especially since I can play it well post-flop. A fold gives up too easily, and a raise with J4s seems overextended given the hand strength and my desire to accumulate information on this opponent.

Facing a button open to 300 and the small blind call, this is a three‑way pot and I’m out of position. 5♣4♥ has very poor equity multiway versus typical BTN opening and SB calling ranges; calling 200 into a 700 pot requires ~22% equity to break even, and my realized equity here is likely well below that. Raising is pointless with this hand and would bloat the pot OOP. Preserve a deep stack for better spots — fold.

Checking is correct with J4d on a K-T-6 board. I have a flush draw (11 outs) but no made hand. Flash's button raise suggests a strong range, so checking keeps the pot controlled, allows me to see his flop aggression, and gets a free card if he also checks. If he bets, I can reassess based on pot odds and the bet size. This is a standard draw-based play where I'm not strong enough to bet/raise but have enough equity to continue with proper odds.

I am on the button with Ac7c on a board of KcTs6s. Pre-flop, I raised to 300 and Claude called from the small blind. The flop contains a King and a Ten, which interact well with a standard calling range from the small blind, but I hold the nut flush draw (clubs) and an overcard (Ace). Claude checked to me. While I missed the board in terms of made hands, I have significant equity and back-door potential. I will fire a continuation bet of about half the pot to maintain aggression and potentially take the pot down now or set up a barrel on later streets if I pick up more equity.

Calling with a flush draw. I have 4d-Jd on K-T-6 board, giving me 11 outs to a flush. With approximately 44% equity and needing only 25% pot odds (1050:350 = 3:1), this is a mathematically sound call. Two cards remain, and the flush draw has strong potential to improve on the turn or river. Flash's aggressive play from the button and flop bet is consistent with a wide range, making my draw attractive for continued play.

Checking the turn with a strong 11-out diamond flush draw. Flash's turn check indicates weakness or a medium holding, and I benefit from seeing the river for free with excellent equity on my draw. With only one card remaining, my flush draw has approximately 44% equity, making a free card extremely valuable. This is a standard draw-based play where checking gives me the best risk-reward ratio.

On the turn, the 8s completes some potential straight draws (97) and more importantly, brings a third spade to the board. I have Ac7c, so I do not have a spade in my hand. While I still have an overcard and a backdoor club draw (though less relevant now), the board has become significantly wetter and more dangerous. Claude checked to me again. If I bet here, I am likely only getting called by hands that beat me (Kings, Tens, or flushes) and I risk being check-raised. Checking back allows me to see a free river card and potentially realize my equity if an Ace or a non-spade club comes, or simply get to a cheap showdown if the river is safe. Given the board texture, checking is the most prudent play.

Checking the river with pair of fours and jack kicker on a Kc-Ts-6s-8s-4s board. This is a weak holding that deserves showdown without further investment. Flash's turn check suggested weakness or a medium hand, so checking keeps the pot small and avoids bloating it with a marginal holding. Betting would likely either induce a fold from worse hands (unprofitable) or get called/raised by better hands (costly). Checking allows me to reach showdown economically and either win a small pot or minimize losses on a weak hand.

The river has brought a fourth spade to the board (Ts 6s 8s 4s). I hold Ac 7c, meaning I have no spade and only Ace-high. My opponent, Claude, has checked the river after calling my flop continuation bet and checking the turn. While a bet might occasionally fold out a weak King or a Ten without a spade, it's very risky because any single spade in Claude's hand makes a flush. Additionally, the turn check-check makes my range look less like I'm holding a high spade myself. In a three-handed game, there is a small chance my Ace-high is good if Claude was on a different draw that missed, but more importantly, a check-back allows me to reach showdown for free without risking a check-raise or a call from a better hand. Checking is the most prudent play here.

T7o is a marginal hand even on the button. While position is valuable, playing weak hands out of position post-flop is a mistake. With only two opponents (the blinds), I need a stronger hand to justify involvement. The cost to call is 100 into a 150 pot, but I'll be out of position against both Minni (SB) and Flash (BB) on subsequent streets, which negates much of my button positional advantage. This hand doesn't justify the call—if I were going to play it, I'd need to raise to take down the blinds preflop, but T7o isn't strong enough for that either. Folding preserves my stack for better spots where I have clearer value.

Heads-up from the small blind with 6♦7♦ and deep stacks (~200BB). Suited connectors have strong postflop playability and in heads-up I want to be proactive while keeping pots manageable. A min-raise to 200 (total) achieves three things: isolates the big blind, picks up fold equity, and lets me play a playable hand in position on later streets. Calling to 100 is a passive but acceptable line; folding is too tight. I'll raise to 200 and re-evaluate if Flash 3-bets large (likely folding to a polarized 3-bet) or proceed postflop if he just calls.

I am in the Big Blind with 5h 2d, a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind, meaning it only costs me 100 more to call to see a flop in a pot that would be 400. However, 52o is nearly unplayable and has very poor realization of equity. Minni has been active and aggressive from the blinds throughout the session. Given my position and the weakness of my hand, there is no reason to defend here and risk being outplayed post-flop. I will fold.

3♦7♦ is a very weak hand, but it’s suited and I’m on the button facing only the SB and BB. Calling 100 is a cheap, low-variance way to capitalize on position and see a flop — I can fold to heavy aggression if I miss. Raising to try to steal is possible, but both Flash (SB) and Claude (BB) defend and apply aggression often; I don’t want to get committed with this marginal holding. Plan: call to see the flop, look to continue only with a diamond/paired 7/3 or strong backdoor equity; otherwise fold to significant pressure.

I am in the Small Blind with 98o. Minni limped on the Button for 100, which is unusual as she often raises. This suggests a wide range, potentially containing some weak hands she wants to see a flop with cheaply, or a trap. However, 98o has reasonable playability in a multi-way pot or heads-up if Claude checks behind. Calling 50 more into a pot of 250 offers 5:1 odds, which is too good to pass up given the potential to hit a straight or two pair. I will complete the blind.

With 2h9o (one of the weakest starting hands), I'm already committed for the big blind at 100. Both opponents merely called, suggesting moderate holdings. Raising would be reckless with this hand. Folding wastes my blind. Checking allows me to see the flop cost-free and make an informed decision based on how the board develops. This is the standard correct play in this spot.

I have hit top pair (Nines) with an 8 kicker on a 9c-4h-5c board. While 8 is not the strongest kicker, this is a limped pot, meaning my opponents' ranges are likely wide and capped. Claude (BB) and Minni (BTN) could have some small pairs, straight draws (like 67 or 32), or flush draws. Leading out with a bet here serves two purposes: extracting value from draws and middle pairs, and protecting my hand against overcards or completing draws on the turn. A bet of around 2/3 pot is standard to keep the pot manageable while charging draws.

I have 9-2 offsuit, which gives me a pair of nines with a terrible deuce kicker on a 9c-4h-5c board. While I have a pair, it's one of the weakest possible holdings - the 2 kicker is essentially worthless. Flash is raising aggressively to 200 on the flop, signaling strength or a strong draw. Minni still has to act behind me, and with only 3 players, there's significant room for additional pressure. I'm likely behind Flash's range (which likely includes overpairs, strong draws, or two pair), out of position, and facing a pot odds situation (200 to call a 500 pot) where I'd need roughly 28% equity. My hand probably has around 20-25% equity at best. Additionally, with 7925 chips, I can afford to wait for better opportunities. Folding the weak pair to aggressive action is the mathematically sound play here.

Fold. I have 3♦7♦ on 9c–4h–5c: no pair, no diamond, only a gutshot to a 6 (4 outs). Pot is 500 and the bet is 200, so I’m getting ~200/(500+200) ≈ 28.6% pot odds. My chance to hit the straight by the river is only ≈16.5% (≈8.5% to hit on the turn), well below the break-even equity. Raising would be a pure bluff with very little fold or redraw equity and calling is -EV. Flash has been aggressive and this bet likely contains value hands; I’ll preserve my stack and fold to pressure.

I am on the button with a pocket pair of fives (5c 5h). This is a solid hand to play in a three-handed game. I have 11,950 chips, which is around 120 big blinds. I'll open with a standard raise to 300 to take control of the pot and see how the blinds react. Pocket pairs have good value, especially if I can hit a set on the flop.

I'm holding AK (Ace-King), one of the premium hands in poker. Flash raised to 300 from the button. With AK in the small blind, I should be aggressive to:

1. Build the pot with a premium hand
2. Apply pressure on both opponents
3. Potentially isolate the pot or define hand strength

A raise to 850 (roughly 2.8x Flash's raise) is appropriate here. This is strong, aggressive poker while maintaining reasonable stack management. AK is best played by building the pot and forcing decisions, especially in a heads-up or 3-way pot where I have initiative. My stack of 7,875 can support this commitment without overcommitting too early.

I’m in the big blind with Qc2h — a weak, offsuit hand with a bad kicker. Flash opened to 300 and Claude 3-bet to 850, putting 1250 in the pot and 750 to call. Calling requires ~37.5% equity (750/(1250+750)) which Q2o does not have against the combined BTN+SB ranges, and a 4-bet with this holding is unnecessary risk. With deep stacks and many hands left, preserve chips and fold to the 3-bet.

I opened to 300 with 5c 5h from the button, and Claude responded with a 3bet to 850 from the small blind. Claude has a relatively shorter stack compared to the table (7075 chips). While pocket fives are a decent hand, they are difficult to play post-flop against a 3bet range, as I will likely see overcards on most flops. Calling 550 more to win a total pot of 1250 gives me decent odds, but being out of position against a 3bet is tricky. However, in a 3-handed game, ranges are wider. Given my stack size and the position, I could call to set-mine, but Claude's 3bet suggests a strong range. Fold is the safest disciplined play here to preserve chips and wait for a better spot, though calling is borderline acceptable for set value.

Raising with pocket kings (KK) from the button is a standard play. This is one of the strongest starting hands in poker, and position is excellent. I'm raising to 400 total chips (a 300 chip raise from the current 100 to call), which is a strong but not overly aggressive sizing that allows my opponents to call profitably with a wider range. This accomplishes several goals: (1) builds the pot immediately with a premium hand, (2) narrows the field by applying pressure, (3) gains information about opponent holdings, (4) generates value from weaker hands that might call. Given my stack size of 8,325 chips and the pot of 150, this raise is appropriately sized to control the hand while keeping opponents interested.

I have A♦3♠ in the small blind facing a button open to 400. With only three players, A3o is worth defending — it has an ace blocker and reasonable playability — but it’s a weak candidate for a bluff 3‑bet. Calling keeps the pot under control, takes a flop cheaply (350 more into a 550 pot, ~39% break‑even) and lets me realize equity heads‑up or against a passive BB. A 3‑bet to 700 commits too much with a poor kicker and will often run into stronger Aces or a 4‑bet from Flash; folding is too nitty in this spot. Plan: flat‑call 400 total, fold to a big 3‑bet, and on the flop proceed aggressively with a top pair or strong draw, pot‑control with air.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Queen offsuit (Ah Qs). Claude has raised to 400 from the Button, and Minni has called from the Small Blind. AQ is a premium hand, especially in a three-handed dynamic. Given the action, I have a strong enough hand to 3-bet for value and to take control of the pot. Raising will thin the field or force the opponents to pay to see a flop. Claude's range as a button raiser is wide, and Minni's call often signifies a medium-strength hand. I will raise to 1300 to build the pot and exert pressure.

3-betting with KK to 3100 total chips (2700 more). Pocket kings is one of the strongest starting hands and should not just call a 3-bet. This raise applies pressure on Flash's 3-bet while Minni is still in the hand. The sizing is strong but not reckless given my 7925 chip stack - I'll have 5125 left after committing 2800 more (900 to call + 1800 to raise). This accomplishes several goals: (1) builds the pot while I have a premium hand, (2) forces Flash to make a decision with real money on the line, (3) potentially isolates one opponent, (4) maintains positional advantage going into flop. Even if Flash has AA, I still have reasonable equity; against most other hands I'm ahead or flipping.

I called the button open intending to fold to a big 3‑bet. That plan is validated here: Flash 3‑bets to 1300 and Claude 4‑bets to 3100. I’m facing a call of 2700 into a 4800 pot (~36% break‑even) and I’d be playing multiway against very strong ranges. A♦3♠ is frequently dominated by the kinds of hands that 4‑bet in this spot (QQ+/AK and value‑heavy lines) and it doesn’t have the equity or blocker value to justify calling or squeezing here. Raising to 4900 would either isolate me against stronger hands or get called by the 3‑bettor, so it’s a negative EV line. Fold and preserve a deep stack for better, cleaner spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Queen offsuit (Ah Qs). I initially 3-bet to 1300, but Claude has now 4-bet to 3100. Claude's stack is quite short relative to the pot, with only about 2125 remaining behind if I call or raise. A 4-bet from the button after my 3-bet from the BB is a very strong signal, typically indicating a range of JJ+, AK. However, in a 3-handed game, ranges are wider. Given the pot odds (calling 1800 to win a total pot that will be 6600) and the fact that Claude is almost committed, I have to decide between calling to see a flop or just moving all-in. Since he only has about 2k left, if I call, we are effectively playing for stacks on almost any flop. However, my AQo is likely behind a standard 4-betting range. Looking at Claude's stack size (5225 total), my 1300 was a significant portion, and his 3100 is even more so. I am getting roughly 2.6:1 on a call. However, a 4-bet/5-bet dynamic here usually suggests I am flipping at best or dominated. Given the aggression shown by Claude (who has been active), I will fold this time to preserve my stack, as AQo performs poorly against a range that is willing to put in 60% of their stack pre-flop.

7♣3♥ is essentially junk — extremely low equity and very poor playability. Although I’m on the button in a 3‑handed pot, I’m facing two active blinds (Flash and Claude) who defend and raise frequently, so my fold equity on a min‑raise will be low and I’ll often face calls or 3‑bets. Calling 100 into a 150 pot requires ~40% equity to break even (100/(150+100)), which 7‑3o doesn’t have versus two ranges. With deep stacks this becomes a marginal, difficult spot postflop, so the best long‑term play is to preserve chips and fold preflop.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit (As 3c). The Button (Minni) has folded, leaving only me and Claude in the Big Blind. Ace-high is a strong starting hand in a heads-up situation. I should either complete the small blind or raise to take control of the pot. Raising from the small blind against a competent big blind is standard to prevent giving them a free flop and to capitalize on the equity of an Ace. I'll raise to 3x the big blind (300 total) to apply pressure.

3♠ 5♥ is a marginal hand that cannot profitably defend against Flash's raise from the small blind. I'm out of position and lack the hand equity needed to justify calling. With effective stacks around 100BB, preserving chips for stronger holdings is optimal. Clear fold.

I am on the button with 5-3 suited, a speculative drawing hand. In a three-handed game, this hand can be played for its potential, but since the blinds have not yet acted and I would prefer to take the pot down early or see a cheap flop, a standard raise is typical. However, given the table dynamics and the need for efficiency, I will fold this weak connector to wait for a better spot, as 5-3 suited performs poorly against most calling or raising ranges from the blinds.

In a heads-up situation against Minni (Big Blind), I'm in the small blind with K-5 offsuit. Flash has already folded, leaving just two players. I need to call 50 chips to win a pot of 150, giving me 3:1 pot odds. While K-5 is not a premium hand, in heads-up play I can profitably call with a much wider range than in full games. The King provides high-card value and potential to make top pair. Given the favorable pot odds and positional advantage post-flop as the small blind (button in heads-up), calling is the correct play. This allows me to realize my hand's potential in a position where I'll act last post-flop.

Check. K6o is a marginal hand in the big blind against a single limper (Claude). There’s no cost to see the flop so I’ll keep the pot small and realize equity rather than inflate the pot with a weak kicker. Claude has shown a lot of passive limping/calling in prior hands, so a tiny isolation raise isn’t necessary — checking lets me see a flop and then fold to heavy action if I miss, call small C-bets with appropriate draws/pairs, or take a free card when checked to. For now, check and re-evaluate on the flop.

I hold K♥5♥ on a 5♠K♣A♥ flop, giving me two pair (Kings and Fives) plus a 10-out flush draw. This is a premium flop for my hand. Since Minni checked from the big blind, she's showing weakness. I'm in position and should bet for value to extract chips from her range while also protecting my hand against overcards on the turn. A 150-chip bet (roughly 75% of pot) is appropriate sizing for a heads-up situation with a strong made hand and excellent draw equity. This bet builds the pot while maintaining good fold equity if Minni's check indicates she has weak holdings.

Call 150. I have top pair (K) but a very weak 6 kicker and there's an Ace on the board, so I don't want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Claude has been a passive limper/caller and his flop bet is small, so the price to see the turn is cheap (≈30% pot odds). Calling keeps worse kings and middle pairs in to extract value later, controls the pot size, and lets me re-evaluate on the turn — folding to heavy aggression if he shows clear strength or continuing if the turn is neutral or improves me.

I have a premium hand: two pair (Kings and Fives) with a 9-out flush draw on the turn. The 9♥ improved both my made hand strength and my drawing potential significantly. Minni called my flop bet, indicating a decent holding, but my two pair is likely ahead of her range. I should continue extracting value by betting the turn. A 350-chip bet into the 500 pot (70% bet) is appropriate sizing for a heads-up situation—it builds the pot while I'm ahead and denies Minni favorable pot odds if she's on a draw. This is strong value-betting with excellent made hand equity and a massive draw to fall back on.

I have top pair (K) with a very weak 6 kicker on K♣ A♥ 5♠ 9♥. Claude limp/called preflop and has shown a small flop bet (150) followed by a larger turn bet (350). Folding would concede too much — I still beat a lot of his limp-heavy range (weaker Kx, mid/small pairs, some bluffs) and the price is reasonable. Call math: the pot is 850 and I must call 350 → I need ~29% equity to justify a call. Against a mixed limp/lead range my K6 has roughly that equity (we pick up outs to trips and can still get value from worse Ks/pairs), so calling to see the river is the +EV play. Raising here is unattractive — my kicker is weak, I have limited fold equity versus a passive limper, and a raise will often commit chips when I'm dominated by Ax or AK. Plan: call the turn, then re-evaluate on the river — fold to large river aggression on non-improving cards, call or raise for value if I improve to trips (or if opponent checks and a safe card falls).

River value bet with two pair (K♥5♥). The board ran out 5♠K♣A♥9♥J♠. My two pair is a strong made hand that should be ahead of most of Minni's calling range. She's shown consistent strength through flop and turn, but likely holds hands like A-x, K-x with weaker kickers, or newly made pairs. A 600-chip bet (50% of pot) is appropriate sizing to extract value without overcommitting. This bet gets called by hands I beat while building the pot with a premium holding.

Call 600. Pot is 1800 and calling 600 requires ~25% equity (600/2400), which is a reasonable hurdle for my hand. I have top pair (K) with a terrible 6-kicker, so I must be cautious, but Claude limped preflop and has run a small→medium value-betting line (150 on the flop, 350 on the turn, 600 on the river). That line contains a mix of Ax, thinner Kx, small pairs and occasional bluffs — there are enough worse kings and non-A hands in his range that my hand has the pot-odds to continue. Raising is unattractive: I have minimal fold equity and would often get called by better Kx or Aces, committing chips with a dominated kicker. Folding concedes too much; I’ll call and see showdown.

7-3 (seven-three) is the worst possible starting hand in Texas Hold'em. While I'm on the button with position, this is not sufficient compensation for the fundamental weakness of this hand. 7-3 has no value - no pairs, not connected, not suited. Even at a cheap price of 100 to call, I should not build the pot with such a weak holding. The small blind and big blind are still in the pot, and there's no reason to invest chips here when I can wait for better opportunities. Folding weak hands from the button is profitable in the long run.

I’m in the small blind with A♣J♣ — a strong, playable hand but out of position. With only the big blind to act and deep stacks, I want to apply pressure, get value from worse A-x and broadway hands, and price out speculative holdings. A raise to 300 total (3x the BB) is a standard sizing: larger than the min-raise to preserve fold equity but not so large that I commit too much OOP. If Flash calls I’ll play postflop for value and fold equity; if he 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and his tendencies (often folding to extreme 3-bets, calling or 4-betting light vs smaller sizing).

I am in the Big Blind with 4d 5h. Minni, who is in the Small Blind, has raised to 300. This is a 3x open from the SB. While 45o has some connectivity, it is one of the weakest hands in poker and does not play well out of position against a range that likely includes many superior hands. Given the current stack sizes and the fact that I'm already in a relatively neutral chip position for the session, there is no need to defend this hand and put myself in a difficult post-flop spot. Folding is the standard and most profitable long-term play here.

Button with K♢8♠ in a three-handed pot. I have deep stacks (~18.5k) and positional advantage over both blinds, so opening a fairly wide range to steal is correct. K8o is marginal but a decent blocker to strong K/A combos and plays well in position postflop. Min-raising to 200 is too small and invites more collisions; sizing to ~3x the BB (300) gives good fold equity and reduces 3-bet frequency. Plan: raise to 300 to take the initiative; fold to a large 3-bet, or call a small one and play position postflop with c-bet/floating lines as appropriate. This balances aggression and risk and maximizes EV over the session.

I am holding 8c 2h in the small blind. Minni raised to 300 from the button. This hand is extremely weak and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position. Given the stack sizes and the strength of my hand, there is no reason to call or bluff here.

Folding Q-2o from the big blind against Minni's button raise. This is one of the weakest possible hands in poker and doesn't warrant playing even with position post-flop. The hand quality is too poor relative to the investment required. Maintaining disciplined hand selection with 11k stack available for better spots.

I am on the button with Ace-Four offsuit. This is a standard opening hand in a three-handed game. My stack is healthy at over 100 big blinds. I will raise to 3x the big blind to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the hand.

Folding K5o from the small blind. While the pot odds are reasonable at 1.8:1, I'm out of position against an aggressive button raiser with a weak hand. K5o plays poorly post-flop: if I hit a king, I'm vulnerable to a better kicker; if I miss, I'm in a weak spot. Discipline to fold marginal hands and wait for premium situations is key to long-term success.

5♠4♣ is a very weak, offsuit, low connector with poor playability out of position. Flash opened to 300 from the button; I’m OOP in the big blind and would need to call 200 more (total 300 committed) to see a flop. That price requires ~30% equity (200 to win the current 450), but against a button opening range I’m often dominated by A‑x, K‑x and pocket pairs and won’t have that equity frequently. Postflop I’ll be out of position which makes extracting value or realizing equity difficult, and raising to 500 as a bluff is an unnecessarily large and risky move with such a poor hand. With a deep stack and many better spots ahead, the +EV play here is to fold and conserve chips.

5-7 offsuit is a marginal/weak hand that doesn't justify playing even from the button. Despite positional advantage, the hand lacks sufficient equity against aggressive opponents. With deep stacks, hand quality is critical. Folding preserves chips for premium situations.

Heads-up vs Flash in the BB. I hold J♠4♦ — a weak, marginal hand but perfectly playable in heads-up because the cost to see a flop is tiny (50 more to make my total 100). As SB I act first preflop but will have positional advantage after the flop, so I want to see a cheap flop rather than inflate the pot with a marginal holding. A min-raise to 200 bloats the pot and invites 3-bets from an aggressive opponent; I don't want to play a bloated pot out of position postflop with J4o. Plan: call to see the flop; fold to a large 3-bet, and postflop use position to c-bet when I hit or apply pressure on very dry boards, otherwise check/control the pot.

I am in the Big Blind with K2o. Minni, in the Small Blind, has completed the blind (limped). In a 3-handed game, K2o has reasonable equity against a completion range, and since there is no raise to call, I can simply check and see a flop for free. Raising with K2o here would be a bluff, and since Minni is a frequent raiser, a limp might occasionally indicate a trap or a hand they want to play cheaply. I will take the free flop.

Heads-up OOP with J♠4♦ on a very dry Q♥3♥2♠ flop. I have no pair and little showdown value, but this board folds out a lot of the BB’s checking range (weak overcards, small pairs) and gives me decent bluff equity. A small-to-medium lead (≈½ pot) extracts folds while keeping the pot controllable if called. Plan: bet 100 to apply pressure; fold to a large raise (I have almost no equity) and, if called, re-evaluate on the turn and only continue barreling on safe cards or when I pick up equity.

I am in a heads-up blind-vs-blind situation in a limped pot. I have bottom pair (Deuces) with a King kicker on a Qh-3h-2s board. Minni has bet 100 into a 200 pot. In a limped pot, my bottom pair holds significant relative strength, and I am getting 3:1 odds to see a turn. While I am behind a Queen or a Three, I am ahead of many of Minni's potential bluffs, heart draws, and gutshot draws (like 4-5 or A-4). Calling is the most appropriate action to maintain the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn.

I c-bet the dry Q♥3♥2♠ flop for 100 and was called. The turn 9♦ gives me no pair or improved draw with J♦4♠ — still very little equity or showdown value. I have position, so I should control the pot rather than barrel a thin bluff that will be called by much of Flash’s continuing range (Qx, small pairs, 9x, heart draws). Checking keeps the pot manageable, lets me see what he does, and preserves the ability to bluff the river if he shows weakness. If he leads out with a sizable bet I’ll fold; I may call a very small probe only if the price is attractive to try and realize my (very limited) equity.

On the turn, the Nine of Diamonds is a fairly blank card that doesn't significantly change the board texture. I have bottom pair (Deuces) and a King kicker in a limped pot. Minni, who bet the flop and was called, has now checked the turn. This check often indicates a lack of a strong Queen or an attempt to pot-control with a marginal hand/draw. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, realize my equity, and keep the pot small with a hand that has some showdown value but cannot withstand much more pressure. I will check behind to see a cheap river.

I have J♠4♦ on Qh-3h-2s-9d-Qs — essentially air (jack-high) on a paired-queen river. Flash called my flop c-bet and then checked the turn, so his continuing range contains a lot of Qx/9x/small pairs and some missed heart draws. Most of that range either already beats me or will call a reasonable river bluff. To bluff profitably I need sizable fold equity; a half-pot or larger bet requires him to fold a large % of his range (≥~33%), and while a very tiny probe (≈100) lowers the threshold, Flash’s line (call flop, check turn) suggests he’s not a complete laydown — he calls small bets often. With almost no showdown value and limited, uncertain fold equity, the lowest-variance, highest-EV line is to check and concede unless he throws out a very small probe that’s cheap enough to call. I will check and fold to any meaningful river bet.

The river brings a second Queen, pairing the board. I have two pair, Queens and Deuces with a King kicker. Minni's line (limp pre-flop, small bet on flop, check turn, check river) suggests a weak hand or a missed draw. While I have showdown value, there isn't much value in betting; a better hand like a 9 is unlikely to fold, and a worse hand is unlikely to call. Checking behind allows me to realize my equity and win the pot if my deuces are good against her missed draws or low pairs/Ace-high.

I’m on the button with Kc2d — a weak, often-dominated hand but valuable because I have position and can attempt a steal. Pot is 150, to call 100. Folding is overly tight; calling is passive and concedes initiative to the blinds. Standard exploitative open on the button is 3xBB to generate fold equity and isolate one opponent. Flash (SB) can be aggressive but often folds to pressure; Claude (BB) defends but rarely 3-bets huge. I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB) to steal or play postflop in position. If I face a large 3-bet (e.g. ~900+), K2o is usually folding territory — I’ll fold to that. If called, I’ll proceed postflop looking to c-bet on favorable textures or check and realize equity on dry boards.

I am holding 5c 2c in the Small Blind. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. While the hand is suited, it is extremely low and plays poorly out of position. Facing a 3x raise, defending with such a weak hand is likely to lead to negative expected value over the long run, especially with Claude still to act in the Big Blind. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Folding Ad3s out of position against Minni's button raise. The hand is too weak to defend the big blind effectively, especially out of position post-flop. Even with reasonable pot odds, the hand's poor equity and vulnerability to being outkicked make folding the most prudent play. Better hands and situations will develop in the remaining 96 hands.

I am on the button with King-Five offsuit. In a three-handed game, this is a standard hand to open for a steal attempt. I have successfully stolen the blinds with similar hands (like K5 in Hand 22) earlier in the session. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the blinds. If I face significant resistance from Minni or Claude, I can fold without much loss, but K-high holds reasonable equity if I am called.

QK is a premium hand that should be played aggressively from any position. In the small blind facing Flash's button raise, I should raise to take control with my strong hand. A raise to 800 (about 2.6x Flash's 300 bet) builds the pot, applies pressure to both opponents, and allows me to play positionally on later streets. This sizing is aggressive but not over-committed given my 10,825 stack.

Fold. I’m in the big blind with Qh6d facing Claude’s reraise to 800 after Flash opened to 300 (pot 1,200; I must call 700). Calling requires ~37% equity (700 into a 1,900 pot) and I’ll be out of position heads-up or multiway postflop against a 3‑bet range that largely dominates Q6o. A squeeze/raise to 1,300 as a bluff is unattractive with deep stacks and poor blockers, and a call leads to difficult postflop decisions with low realized equity. No strong reads suggest an ultra‑light 3‑bet, so folding and preserving my stack is the +EV play here.

I opened from the button with K5o, which is a marginal hand primarily used for steal attempts. Claude has 3-bet me from the small blind. K5o is significantly behind Claude's likely 3-betting range (typically consisting of big pairs and strong broadway cards). Continuing in a 3-bet pot with a hand that is easily dominated and lacks post-flop playability is a losing strategy. I will fold and preserve my stack.

K3o is a marginal hand with poor equity. While I'm on the button with good position, this is too weak to call 100 chips against two opponents. The 1.5:1 pot odds don't justify playing such a weak hand post-flop when out of position for subsequent streets. I've been maintaining tight hand selection throughout the session, and K3o doesn't meet the threshold for a profitable play here.

I’m in the small blind with Q♥T♥, pot 150 and only the BB left to act. Heads-up this is a strong, playable hand with position postflop (button acts last heads-up), and with deep stacks I want initiative and fold equity. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the BB) to isolate the BB, force folds from weaker hands, and give myself easier postflop play in position. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold QTo vs a large 3-bet); if he calls I’ll use position to play for value or fold equity on favorable flops.

I am in the Big Blind holding 7-4 offsuit. Minni raised to 300 from the Small Blind after Claude folded. While I would have position on Minni post-flop in this head-up scenario, 7-4 offsuit is an extremely weak hand that does not realize its equity well. Even with the 2:1 pot odds offered, I prefer to fold and wait for a more profitable situation rather than defending with the bottom of my range.

On the button with K♦Q♠ (KQo) and two blinds left, this is a clear open-raise spot. Stacks are deep (~185bb) and I have position on both opponents, so I want to seize the initiative, get fold equity, and avoid giving them a cheap flop to see. A standard 3xBB open to 300 is size-consistent with the table and balances building the pot with a strong but non-premium hand. Plan: open to 300; if I face a small/medium 3-bet I can continue (call or 4-bet light depending on sizing and opponent), while a large polarized 3-bet will be treated more cautiously. Raising to 300 maximizes value and leverage of position while keeping options open postflop.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace of Spades and Four of Diamonds. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. While Ace-high is often the best hand, Ace-Four offsuit is a relatively weak hand to play out of position against a button raise, especially with a deep stack. However, in a three-handed game, folding an Ace to a single raise might be too tight. Looking at previous hands, Minni is quite active from the button. That said, calling puts me in a difficult spot post-flop out of position, and raising (3-betting) with A4o as a bluff is risky given Minni's tendency to call. Given the current stack sizes and the need to preserve my stack for better spots over the 150-hand match, I will fold and wait for a stronger hand or a better position.

I'm holding 3-2 offsuit in the big blind, which is the absolute worst hand in poker. Minni raised to 300 from the button, and Flash folded. 

While I'm getting reasonable pot odds (450:200), the problem is that my hand has almost no equity against Minni's button-raising range. Even in the most favorable scenarios, 3-2o is winning at best ~20% of the time against a typical raising range.

Based on Minni's play throughout the session, she's showing a reasonably aggressive but not extremely loose raising pattern. Calling 200 more with effectively the worst hand in poker is a losing proposition long-term. The pot odds don't compensate for how weak this hand is.

Folding is the mathematically correct play. Losing the 100 BB now is better than losing more chips with a dominated hand.

I am on the button with Ace-Ten suited (AhTh), which is a very strong starting hand in a three-handed game. My stack is healthy at over 100 big blinds. I will raise to take control of the pot and potentially take it down pre-flop, or build a pot with a premium hand. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here.

Folding K5o from the small blind. This hand is too weak and out of position. While I have decent pot odds (1.8:1), my hand is dominated by much of Flash's button raising range. With a healthy 111+ BB stack, I should be selective and fold marginal hands that will put me in difficult post-flop spots. King-Five offsuit is not strong enough to play against an aggressive button raiser when I lack positional advantage.

Flash (button) opened to 300. Pot is 450 and I need 200 to call (total commitment 300), which requires ~30.8% equity to break even. T4o is a marginal hand but has reasonable equity versus a wide button opening range and with deep stacks I can realize equity postflop. A light 3-bet to 500 is possible, but out of position with a weak hand I don’t want to bloat the pot or face difficult decisions; I prefer to defend and see a flop. Plan: call, play cautiously OOP — fold to large C-bets when I miss, continue with any tens, pairs, or strong draws, and extract value when I improve.

Check. I completely missed this K♠-9♥-5♠ flop with T♠4♥ — no pair, weak kicker and only a runner‑runner/spade possibility (very low equity). Out of position vs a preflop raiser, my priority is pot control and folding to normal continuation bets. I’ll check and give up to a standard c‑bet; only consider calling a very tiny probe or continuing if I pick up real equity on the turn. If Flash checks back I can take a free turn card and reassess.

I raised pre-flop with Ace-Ten of hearts and was called by Minni in the big blind. The flop is 9h Ks 5s. I have one overcard (Ace), a backdoor nut flush draw (with the 9h on board and AhTh in hand), and a backdoor straight draw. Minni has checked. This is a good board to continuation bet on as the King is often in my range as the pre-flop raiser, and I have significant equity with my backdoor draws and overcard. A bet of about half the pot should be enough to take it down now or set up for future streets.

Pot is 1,000 and Flash fires 350; calling 350 to win 1,350 requires ~25.9% equity. With T♠4♥ on K♠-9♥-5♠ I have no pair, weak kicker and only marginal backdoor/paired outs: 3 tens + 3 fours → ~24.1% to pair by river, plus a small runner-runner spade flush chance (~4%). Much of that raw equity is ineffective versus a button raiser who often holds Kx, pairs or strong overcards, and OOP I’ll face difficult decisions on turn/river. A bluff-raise here is high variance and unlikely to fold enough of his range. Preserve stack and fold to a standard c-bet.

Raising to 350 from the Button with A3. This is a strong position play that applies pressure to both blinds. The Ace is a strong card, position is optimal, and a moderate raise is sized appropriately relative to our stack and the pot. This can win the pot immediately or allow us to play in position post-flop if called.

Claude opened to 350 from the button. I have 8♦2♥ — the worst offsuit combination. Calling 300 more into a 500 pot gives me poor pot odds (300 into 800 → ~37.5% break-even) and I’d be out of position against both opponents post-flop with almost no equity vs a BTN opening range. A min 3-bet bluff to 600 is technically an option but extremely thin and easily exploited by the button or the big blind; there’s no reason to invest chips with such a dominated hand. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I have King-Queen of diamonds, a premium suited connector, in the Big Blind. Claude raised from the Button, which typically represents a wide range of hands. Rather than just calling and playing out of position, I will 3-bet to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop. A raise to 1100 is a standard sizing against a 3.5x open in this position.

Flash's 3-bet to 1,100 from the big blind indicates a strong hand range - likely premium pairs (AA, KK, QQ, JJ) or strong aces (AK, AQ). While I'm on the button with good position and have 3:1 pot odds to call, A3o is too weak to continue in a 3-bet pot against an opponent showing this level of strength. My hand has poor equity against Flash's likely range. Although A3 is playable in many spots, this situation calls for discipline - I should preserve my chips for better opportunities where I have stronger holdings or superior position advantages. Folding is the mathematically sound play despite the reasonable pot odds, as the hand's weakness makes the call unprofitable against this opponent's likely holdings.

I’m on the button with 9♦4♠ — a very weak, poorly-connected offsuit hand with almost no postflop playability or blocker value. The pot is 150 and it costs 100 to call, which means I’d need ~40% equity to justify a call (100/(150+100)=40%) — far more than 9-4o has multiway against the blinds. I could attempt a small open to 200–300 as a steal, but vs two competent deep-stacked opponents that would be a thin, easily punished bluff and invite 3-bets. No compelling reason to invest chips here; preserve my deep stack for better spots and fold.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit. Since the button folded, it's a Blind vs Blind situation. Ace-high hands are generally strong in this configuration. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and take control of the pot. Claude has shown some aggression with 3-bets lately, but I must maintain a solid opening range from the SB.

J6o (Jack-Six offsuit) is a weak starting hand that doesn't warrant playing out of position against Flash's raise. While I'm getting 2:1 pot odds to call 200 more, the hand quality is simply insufficient. Flash is being aggressive from the small blind with a likely wide range, but even in heads-up play, J6o plays poorly postflop out of position. With a healthy stack of 10,675 and 90 hands remaining, I should preserve chips and wait for stronger holdings. This is a standard fold from the big blind against a raise.

I am on the button with J4o. In a 3-handed game, this hand is too weak to open raise from the button, and definitely not worth calling or raising if someone else had acted. Since the action is on me and the pot is small, folding is the standard and most disciplined play with this hand to avoid post-flop complications with a weak holding.

With KJ of clubs in the small blind heads-up against Minni's big blind, this is a premium starting hand that justifies aggression. I'm raising to 250 total (adding 200 to my initial 50) to build a pot, apply pressure, and take control of the action with a hand that has strong equity. The raise is sized to encourage Minni to make a decision while establishing that I have a solid hand range.

Facing a 250 open from SB with the button folded, I’m in the BB with 8h3s — a clear trash hand. To call I must put in 150 into a 350 pot (the pot would be ~500 after a call), so I’d need ≈30% equity to break even. 8‑3 offsuit has far less equity vs a typical SB raising range and plays terribly out of position postflop. 3‑betting here as a bluff (min raise to 400 is the smallest legal reraise) is also a poor look — I have no meaningful blockers or fold equity, and risking chips with such a weak holding is unnecessary given my healthy stack. Preserve chips and fold.

I'm on the button with AK (Ah-Kc), one of the premium starting hands in poker. This is an ideal position to play aggressively. The current situation: small blind is Minni (18,175 chips), big blind is Flash (10,900 chips), and I need to put in 100 more to call.

AK should be played aggressively pre-flop to build the pot and define hand ranges. With position advantage and a premium holding, a standard 3x raise to 300 total is the correct play. This gives me good odds for value, builds the pot for when I hit the flop, and positions me well given my information advantage as the button.

I've been playing tight overall (Hand #46, #47, #49 show good fold discipline when appropriate but aggression with premium hands), so a raise here is consistent with my game plan and exploits my positional advantage.

I’m in the small blind with Q♠4♦ facing a button open to 300 (I must call 250). Calling 250 into a 450 pot requires ~36% equity to break even. Q4o is poorly positioned, often dominated by a button raising range, and has weak postflop playability out of position. A 3‑bet bluff is possible but Q4o is too weak to do so profitably here and would commit chips with little fold equity against reasonable opponents. With deep stacks there’s no compelling implied-odds reason to gamble from the SB with this hand — fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d Th. Claude raised from the Button to 300. With 100 already in the pot, I need to call 200 more to see a flop. The pot odds are 2.25:1, which is quite favorable. 8d Th is a reasonable hand to defend with in this spot, as it has decent connectivity and equity against a wide button opening range. I will call to see a flop.

On the flop of 9h 6s 3s, I have 10h 8d. I have one overcard to the board (10) and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7 to make a straight). Claude opened from the button and is likely to continuation bet a wide range of hands. However, it is my turn to act first. In this spot, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most balanced play. I will check and see how Claude reacts to this board texture.

I'm on the button with AK (Ah-Kc), one of the strongest starting hands, though I've completely missed this 9h-6s-3s flop. Flash just checked from the big blind, indicating weakness. This is a textbook spot for a continuation bet. With position and Flash's passive action, I should leverage my positional advantage to take down the pot. The board is relatively dry with no draws, and my aggression pre-flop makes a flop bet credible. If Flash has a weak unpaired hand (which his check suggests), this likely wins immediately. If he has a small pair or weak holding, he'll likely fold. Even if called, I have 6 clean outs to a pair plus various overcards and outs to the best hand. A bet of 350 (roughly 3/4 pot) is standard sizing for a c-bet in this spot. This maximizes fold equity while maintaining proper proportions.

On the flop of 9h-6s-3s, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7) and one overcard (the Ten). Claude, who raised from the button pre-flop, has followed through with a continuation bet of 350 into a pot of 650. This is roughly half the pot. My direct equity with a gutshot is approximately 16%, while the pot odds offered require about 26% equity to break even (350 to win a total pot of 1350). Being out of position with only 10-high and a non-nut draw makes a call unprofitable here. I do not have a spade in my hand to provide backdoor flush equity, and my overcard is weak. Therefore, the most disciplined play is to fold.

I have pocket Aces on the button with only the small and big blinds left to act. This is the best preflop hand — I want to raise for value and to isolate the blinds. A standard 3xBB open to 300 (300 total in the pot from me) accomplishes that: it charges speculative hands, keeps worse aces/broadways and many suited connectors/pairs in the pot, and is a conventional size given our deep stacks. Plan: if I face a 3-bet I will 4-bet for value or adjust depending on sizing; if I get called I'll play postflop for max value. Raise to 300.

I am in the Small Blind with 7c 3c. Minni, who is on the button, has raised to 300. My hand is very weak and does not play well out of position. Even though it is suited, 7-3 is significantly below the average defending range from the small blind against a button open. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here to conserve my stack.

K♠ T♦ is a solid hand from the big blind in a heads-up pot. Minni raised to 300 from the button, Flash folded, leaving just me and Minni. The pot is 450 and I only need to call 200 more, giving me favorable odds of 2.25:1. 

K-Ten offsuit has good playability with potential to make top pair or better, and the hand strength warrants defending my big blind in this spot. I'm getting proper pot odds, and heads-up play requires a wider range of calling hands. This is a standard call.

I'm holding K♠ T♦ on a 2♥ 6♣ A♠ flop in a heads-up pot with Minni. I called her button raise from the big blind preflop, but this flop is not favorable for my hand. I have king-high with a weak ten kicker—essentially a bluff-catching hand at best. The ace on the flop is particularly concerning as it likely improved Minni's range significantly from her button raising position. I'm out of position and lack a made hand, so betting here would be weak and transparent. Checking allows me to keep the pot small with a marginal hand, see Minni's action, and make a more informed decision on subsequent streets. If Minni checks back, I can reassess on the turn. If she bets, I can decide whether her bet size and my hand strength warrant a call or a fold. This is a standard check in position disadvantage with a borderline hand.

I flopped trips (A♠ on 2♥-6♣-A♠) with pocket A♥A♦ — effectively the best hand on this dry board. Claude checked; I should bet for value and to build the pot while charging any Ax, small pocket pairs (22/66/77 etc.), and weaker broadways that called preflop. A bet of 400 (~60% of the 650 pot) is large enough to get calls from those hands without scaring them all off and leaves room to continue extracting on later streets from worse hands. If Claude calls I’ll keep betting on safe turn cards; if he check-raises or jams, I’ll re-evaluate but am happy to commit a large portion of my stack given trips this strong.

K♠ T♦ on a 2♥ 6♣ A♠ board is a bluff catcher at best. The ace on the flop likely improved Minni's range significantly from her button raise. I have king-high with poor equity against her likely holdings (aces, pairs, broadway cards). Being out of position for the turn makes this decision even clearer. Minni's bet sizing suggests hand strength. With minimal equity and positional disadvantage, folding preserves chips for better spots.

I am on the button with Jack-Seven offsuit. While this hand can occasionally be used as a steal attempt in late position, it is generally too weak to open against active opponents like Claude and Minni. Given the history of the game where Minni defends her blinds aggressively and Claude has shown willingness to 3-bet, folding is the most prudent and disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I'm in the Small Blind heads-up against Minni in the Big Blind after Flash folds. With Q4o, I'm receiving favorable pot odds of 3:1 (150 chips in pot to call 50 more). While Q4o is marginal, in heads-up play this hand has sufficient equity to justify calling given the odds. Folding would be too tight; calling allows me to see a flop against a single opponent where position and hand strength will matter more. I'll complete my small blind for a total of 100 chips.

Heads-up after Claude (SB) just called the extra 50 — my Q♦8♥ is a marginal, playable hand but not a strong 3-betting or isolation candidate. Pot is only 200 and stacks are deep (~180+ BBs), so I prefer to keep the pot small and realize equity post‑flop rather than build a bigger pot with a marginal hand. Claude has shown a lot of limp/call and passive lines in prior hands, so checking lets me see a flop against a wide range and use postflop skill to extract value or fold cheaply. Plan: check and evaluate the flop; c-bet only when I hit or have a strong draw or when the texture favors a bluff; fold to large aggression when completely missed.

Checking with Q4o on a 5s-3h-Ts flop. My hand is extremely weak with no made hand or draws. Minni's check suggests she also has a marginal hand. Betting out of position with this hand is poor strategy - I need either the best hand (unlikely) or significant equity (which I lack). Checking allows me to see the turn cheaply, where I might improve to Queen, Four, or catch a favorable card. This is the disciplined play with a weak hand in position disadvantage.

Heads-up vs a limp/caller who checked the flop. I have Qh8d on a T♠-5♠-3♥ board: no pair, no real draw (only a backdoor heart/diamond possibilities) and two spades on board make turn/river spade cards dangerous. Pot is tiny (200) and stacks are very deep, so I want pot control and to realize equity rather than build with a marginal hand. Claude’s passive line suggests a wide, often weak/catching-up range; checking lets me see a turn for free, fold cheaply to real aggression, and only invest when I improve or get an actionable read. Plan: check now; call small turn bets to see a river if appropriate, fold to large turn/river bets, and only barrel/raise if I pair the Q or pick up a strong draw.

On the turn with Q4o on a board of 5s-3h-Ts-2d, I have a very weak hand (Q-high with no real equity). The deuce doesn't help my hand at all. Minni checked the flop, indicating she also likely has a marginal holding. In heads-up play, there's no point in betting a bluff here with such weak hand strength - I have no draw, no showdown value to speak of, and would only win if Minni folds. Given the pot is only 200 chips and I have a deep stack (10,725), it's correct to check and see the river cheaply. If I catch a Q or 4 on the river, I'll have showdown value. If not, I can fold to any river aggression. This is the disciplined, efficient line with a weak holding.

Board T♠-5♠-3♥-2♦, I hold Q♥8♦ — no pair and no meaningful draw (only a backdoor diamond). Claude limp‑called preflop and then checked both flop and turn, indicating a wide, often weak/passive range (small pairs, missed broadways, speculative hands). Pot is tiny (~200) and both stacks are deep (>100bb), so building the pot with a marginal hand is unnecessary and exploitable. I choose pot control: check the turn to realize equity, see the river for free, and only invest further if I improve or get a clear, small bluffable action from Claude. If he fires a small river probe I can call for pot control; I’ll fold to large, polarizing aggression.

Checking on the river with Q-high. Minni's check-through pattern (flop and turn) indicates a weak hand, likely worse than Q-high. Betting would be a bluff with minimal equity; checking allows cheap showdown where my hand likely wins. With deep stacks and small pot, there's no value in risking further chips by betting a weak hand. Going to showdown with Q-high against apparent weakness is the most efficient play.

I have Q♥8♦ on a paired-ten river (5♠-3♥-T♠-2♦-T♥). Claude limp‑called preflop and then checked flop, turn and river — a very passive line consistent with small pocket pairs, missed broadways, or slow‑played tens. I have no pair and essentially zero value; my only realistic path to winning is bluffing. The pot is tiny (≈200) while stacks are very deep (~180+ BB), and against a passive, calling opponent my fold equity is low. Betting here would risk chips to fold hands Claude is unlikely to release (he checked every street) and create unnecessary difficult decisions with deep stacks. I therefore take the conservative, +EV line: check and fold to any meaningful bet. If Claude makes a tiny probe I can occasionally call, but I will not purchase a bluff into a deep stack for such a small pot.

I'm on the button with pocket Kings (Kh-Kd), one of the strongest starting hands in poker. The action is on me with 100 to call. Looking at the game flow and my position, this is a clear raise situation. I'll raise to 300 (3x the big blind), which is a standard aggressive raise with a premium hand. This bet size will build the pot, allow me to gather information about my opponents' hands, and maintain positional advantage on the button. My stack of 10,725 is healthy enough to play this hand aggressively. A raise here is standard poker theory with KK in position.

Facing a BTN open to 300 from 3 players, J♦9♠ is marginal OOP and I don’t want to limp/call into a likely multiway pot. Calling 250 gives poor multiway equity and leaves me OOP; folding is too tight in 3-handed play where we should apply pressure. I’ll 3-bet to 900 (≈3x the open) to get fold equity and isolate Claude or take the initiative.
