Size is big enough to discourage some cold calls from the BB but small relative to our deep stacks so we keep postflop maneuverability. Plan: fold to a large 4-bet, call/single-call and proceed cautiously OOP (c-bet good flops, pot-control dry boards, fold to heavy resistance unless we improve).

I am holding 7c 2c in the Big Blind. Claude raised from the Button, and Minni 3-bet from the Small Blind. My hand is one of the weakest possible starting hands, and even though it is suited, it is not worth calling a 3-bet out of position. I will fold.

Pocket Kings is the third-best starting hand in poker and plays excellently heads-up. After my initial raise to 300, Minni re-raised to 900 from the small blind, showing aggression. Flash folded, leaving this as a heads-up battle. With only 600 more to call, folding is not an option. A re-raise applies pressure, builds the pot while I'm likely ahead, and maintains my positional advantage on the button. Raising to 2,200 total is an aggressive but measured response that balances pot building with stack preservation. This is standard 3-bet strategy with a premium hand in position.

Fold. Sequence: Claude opened to 300, I 3‑bet to 900 to isolate and take the initiative, Flash folded and Claude 4‑bet to 2200. The pot is ~3,200 and I must call 1,300 to continue. J♦9♠ off is a marginal hand that is largely dominated by a BTN 4‑betting range (AA, KK, QQ, JJ, AK and strong broadways). Calling 1,300 into 3,200 requires ~29% equity to break even; J9o’s equity vs a realistic 4‑bet value/heavy range is well below that, and I’d be playing the rest of the hand out of position with limited fold equity. A 5‑bet bluff here is too risky (small 5‑bet will often be called or jammed and I don’t block enough of Claude’s value/bluff combos). Per my preflop plan — fold to a large 4‑bet — this is the lowest variance, highest EV play over the long run.

Button with Td3h is a weak offsuit hand but this is a classic spot to open-steal from the button against the blinds. The pot is only 150 and I have deep stacks/position, so a standard 3xBB open to 300 maximizes fold equity while keeping the pot small if I get called. Plan: c-bet on dry flops, proceed cautiously postflop (fold to significant 3-bets or large aggression since this hand has poor postflop playability). If I face a single caller I can continue to use position; fold to a sizable 3-bet — this is a steal attempt, not a get-it-in spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Nine offsuit. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. While Q9o has decent equity against a wide button opening range, playing it out of position for the rest of the hand is difficult, especially with an aggressive player like Claude still to act in the Big Blind. To maintain my stack and avoid marginal spots out of position, I will fold this hand.

K2o is a weak hand with limited playability. While I'm facing a 200 chip call with 450 in the pot (2.25:1 odds), K2o doesn't have sufficient equity to justify the call, especially out of position against Minni's button raise. Minni has demonstrated an active raising style throughout the session, but even against a wide button range, K2o performs poorly post-flop in an out-of-position scenario. The risk-reward doesn't favor playing this weak holding. Folding is the standard play here.

I am on the button with 8s 7d. In a three-handed game, this hand is close to the bottom of a standard opening range. Given that Minni (Big Blind) has been playing aggressively and frequently defending or re-raising, and Claude (Small Blind) has also shown willingness to 4-bet, I will choose to fold this hand to avoid playing a weak offsuit connector out of position or facing a three-bet.

Raising to 250 with JcTs from the small blind. This hand has strong broadway equity and can win both preflop and postflop. I'm getting good odds to call, but raising is better as it applies pressure, builds the pot with a strong hand, and allows me to control the action. A 2.5x raise is standard and isn't overly aggressive while still putting Minni in a difficult decision with her big blind range.

I have A♣Q♥ in the big blind facing a 250 open from the small blind (to call 150). This is a clear 3-bet spot: AQs is ahead of a lot of the button’s likely opening range and I want to isolate, deny good odds to his wide calling hands, and take the initiative. I’ll make a standard-sized 3-bet to 900 total (≈3.5–4x his open) — large enough to charge weaker Ax, Kx and many broadways, but not so huge that I overcommit unnecessarily. If he folds I win the pot; if he calls we have strong equity and can play postflop knowing his range is wide; if he 4-bets big I can re-evaluate (and often fold to heavy 4-bets given stack depths and my hand).

JcTs is a strong broadway hand with good equity against Minni's 3-bet range. While I'm out of position, I have decent fold equity if the board misses her holdings, and strong playability with top pair or better. Minni has shown a fairly aggressive playing style throughout the session, suggesting her 3-bet range is somewhat wide. Calling 650 more into a 1300 chip pot gives me 2:1 odds on the call, which is reasonable for a hand with equity against her range. A 4-bet would commit too much with this hand out of position. I'll take a flop and make postflop decisions based on the board texture and her actions.

Checking on the flop with JcTs against Minni's 3-bet. The Ad is highly problematic for broadway cards, and Minni's range includes many hands that improved significantly (Aces, premium pairs). With no made hand and poor equity, checking allows me to see her strength while controlling pot commitment. Out of position with a weak holding, I need information before committing further chips. If she checks behind, I keep the pot manageable. If she bets, I can fold or call based on pot odds and read on her hand strength.

Flop: Ad 4h 8h. I have A♣Q♥ — top pair with a good kicker and a backdoor heart. Claude cold‑called my 3‑bet preflop, so his range is wide (Ax, broadways, medium pairs). He checked the flop, which can be weakness or a deception/slowplay. In position with top pair I want to both get value and protect against the two‑heart board, so I’ll bet for value and to charge heart draws. A ~2/3‑pot size balances extracting value from worse Aces, Kx and medium pairs while making draws pay to see cards. If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll reassess the turn (be particularly cautious if a heart or a 4/8 pairs the board). If he check‑raises big, that typically represents sets or a very strong A♥/As combo and I’ll strongly consider folding to large aggression.

Folding JcTs on Ad 4h 8h after Minni's 3-bet and aggressive flop bet. The Ace flop severely diminishes my broadway hand to essentially J-high with limited equity (25-32% against her range). Although pot odds are borderline at 3.5:1, I lack sufficient equity, am out of position, and her confident betting pattern indicates strong holdings. The poor postflop playability of unimproved JT makes this a negative expectation fold despite being near breakeven on immediate odds.

With Jack-Three offsuit on the button against two opponents who have already posted blinds, this is a clear fold. J3o is a marginal hand at best—it lacks the strength or connectivity to justify entering the pot. While my button position is favorable, it doesn't compensate for the weakness of this holding. Against Minni (small blind at 18,725) and Flash (big blind at 10,400), I'll be out of position post-flop with a hand that's unlikely to win without significant help. The cost to call (100) to win a 150 pot (1.5:1 odds) isn't justified given my hand strength and positional disadvantage once we see a flop. Folding preserves my stack (10,725) and allows me to wait for stronger opportunities.

Heads-up vs the big blind from the small blind. J♦6♠ is marginal but worth playing aggressively in this spot — calling 50 is cheap but cedes initiative and lets the BB see the flop for a discount. I’ll raise to the min (200 total) to take the lead, apply fold equity versus Flash’s wide defending range, and keep the pot manageable with deep stacks. If he 3-bets big I’ll fold or re-evaluate by sizing; if he calls I’ll C-bet appropriate flops and play position/aggression postflop.

I have Ace-King offsuit (AKo) in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand. The Small Blind (Minni) has raised to 2x (200). In a heads-up situation against the SB, I want to 3-bet for value and to gain initiative. A standard 3-bet size from the BB against a 200 open is roughly 3.5x to 4x, so I will raise to 700 to put pressure on Minni's wide opening range and build a pot with my superior hand.

I opened to 200 with J♦6♠ to seize initiative, but Flash’s 3‑bet to 700 changes the math. I’d have to call 500 into a 900 pot (pot ≈1400 after my call), so I need ~36% equity to break even — J6o OOP doesn’t have that vs a 3‑betting range and is frequently dominated and hard to play postflop. A small 4‑bet won’t fold enough hands and a large shove is too high variance with a marginal holding. With deep stacks and better spots ahead, folding preserves equity and avoids playing a weak hand out of position against aggression.

Q♦6♠ on the button is a marginal hand but in a 3-handed game vs only the blinds it's part of a standard open-steal range. I want to use position and fold equity rather than limp and play a dominated hand multiway. I will raise to 300 (around 3x the BB) — big enough to be credible and pick up the blinds often, but small enough not to overcommit with a weak holding. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet (Q6o performs poorly vs a 3-bet range); if called, play postflop in position, c-betting favorable textures or exercising pot control when behind.

I am in the Small Blind with 5s2c, a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. While I have the chips to defend, this hand has poor playability post-flop and the stack-to-pot ratio doesn't favor a bluff or a call here, especially with the Big Blind (Claude) still to act. Folding is the most profitable long-term play.

Calling J7o from the big blind against Minni's button raise. Minni has shown a very aggressive raising pattern throughout the session, indicating a wide button range. I'm getting 2.25:1 pot odds (450 to call 200), which provides adequate equity for a hand with straight-making potential. While J7o is weak, defending the big blind with decent odds against an aggressive opponent is standard strategy. I can exploit Minni's tendencies postflop and make position-adjusted decisions based on board texture.

Minni checked from the button after raising pre-flop, suggesting weakness or a cautious approach. My J7o has an open-ended straight draw (any King or 6 completes a straight for 8 outs), plus potential over-card value with the Jack. The dry 3-8-9 flop is unlikely to have connected well with Minni's wide button raising range, which they've demonstrated throughout this session. Betting 450 applies pressure on what is likely a weak hand, forces Minni to make a decision, and my semi-bluff has sufficient equity if called. This aggressive action exploits Minni's tendency to give up on flops and is standard strategy for protecting the big blind with a draw against aggressive opponents. The bet size (~70% of pot) is appropriate given stack sizes and board texture.

Board is 3c 8h 9h; I have Qd6s — no pair, no heart draw, only two overcards (Q and 6). Claude called preflop and now bets 450 into the ~650 pre-bet pot (pot shows 1100 after his bet). To call 450 I'd need ~29.0% equity (450 / (1100+450) ≈ 29.0%). Realistic outs: 3 queens + 3 sixes = 6 outs → ~24.1% to hit by river (and many of those hits can still be dominated). Claude’s line (call preflop then a sizable flop bet on a two-heart, connected board) is consistent with a lot of made hands and strong draws, so I don’t trust his bluff frequency is high enough to make a call + turn play profitable. Raising to 900 as a bluff is too risky — it commits more chips and will be called or three-bet by many hands that beat me. With poor raw equity and a clear fold-worthy situation, I preserve chips for better spots and fold.

I am on the button with Deuce-Four of Hearts. While suited, this is one of the weakest hands in poker. Both Claude and Minni have shown significant pre-flop aggression in previous hands. Raising here would be a pure steal attempt with very low equity if called. Given the stack sizes and the aggressive nature of the blinds, it's better to wait for a stronger hand and preserve chips.

I'm in the small blind with 6h-Qs, a weak offsuit hand with limited potential. While I only need to call 50 more chips (3:1 pot odds), I must consider several factors: (1) 6-Q offsuit plays poorly postflop and has limited equity, (2) I'll be out of position against Minni in the big blind, (3) Minni has demonstrated consistent aggression throughout this session, and (4) my stack is moderate, making it prudent to preserve chips for stronger opportunities. The small blind is the correct position to fold weak hands when facing aggressive opponents. Folding here is a disciplined decision.

4-3 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. While I'm in good position on the button, this hand lacks both high card value and connectivity to make straights effectively. The hand is too weak to justify playing, even with favorable position. I'm only risking 100 chips to call, but the risk/reward is unfavorable given how poorly 4-3o plays post-flop. With a healthy stack (11,025) and 80 more hands to play, I should preserve chips for premium hands. The pot is also small (150 chips), so there's minimal benefit to entering. Folding preserves my edge and allows me to play tighter ranges with better hands.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with A♠T♦ — this is a strong, playable hand that does well both as a value hand and as a blocker to A‑x hands. Pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to call, but calling is passive and surrenders initiative. I want to seize position postflop and get fold equity, so I’ll open‑raise. Min raise is to 200 but I’ll size to a standard 3x BB to 300 (total commitment) — big enough to isolate the big blind and build a pot when I have the likely best hand, but small relative to deep stacks so I can fold or continue comfortably if I face heavy resistance. If Flash calls I play position postflop; if he 3‑bets I’ll reassess based on sizing and range.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit (Ad 8s) in a 3-handed game. The Button has folded, and the Small Blind (Minni) has raised to 3BB (300). Ace-Eight is a strong hand in a blind-vs-blind scenario. Since I have position on the Small Blind for the rest of the hand and the hand is strong enough to play against a wide SB opening range but not necessarily strong enough to 3-bet for value against a potential 4-bet, calling is the most profitable long-term play here.

I have top pair with the best kicker (A♠T♦ on A♥5♠7♦) and I’m in position as the preflop aggressor. This is a relatively dry flop (only a single spade and no obvious coordinated straight cards), so my hand is ahead of most of the BB’s calling range (weaker A‑x, Kx, medium pairs, and some sweeper draws). I want to extract value, deny equity to backdoor draws, and keep worse aces and medium pairs in the pot. A standard value c‑bet of about 50% pot is ideal — big enough to get value and protect, but small enough to get called by worse hands. If I get raised I’ll give strong consideration to folding to a large raise (that typically represents sets/two‑pair); if called I’ll proceed to control the pot in position and continue extracting value on safe turn cards.

I have Top Pair with an Eight kicker on a dry board (Ah-5s-7d) in a blind-vs-blind situation. My hand is strong, but raising here might isolate me against better Aces or sets, whereas calling allows Minni to continue bluffing or betting with worse hands. Given I have position and a solid made hand, calling is the most effective way to control the pot and extract value.

I’m in position with top pair/top kicker (A♠T♦) on A♥5♠7♦3♠. The pot is 1,200 and Flash called my flop bet, so his range contains worse A‑x, medium pairs and some two‑card spade combos that can turn into a river flush. The 3♠ increases the backdoor/spade potential but I hold the A♠ which blocks the strongest spade combos. I want to extract value from worse aces and medium pairs and charge any two‑spade draws heading to the river, while keeping the pot under control with deep stacks. A standard value bet of roughly 50% pot (~600) accomplishes that: it gets called by worse hands and makes draws pay, but doesn’t overcommit me if I face heavy resistance. Plan: if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the river (value bet thin on safe cards, check or fold to large aggression on scary rivers); fold to an oversized check‑raise on the turn which would represent sets/two‑pair.

In this blind-vs-blind situation, I have Top Pair (Aces) with an Eight kicker on a relatively dry board. Minni's second barrel on the turn for half-pot (600 into 1200) could represent a variety of hands, including stronger Aces, sets, or semi-bluffs. Since I am in position and my hand is a strong mid-strength hand in this range, calling is the most appropriate action. Raising would likely only be called by better hands, while calling allows Minni to continue with bluffs or thinner value bets on the river.

I have A♠T♦ on A♥5♠7♦3♠4♥ — top pair with the best kicker and I’m the preflop/streets aggressor in position. Flash called my preflop raise, my 300 flop c‑bet and my 600 turn bet, which looks like a sticky calling range (worse A‑x, medium pocket pairs like 5s/7s, and some missed/backdoor draws). The river 4♥ is a harmless card — it doesn’t complete straights or a flush (and my A♠ blocks a lot of spade combos), so this is a good spot to extract thin value. I’ll make a value bet of about 50% pot (1,200 into 2,400) to get called by worse aces, pairs and missed draws while not bloating the pot against the rare two‑pair/set. If he raises big, I’ll give that credit and fold to avoid losing a large portion of my stack.

I have Top Pair (Aces) with an Eight kicker in a blind-vs-blind situation. The board has become quite coordinated on the river, with the 4h completing potential straights for any 6 (3-4-5-6-7) or 2 (A-2-3-4-5). However, Minni's triple-barrel line (300 on flop, 600 on turn, 1200 on river) is a standard aggressive line in a 3-handed game and could be for value with a weaker Ace, or a bluff with missed draws like a backdoor flush. Given the pot odds (3:1), I only need to be right 25% of the time to make calling profitable. While my hand is only a bluff-catcher against her value range (which includes many two-pair combinations and straights), it is too high in my range to fold in this specific positional battle. I will call to see the showdown.

I’m on the button with Js6d and it’s folded to me facing only the blinds. This is a marginal hand but position is a big advantage — I want to take the initiative and apply pressure to the blinds rather than limp. A small standard open to the minimum (200 total) preserves stack depth, maximizes fold equity, and keeps the pot manageable if called. If I face a significant 3-bet I will fold this holding; if called I’ll c-bet favorable textures and play cautiously on coordinated/overs cards. Overall this raise aims to steal the blinds or see a flop in position with a reasonable SPR.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Nine of hearts, a strong suited king. Minni opened with a small raise to 200 from the button. Given the pot odds and the strength of my hand in a 3-handed game, this is a clear continue. I could raise, but calling allows me to keep the pot manageable out of position and see a flop with a hand that has good post-flop playability and nut-flush potential.

Td7c is a marginal hand that should not be played in this spot. Despite getting 5:1 pot odds, I'm facing two opponents and will be out of position postflop. The hand has modest potential (four-card straight draw possibilities) but lacks the value to justify calling in a multiway pot, especially given my relatively tight stack of 10,925 chips. This is a good spot to fold and preserve chips for stronger opportunities where I'm not playing a weak, unpaired hand from the blinds against multiple opponents.

On the flop of Kd-5d-Ac, I have middle pair (Kings) with a Nine kicker. This is a solid but vulnerable hand on a board that is very likely to have connected with the button's opening range (which contains many Aces and some Kings). Minni checked back or hasn't acted yet? Wait, the log says Minni raised pre-flop, I called, Claude folded. Now on the flop, I am first to act. Checking is the most standard play here. I want to control the pot size with a medium-strength hand. If Minni bets, I will likely call one street to see how the turn develops, as I have a pair and some backdoor potential, though the Ace is a scary card.

I opened to 200 from the button with Js6d and got one caller from the SB. The flop A♣ K♦ 5♦ is very unfriendly to my hand — I have no pair, only a lone 6 with a remote backdoor diamond runner, and both Ace and King are big overcards that hit the caller’s likely calling range (AX, KX, paired hands, small pairs). Flash checking here can be either weakness or pot‑control with a strong holding, and my fold equity is limited. A c-bet as a bluff would be thin and likely to get called by a lot of hands that beat me; betting also bloats a pot I’m behind in. With position and lots of turn cards to realize equity or bluff on later streets, the lowest‑variance + highest‑EV line is to check and use position: call small bets to float and continue bluffing on favorable turns, but fold to large, polarizing pressure. Therefore I check and see the turn.

On the turn, the board is 5d-Kd-Ac-Ts. I have a pair of Kings with a nine kicker. The flop was checked through, which suggests Minni might not have an Ace, or at least isn't playing one aggressively. However, the Ten of Spades on the turn is another high card that could have improved her range (e.g., QJ for a straight, or sets of Tens). With a medium-strength hand out of position, checking is the most effective way to control the pot size and reach a showdown. If Minni checks again, I can likely see the river for free; if she bets, I will re-evaluate based on the sizing, but my pair of Kings is generally strong enough to consider a call on this line.

I opened from the button and got one caller. Flop (A♣ K♦ 5♦) was very hostile to my J♠6♦ so I checked to keep the pot small; Flash checked the turn as well. I have no pair and only a faint river equity (4 queens for a straight, 3 remaining jacks to pair — plus weak 6s — so ~7 'outs' but many of those still lose to A/K or better pairs), and with stacks ~8k/20k vs a 500 pot I have almost no fold equity when betting the turn into a caller with lots of A/Kx, pairs and floats. Betting here would bloat a pot I’m a big dog in and often get called; check‑back is lower variance and preserves the option value of bluffing or value‑betting the river if I hit (Q/J) or if Villain shows weakness. Plan: check, fold to large polarizing aggression, call small probes to float and attempt to seize the pot on a favorable river, otherwise give up.

On the river with a board of 5d-Kd-Ac-Ts-8c, I hold second pair (Kings). The action has been checked through on both the flop and the turn. This sequence suggests that Minni, who raised pre-flop from the button, likely does not have an Ace or a very strong King. By checking again on the river, I am looking to reach a cheap showdown with my medium-strength hand. If Minni bets, I will have to evaluate the sizing, but in a pot where no one has shown interest, my pair of Kings is frequently the best hand.

Board A♣ K♦ 5♦ / T♠ / 8♣ and I hold J♠6♦ — I have no pair, no legitimate river equity and no credible blocker to strong parts of SB’s range. Pot is tiny (500) versus deep effective stacks (~8k+), and Flash called preflop then checked every street — range is likely A‑x, K‑x, some tens or small pairs and occasional floaters. Betting here has very little fold equity: a small bluff will get called for good pot odds by Kx/Ax/Tx/pairs and a large bluff is too expensive if called. With no value and minimal fold equity the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV line is to check and fold to significant aggression at this price — don’t bloat the pot on air.

I am on the button with 9-4 offsuit. This is a very weak hand. In a 3-handed game, I have the advantage of position, but 9-4o is significantly below the opening range for a button. Even with a 1.5bb call to make (since I am the dealer and have already posted the small blind equivalent of the pot's dead money in some structures, but here I am the button and facing the blinds), the hand is too weak to play. Standard play is to fold and wait for a better spot, as Claude and Minni have both shown willingness to be aggressive pre-flop.

In heads-up play against Minni's big blind, K4o gets proper odds (3:1) to call the remaining 50 chips. Although K4 is weak, it has showdown value, and the pot odds justify seeing the flop. Folding wastes my SB investment. A raise would overvalue this hand, so calling is the optimal play.

Heads-up after a limp from the small blind. I have Q♠7♦ — a marginal, often-dominated hand with poor playability out of position. Pot is small (200) and I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a weak offsuit queen. A min-raise to 200 is feasible to isolate, but Claude looks capable of calling/playing back and I’d rather realize equity cheaply and keep pot control with deep stacks and many hands left. I’ll check and see a flop; plan to call small c-bets vs a wide limp-range but fold to large aggression on unfavorable boards, and only turn aggression when I pick up real equity or precise bluffing spots.

On the flop with K♠4♦ against Qd-9h-3d, I have only King-high with a weak kicker. This is a vulnerable hand in heads-up play. My options are limited: betting K4o into Minni is weak and invites aggression from a frequently aggressive opponent, while I retain minimal equity against most of her range. I have some showdown value with the King and a backdoor diamond draw (4♦ gives me one-card flush draws). Checking allows me to see Minni's action without committing more chips to a marginal holding. If Minni checks, I can control the pot size and potentially reach showdown cheaply. If Minni bets, I can make an informed decision on whether to call, fold, or raise based on her action. This is the most prudent play given the hand strength and board texture.

I have top pair (Q♠) with a weak kicker on Q♦9♥3♦. Claude limped preflop, so his range is wide (worse queens, small pairs, 9x, and many diamond draws). Betting for value and protection is correct here — I want to charge diamond draws and get value from worse Qx/9x/pocket threes while keeping the pot controlled with a marginal kicker. A half-pot bet (~100 into 200) hits that balance: it prices draws, keeps worse hands calling, and lets me fold to strong check-raises without bloating the pot. Plan: bet 100; if Claude raises big I’ll strongly consider folding, but I’ll call small raises and re-evaluate on the turn.

On the flop Qd-9h-3d with K♠4♦, I'm getting 4:1 pot odds (400 in pot to call 100). While K4o is weak against Minni's aggressive range, I have several factors working in my favor: (1) King-high retains showdown value, (2) I have a backdoor diamond draw (two diamonds would make a strong holding), (3) the pot odds are attractive relative to my equity, and (4) Minni frequently continuation bets with a wide range. Folding would waste my SB investment and overlook the implied odds. Raising seems overvalued with this weak hand. Calling keeps the hand alive, allows me to see the turn with more information, and is consistent with a balanced approach against aggressive opponents. If the turn brings a diamond, a face card, or a helpful card, I can apply pressure or call more confidently on future streets.

On the turn with K♠4♦ against Qd-9h-3d-8d, I have improved my hand. The 8d is significant—I now have a four-flush (a near-complete flush draw with 9 remaining diamond outs). With a flush draw, my equity improves to roughly 20-25% against Minni's range. However, K-high remains vulnerable, and the hand doesn't warrant a turn bet as a value play. Checking is superior because: (1) It allows me to control pot size while keeping maximum equity—if Minni checks, I see the river cheaply; if she bets, I get better odds for a turn call with 9 flush outs plus King-high kicker; (2) The implied odds favor calling a reasonable bet here—if I hit my flush, I win a large pot; (3) Against Minni's aggressive tendencies, betting K4o with a draw is too transparent and invites larger aggression; (4) Checking preserves the line consistency from the flop. If Minni bets, I'll re-evaluate the pot odds and action, but from first-to-act, checking is the most balanced, equity-preserving play.

I have top pair Q with a very weak 7-kicker (Q♠7♦). The turn 8♦ makes the board Q♦-9♥-3♦-8♦ — there are now three diamonds on board, so any two-diamond holding has a made flush. Claude limped/called the small flop bet, so his range is wide and includes single-pair hands, small pairs, and diamond combos. My hand is vulnerable: I’ve only one diamond (four to a flush) and a poor kicker, so betting the turn risks getting called (or raised) by hands that already beat me or by draws that have good equity. Pot control is the right line — avoid bloating the pot with a marginal one-pair hand. I’ll check the turn and re-evaluate on the river: call reasonable/​small river bets for protection and to catch bluffs, but fold to large river bets that represent completed flushes/strong hands.

On the river with the completed board Qd-9h-3d-8d-3h, I hold K♠4♦, which gives me a pair of 3s (the pair on board) with K-Q-9-8 kickers. This is a made hand with legitimate showdown value. Minni's turn check suggests either weakness (she's given up) or a medium-strength hand—not the aggressive line of someone with a strong hand. On the river, I need to evaluate my position: (1) Betting K-high with 3s pairs has minimal value gain—worse hands typically fold anyway, while better hands (8s, 9s, pair of Qs, or better kicker with 3s) call and win. (2) My pair of 3s beats any unpaired hand or weak holdings, which is exactly what Minni's checked line suggests she might have. (3) Checking allows me to reach showdown safely and win the pot if Minni has missed draws, weak cards, or King-high type hands. (4) The pot-to-stack ratio (400/10,725) doesn't warrant inflating the pot with a mediocre showdown hand. Checking is the prudent play to realize my hand's showdown value without overextending against Minni's cautious line.

I have Q♠7♦ — top pair but with a very weak kicker on Q♦-9♥-3♦-8♦-3♥. Claude limped preflop and called my small flop raise, so his calling range is wide and includes better Qx kickers, 9x, small pairs and the two-diamond combos that made a flush when the 8♦ turned. I checked the turn to control the pot against those dangerous made hands and to avoid bloating the pot with a marginal one-pair hand. With Claude checking the river as well, thin value is scarce and betting only gives fold equity against hands I already beat while exposing me to raises from hands that dominate me (better queens) or completed flushes. I’ll check and take showdown; if he now leads out for a small stab I’ll consider a call, but I’ll fold to meaningful river aggression.

7-Q offsuit is a weak, marginal hand that doesn't justify a call even from the button. Although position is favorable and pot odds are reasonable (150:100), I should be selective with my range and only play stronger hands. This folds cleanly without investing chips in a weak spot.

6♠2♣ is one of the weakest starting hands and plays terribly out of position. Pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to call, but calling OOP with this hand frequently leads to multi-street losses or being dominated on the flop. Flash has shown aggressive tendencies, so I’m likely to face pressure postflop and won’t have good equity or playability. Raising is absurd with this holding. With a deep stack and plenty of hands left, folding and preserving chips is the highest EV play here.

On the button with Kc2s facing only the blinds, I want to open for value and fold equity rather than limp. K2o is marginal but in position I can frequently steal the blinds and play postflop with initiative. I size to 3x the big blind (300) — larger than the min-raise to get more folds and avoid giving cheap prices to the SB/BB, but not so large that a 3-bet commits me. Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet, otherwise play postflop aggressively in position.

In the Small Blind with 9h 8c against a 3x raise from the button (Minni), I am looking at a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. While 3-handed play involves wider ranges, 98o is typically a fold in this spot to avoid being trapped in a bloated pot with a weak hand against a large stack.

Call with 8-Td from the big blind against Minni's button raise. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds with a hand that has ~40-45% equity against a wide range. The hand has straight potential (any 9, 6, 7 helps). Minni's button raise ranges are frequently wide based on her playing patterns. Positive expected value play with reasonable stack cushion.

Checking the flop with 8-Td on 5h-Ac-2h. The Ace strongly hits Minni's button-raising range, and I'm out of position. While I have 12-15 straight draw outs and ~30-32% equity, betting here is poor EV against likely made hands. Checking allows me to control pot size, get favorable odds to call if Minni c-bets, and keep the pot manageable. The draw has sufficient equity to see another card if I can get appropriate odds.

I opened preflop and Claude called from the big blind, so his calling range is wide and includes many Ax combos, Kx and medium pairs. The flop (5♥ A♣ 2♥) contains an Ace that hits his range hard; I hold K♣2♠ — only bottom pair. Betting here as the preflop aggressor would fold out pure air but will be called too often by Ax and many hands that dominate me, so c-betting has poor risk/reward. With position and a marginal made hand I prefer pot control: check to keep the pot small, realize equity vs his weaker holdings, and use the turn to re-evaluate (call small bets to see more cards, fold to large, polarized aggression). Checking also allows me to induce bluffs or extract thin value later when he shows weakness.

Checking is the optimal play on this turn. I have an 8-out straight draw on a board where Minni showed weakness by checking the flop. My equity is roughly 30-35%, making a bet with this hand inferior to taking a free card. Checking allows me to see the river for free and significantly improves my position if a 6 or 9 comes. Given my marginal current hand strength and out-of-position disadvantage, controlling the pot size and improving my draw odds justifies the conservative line.

I remain pot‑controlled. I have only bottom pair (K2) and Claude’s check‑check line heavily weights both air and Ax/medium pairs. A turn bet will get called too often by better hands (and won’t fold enough worse hands to be profitable) and would bloat the pot out of position. Checking lets me realize equity, induce bluffs, and re‑evaluate on the river — I’ll call small river bets, fold to large raises, and consider a thin value bet if he checks again.

River with 9-high (8-T on 5-A-2-4-9 board). No made hand, no outs, complete air. Minni has shown weakness throughout the hand with check-downs on flop and turn, but she still has a button-raising range with many hands that beat me. Bluffing 9-high in a 650-chip pot has poor risk-reward. Checking allows me to control EV: if Minni checks back, there's a small chance she has air; if she bets, I fold and save chips. This is standard exploitative river play against a weak board texture.

I have K♣2♠ — bottom pair (pair of twos) with a decent kicker. Claude called my preflop raise and then checked flop, turn and river, a line that can include both Ax (which beat me) and a lot of weak or missed hands. In position I prefer a small thin-value bet to extract from worse Kx and other marginal holdings while keeping the pot controllable. I size ~1/3 pot (200) to target thin calls and to keep my loss limited if he shows strength; if he raises I’ll interpret that as a hand that dominates me (A or better) and fold. Bet 200.

River decision with 8-T on 5h-Ac-2h-4s-9c board (9-high, no made hand, no outs). Minni checked flop and turn, then bet 200 into 850. To call profitably, I need approximately 19% equity (200/1050). My hand has essentially zero showdown value - 9-high beats only the rarest air holdings. Given Minni's check-check line followed by a river bet, she likely has at least a weak ace, pocket pair, or a hand like K-Q that made a pair. My previous plan on this hand was explicit: check all streets to control pot size and fold if bet on river. This is not a spot to make a thin value call or speculative bluff-catch with 9-high. Folding preserves chips for better opportunities and is consistent with sound exploitative play against Minni's tendency to have some showdown value when betting rivers after passive lines.

I have been dealt pocket kings on the button, which is the second-best starting hand in Texas Hold'em. My goal is to build a pot while also inducing action from my opponents in the blinds. A standard raise to 300 chips is consistent with my previous opening sizes throughout the game, which keeps my range balanced while extracting value from my monster hand.

72o (seven-deuce offsuit) is the worst starting hand in poker. Despite being in the small blind and only needing to call 250 more, this hand has virtually no playability. Flash's button raise is standard aggressive play, and even accounting for 1.8:1 pot odds, I would need 36% equity to break even - a threshold 72o falls well below. Folding is the clear mathematical and strategic choice. Preserving chips for better spots is crucial in a 150-hand cash game.

Facing a button open to 300 from Flash, I’ll defend my BB with 7♠8♥ by calling. It costs me 200 more into a 450 pot, so I need roughly 200/(450+200) ≈ 31% equity — 7-8 has acceptable equity vs a wide button opening and good postflop playability (straight/two-pair potential) with our deep stack and implied odds. I’m out of position, so I don’t want to 3-bet a marginal, offsuit connector (no strong blockers) and don’t want to fold a hand that plays well multi-street against a wide opener. I’ll call 200 more (300 total committed) to see a flop and proceed cautiously OOP.

Check. With 7♠8♥ on 6♠5♥Q♥ I have an open‑ended straight draw (4s or 9s = 8 outs, ~31–32% to hit by river) but no pair and I’m out of position vs the button opener. Standard, +EV play is to exercise pot control and realize equity rather than building a big pot OOP with a draw. Plan: check and call reasonable c‑bets (small to medium size — roughly up to ~2/3 pot, since my equity + implied odds justify calling); fold to an extreme overbet/river shove if ranges or sizing indicate I’m beat. If the opponent checks back I get to a free turn card and can continue on many favorable turns (4/9, a 7/8, or a safe card to lead).

I hold pocket kings on a queen-high board, which is a very strong position. The board has a flush draw (hearts) and some straight potential, but I have the overpair. Since Minni checked, I will place a continuation bet for value, hoping to get called by a queen or a draw while also protecting my hand against potential overcards or completed draws on the turn.

I have 7♠8♥ on 6♠-5♥-Q♥ — an open‑ended straight draw to 4/9 (8 clean outs, ~31–32% to hit by the river). Pot is 1050 and Flash bet 400, so calling 400 would make the pot 1450; pot odds = 400/1450 ≈ 27.6%, which is worse than my ~32% equity. Villain opened on the button and is c‑betting a relatively small size, so his range is wide and I expect enough fold equity and implied odds if I hit. I’m out of position, so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal OOP semi‑bluff (min‑raising to 800 would force tougher decisions and reduce my ability to realize equity). Plan: call the 400 (300 preflop + 400 flop = 700 total committed), see the turn. If I hit a 4/9 (or pair a 7/8) I’ll look to extract value; if I miss and face heavy turn aggression I’ll fold.

Check. I’m out of position with 7♠8♥ on 6♠-5♥-Q♥-6♦ — I’ve got only an open‑ended straight draw (8 outs) but with one card to come my chance to improve is ~8/47 ≈ 17%. The pot is already 1,450 and leading or barreling OOP here would bloat the pot with a longshot draw and reduce my ability to realize equity. Flash has been opening/CBing wide, so checking keeps the pot manageable, lets him bluff some rivers, and allows me to call small turn bets and fold to large overbets. Plan: check, call reasonable sizing on the river to realize equity when appropriate, and if I hit a 4/9 on the river look to extract value; fold to big river jams when I miss.

I have an overpair with pocket kings. The turn card is a six, pairing the board. While this could potentially have improved Minni's range if she defended with a six, it's more likely she has a queen or a drawing hand. By betting now, I continue to extract value from her queens (AQ, QJ, QT) and force her to pay to see the river if she is on a heart draw or a straight draw. I will bet around 60% of the pot to maintain pressure.

Fold. On the turn I only have an OESD (8 outs: any 4 or 9) with ~17% to hit the straight by the river. Flash’s turn bet is 900 into a 2350 pot, so a call requires ~27.7% equity (900/(2350+900)), which is significantly more than my ~17% equity. I’m out of position, there’s no backdoor flush, and raising OOP as a pure semi‑bluff with just this draw is too risky. Earlier calls (preflop/flop) were defensible; this turn shove/raise by villain forces a fold — preserve the stack and wait for a better spot.

8-6 off-suit is too weak to justify calling 100 chips even from button position. No premium qualities, poor playability post-flop, and adequate stack allows for waiting on better opportunities. Tight, disciplined play is more profitable than marginal spots.

Heads-up to the big blind with Q♣7♥ from the small blind. In heads-up play I should open a wide range — limp-fold is too passive and gives the BB cheap equity. Raising to the standard 2x BB (200 total) takes the initiative, creates fold equity, and denies the BB correct odds to see a free flop. If called I’ll continuation-bet appropriate flops and play straightforwardly postflop; if faced with a 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate given stack depth and opponent tendencies. Raise to 200 to seize control of the hand.

I am in the big blind with 9s-4h, a very weak hand. Minni has raised to 200 from the small blind. While I only need to call 100 more to see a flop, 94 offsuit does not play well post-flop, especially out of position against a player who has shown aggressive tendencies. Given the hand is essentially bottom-of-the-range, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack.

Button vs SB and BB, 3-handed, deep stacks (≈204bb). JTo offsuit is well inside a standard button open range — good opportunity to steal the blinds and play postflop in position. I want a sizing that gives fold equity and makes callers pay to see flops, so I’ll open to 3x the big blind (300 total). Plan: if one opponent calls I’ll use position to c-bet favorable boards and control pot size; if both call I’ll tighten continuation frequency and proceed cautiously on coordinated/low boards. If I face a sizable 3-bet (big, polarizing sizing) I’ll fold JTo; I may defend/call small, non-committal 3-bets against a wide opponent to leverage position, but will avoid getting stack-invested with this hand. Raise to 300 balances stealing, postflop playability, and protection against cheap multiway flops.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Four offsuit. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300. In a three-handed game, Ace-x is generally strong enough to defend, especially when out of position, but A4o is on the weaker side of the calling range. However, Minni has been very active with button raises. I will call to see a flop and evaluate, as folding seems too tight and raising might bloat the pot with a hand that doesn't play exceptionally well post-flop when called.

3-betting with AQ (premium hand) from BB position. Minni's button raise and Flash's passive call suggest neither has exceptional strength. A raise to 900 total (600 additional) applies pressure, builds the pot with my strong holding, and likely wins the hand outright given opponent tendencies shown in previous hands where Minni folds to 3-bets. This also takes control of the hand and gets value with one of the best non-pair holdings.

I opened to 300 on the button with J♣T♦ and Claude (BB) 3‑bet to 900 (pot 1500, I must call 600). JTo is marginal but well within a defend range on the button against a standard 3‑bet of this size: I get ~28.6% immediate pot odds and, importantly, I have position post‑flop. The 900 sizing is not so large that I should fold out of hand, and 4‑betting as a bluff with this hand is unnecessary — I don’t want to commit a lot of chips with a marginal holding. Plan: call the 600 (900 total) to see the flop, use position to apply pressure on favorable flops, keep the pot controlled on messy/multi‑way boards, and fold to heavy action or a big 4‑bet. If the SB folds and it’s heads‑up, I can play post‑flop aggressively; if the SB calls and it becomes multi‑way I’ll tighten my continuation frequency.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Four offsuit. The Button (Minni) opened, I called, and then the Big Blind (Claude) put in a 3-bet to 900. Minni has already called that 3-bet. I am now facing a 600-chip call to win a total pot of 2100. While the pot odds are mathematically decent (600 to win 2100), the situation is unfavorable. I am out of position against two players, one of whom (Claude) showed strength with a squeeze play, and the other (Minni) has deep stacks and is capable of calling 3-bets. Ace-Four offsuit is easily dominated by Claude's 3-betting range and doesn't play well in a multi-way 3-bet pot. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities where I have either initiative or position.

I'm betting a standard continuation bet on the flop with AQ, which has strong equity. The 7c 2c Jd board is relatively safe - I hold two overcards (A and Q beat everything except a set or two pair), and the weak 2c doesn't help Minni's likely range much. After 3-betting pre-flop from the BB, I need to follow through with aggression to maintain my image and likely win this pot. A bet of ~1/3 pot (700 into 2100) applies reasonable pressure while remaining balanced - it can fold out mid-strength hands like pocket pairs or weak Jacks, while also building value if Minni calls with worse holdings. Minni's tendency to call 3-bets rather than 4-bet suggests moderate strength, making this a good spot to leverage my premium holding and aggression from the button raise/3-bet dynamic.

I have top pair (Jacks) with a decent Ten kicker and the J♣ giving a tiny backdoor club route. Claude was the preflop 3‑bettor so his range contains strong hands (QQ+/AK/AQ/strong Kx) but also many missed broadways; his flop stab is very small (700 into 2800 ≈ 25%), which often constitutes a continuation probe rather than overwhelming strength. With position and a hand that fares well versus his missed portion of the range, I prefer pot control and information-gathering over bloating the pot by raising. Calling 700 is cheap, keeps worse hands and bluffs in his calling range, and lets me re‑evaluate on the turn (we can extract value on blanks or punish further weakness, fold to heavy aggression if he shows real strength). If he checks the turn I’ll consider a value bet; if he barrels large I’ll proceed cautiously given possible overpairs.

On the turn with AQ (two overs) against Minni's called range. The 9h is a relatively neutral card that doesn't significantly help either hand. I've established a strong aggressive narrative through my 3-bet pre-flop and c-bet on the flop, and Minni has shown moderate strength by calling both streets without aggressive resistance (no 4-bet pre-flop, no flop raise). A turn bet of 1500 into the 3500 pot maintains my aggression while:1) Applying pressure on mid-strength holdings like pocket pairs or weak Jacks that might fold, 2) Building value if Minni calls with hands I'm beating (like lower pairs), 3) Maintaining consistency with my aggro narrative. My hand equity is strong against most realistic holdings in Minni's range (any pair below JJ, draws, or weaker broadway combos). With 8775 remaining, I have room for river flexibility. This bet size (~40% of remaining stack) allows me to credibly follow through on the river depending on the card and Minni's action.

I have top pair (Jacks) with a reasonable Ten kicker and position. Claude is the preflop 3‑better so his range includes overpairs (QQ+/AK), some sets (77/22) and also many non‑pair or float hands — his flop stab was small and his turn sizing (1500 into ~3500) is a standard, non‑polar barrel. Raising here would bloat the pot and risk getting raised/shoved by the portion of his range that already beats me (QQ+/77/22); it would also fold out worse hands and bluffs I want to keep. Calling 1500 keeps the pot controlled, keeps his bluffs and weaker holdings in, and uses positional advantage to re‑evaluate on the river. I’m committing a small portion of my ~18.8k stack (≈8%) for a good decision spot on the river. Plan: call now, and on the river value bet if he checks or fold/shore up if he shows strong, polarized aggression.

Betting for value on the river after Minni checks. With AQ (ace-high), I'm likely ahead of Minni's calling range which probably consists of pocket pairs, weak Jacks, or missed broadway combinations. The river card (Ts) is neutral and doesn't significantly change the dynamics. Minni's check indicates either weakness or a hand wanting to control pot size. A bet of 2500 (approximately 1/3 of the 6500 pot) extracts value from weaker made hands while being reasonable sizing that Minni will call with lower pairs and marginal holdings. This leverages my aggressive narrative established through the 3-bet, c-bet, and turn aggression. Leaving 4775 behind provides flexibility for any river action while maximizing value on the river street.

I have two pair (Jacks and Tens) on the river — a very strong but not invulnerable hand. Claude was the preflop 3‑better and has shown consistent aggression (small flop stab, turn barrel, small river bet), so his line contains both value (overpairs/sets/trips/occasional two‑pair) and bluffs/missed broadways. The math is simple: calling 2500 into an ~9000 pot (~14k after my call) requires only ~18% equity. Given the small river sizing, many of his hands (AK/AQ/Kx, some bluffs, weaker Jx/Tx) will call or fold to a raise, and only a limited subset (QQ+/99/77/TT/22) clearly beat me. Raising would bloat the pot and polarize into the very combos that beat me (and could induce a shove), so the +EV play is to call, keep worse hands and bluffs in, and let position/vertically evaluate if he jams his remaining chips.

I am on the button with Q3 offsuit. While it is a 3-handed game, Q3o is generally too weak to open for a raise, especially given that Claude (SB) has shown significant aggression recently and Minni (BB) is a loose-defending big stack. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Heads-up small blind with A8o against Minni's big blind. Getting 3:1 pot odds to call. A8o has reasonable playability in this spot with flexibility to improve postflop. Calling preserves my stack while seeing the flop with a hand that plays well heads-up. This is a mathematically sound decision at excellent pot odds.

I have 9♣6♠ — a weak, offsuit hand — and there was no raise preflop (SB just called). Pot is only 200 and my to-call is 0. Raising to 200 would be thin and would bloat a pot OOP; with this holding I prefer to see a flop cheaply. I'll check, see the flop, and proceed based on texture: fold to heavy turn/river pressure if I miss, call small continuation bets with decent draws or a pair, and only invest further with clear equity (pair+ or strong draw).

Check on the flop with A8o (middle pair) in a heads-up pot against Minni. This preserves my short stack, gives me information about Minni's hand strength, and maintains positional advantage for post-flop streets where I act last. The board is coordinated (J-8-5cc) and my middle pair is vulnerable to many of Minni's big blind holdings, so controlling pot size is prudent.

Claude limped preflop and checked the flop. I hold 9♣6♠ on J♦ 8♣ 5♣ — no pair, only a gutshot to a 7 (4 outs, ~16.5% to hit by river) and a very slim backdoor two-card club flush. The pot is tiny (200) and I have position. Betting here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and face callers from a wide limping range (Jx/8x/5x/suited clubs) that dominate me. I’ll check and see a free turn, then only invest further if I improve (pair, straight, or a strong draw) or get a very weak check/raise line to exploit. If Claude bets the turn and I miss, I’ll fold to sizable pressure; I may call small bets with my equity but won’t lead out with this hand.

I'm holding A8 on a Jd-8c-5c-Kh board, giving me a pair of 8s with an Ace kicker. Minni checked the flop on a coordinated board, suggesting she likely doesn't have a strong made hand like KK or JJ. The King on the turn is a bit concerning (could give her a pair), but her flop check is telling. With a short stack of 4675, I need to build value with my likely best hand rather than give free cards. A bet of 150 (roughly 75% of pot) is sized appropriately to: (1) get value from weaker holdings that might call, (2) avoid committing too much with my limited stack, and (3) discourage her from calling a weak hand to hit the river. This maintains control of the hand and builds the pot when I'm likely ahead. If she raises, I have information; if she calls, I'll re-evaluate on the river with a larger pot.

Board: J♦ 8♣ 5♣ K♥. I hold 9♣6♠ — no pair, no clean draw. Claude limp‑called preflop, checked the flop and now makes a small turn stab to 150 into a 350 pot. Calling 150 to win 500 requires ≈30% equity. My realistic outs are 3 nines + 3 sixes + 4 sevens = 10 unique outs → ~22% raw equity, and many of those outs are not clean (if Claude has Kx or Jx my pair will still lose). The backdoor club draw is dead after the K♥ turn. Raising would be a thin bluff and unnecessary with such a weak holding. Given the pot odds and opponent’s line, my equity is below break‑even — fold and wait for a better spot.

Calling 100 from the button with Q8 suited. This is a standard call - good position, decent hand with two-way potential, minimal cost to see the flop, and I can apply positional pressure post-flop. Q8 is above the threshold for button defense against blind positions.

I’m in the small blind with J♣5♣. Button limped and the big blind is still to act; the pot is 250 and I only need to put 50 more to complete to 100. J5s is marginal but has some playability (backdoor flush/straight potential). Raising into two players out of position is suboptimal here — a min-raise to 200 would commit me to tough postflop decisions and risk a 3-bet from the BB. Calling is cheap (50 into 250 gives great pot odds) and lets me see a flop with a playable hand while keeping pot control. Plan to proceed cautiously postflop: barrell/c-bet with good equity or fold to significant aggression when missed.

I have Ace-Four offsuit in the Big Blind. Both the button (Claude) and the small blind (Minni) have limped in. While A4o has some strength, it is not strong enough to justify a raise against two opponents from out of position, especially since I can see the flop for free. Checking allows me to realize my equity and see a flop without bloating the pot with a mediocre starting hand.

I check to control the pot. I have middle pair (5s) with a J kicker on K♠ Q♦ 5♦ — decent but vulnerable with two overcards and two diamonds on board and two opponents left to act. Leading out into two opponents has low fold equity and will often be called by K/Q or diamond draws, putting me in tough OOP decisions. Checking keeps the pot small, lets me gather information, and allows me to call a modest bet but fold to large aggression. Plan: if both check I'll see a cheap turn; if I face a small c-bet (~<=1/3–1/2 pot) I'll call for my pair + backdoor club equity; if I face a large bet or raise I'll fold unless I improve.

I have Ace-high on a board with two broadway cards (K and Q). Since the pot was limped pre-flop and the SB checked, I will check as well to see a free turn card. My hand has very little showdown value right now and doesn't warrant a bet against two opponents.

I have top pair (Queen) with an eight kicker on a King-high board. Both opponents checked to me, indicating weak holdings. With position on the button and a relatively dry board (Ks 5d Qd), this is a good spot to take the initiative. A value bet of 200 (roughly 2/3 pot) accomplishes several things: (1) builds the pot when I have a solid hand, (2) allows me to gauge opponent strength and extract value from weaker holdings, (3) maintains aggressive button play, and (4) leaves me in control of the hand. My stack of 4775 is sufficient to apply pressure without overcommitting. The flush draw is still live as a backup equity source, and my Q8s has good playability. This is standard value betting with top pair.

I have middle pair (5♣) with a J kicker on K♠ Q♦ 5♦. Claude’s bet to 200 into a 500 pot (~40% pot) is a standard c‑bet and gives me good pot odds to see a turn. My hand has showdown value plus backdoor club equity, but is vulnerable to K/Q and diamond draws. Raising OOP here would be thin and needlessly inflate the pot; folding is too weak given the odds. I’ll call to control the pot, gather info, and re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to large raise or continued heavy aggression if I don’t improve; if I hit a 5, J, or a club I can be more aggressive.

On a board of Ks-5d-Qd, I have Ace-high with no significant draws. The pot was limped pre-flop, but Claude has now bet 200 and Minni has called. Given that there are two broadway cards on the board, it is highly likely that at least one of my opponents has a piece of the board (a pair of Kings, Queens, or even Fives). My Ace-high has very little showdown value here and calling would be a waste of chips since I am likely behind and have few ways to improve to the best hand.

Check. I have a marginal one-pair hand (5♣ with J kicker) on K♠ Q♦ 5♦ 2♠. Claude’s line — flop c‑bet (200) then checking the turn — looks like weakness or pot control with K/Qx or a missed diamond/air combination. I have only ~5 clean outs (3 J’s + 2 fives) to improve (~11% on the river), so betting for thin value is negative EV: worse hands that would call (K/Q) already beat me, and a small bet won’t fold many value hands. Checking keeps the pot contained (pot is 700), lets me see the river cheaply, and preserves fold equity if he leads out. If he bets the river and I don’t improve I’ll fold to large bets and consider calling small ones with showdown value.

Top pair (Queen-8) on a dry Ks 5d Qd 2s board. Minni checked the turn after calling my flop bet, indicating weakness. Position, hand strength, and board texture all favor a value bet. Sizing of 350 (roughly half pot) maintains aggression while keeping Minni in the hand to extract value from weaker holdings or underpairs. Standard turn play with top pair and positional control.

Call 350. I have a marginal but real one-pair (5♣) with a J kicker. Claude’s line (limp/c-bet on the flop, smallish turn bet) is wide — he can have K/Qx, some weak broadways, small pairs or bluffs. His 350 into a 1050 pot is a small sizing, so my call is offered ~25% pot odds. Even though I only have ~5 clean outs to improve (~11% to hit on the river), my pair has showdown value against a lot of his missed/weak hands and the price is correct to see the river. Raising is thin and would inflate a pot I’m likely behind in; folding is too weak given the odds. Plan: call to see the river, fold to a large river shove if I don’t improve, call small river bets for showdown, and if I hit a J or 5 consider value-betting/raising.

I have one pair (5♣) with a J kicker on Ks Qd 5d 2s 6s — a real but marginal hand that is dominated by obvious parts of Claude’s range (Kx, Qx) and crushed by any completed spade flush (there are three spades on board). Claude limped pre, c‑bet the flop and bet the turn; that line looks value‑heavy but fairly wide, so I can expect a mix of top pairs, some small pairs and occasional missed draws/bluffs. Betting for thin value here is negative EV because most hands that call a bet beat me, and a bluff is unlikely to fold enough better hands to justify bloating the pot. I’ll check to preserve stack and go to showdown; if Claude fires a small river bet I can call for pot odds, but I’ll fold to large river aggression since my hand is easily dominated.

River value bet with top pair (Queen-8). Minni's checking pattern throughout the hand (flop, turn, river) indicates weakness. He likely holds AQ, QJ, or a medium pair—hands that will call a reasonable river bet but likely fold to larger aggression. My hand is most likely the best given the action. A 700 chip bet (50% of pot) is appropriate sizing to extract value while maintaining fold equity. With a large enough stack remaining (3525) and positional advantage, this is standard river betting strategy with a strong but not dominant hand.

I have J♣5♣ — a real, but vulnerable, one-pair hand (pair of 5s) on Ks Qd 5d 2s 6s. Claude limped/c‑bet and barreled the turn and river; his line is wide and includes both value (small pairs, two-pair/trips, completed spade flushes, some K/Qx) and missed diamond draws that can be turned into bluffs. He bet 700 into the pot; calling 700 to win a 2,800 pot (2100 current + my 700) requires ~25% equity. My pair beats a lot of missed draws and smaller pairs and the price is correct to see showdown. Raising is thin (I have poor fold equity and would get called by better), and folding surrenders too much given the pot odds and his wide limp/c‑bet range. I’ll call the 700 and take it to showdown, folding only to a larger shove if it appears.

I’m on the button with 6c6s and deep stacks (≈249bb). Pot is 150 and a cold call for 100 is passive and likely to let both blinds see a cheap flop. I want to take the initiative, isolate the blinds and retain fold equity while keeping strong implied odds for set-mining. A standard 3x open (to 300) is appropriate — bigger than the min-raise (200) so I don’t give cheap odds to two defenders and it’s consistent with table sizing. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (often folding to a very big 3-bet, calling or occasionally 4-betting vs small aggression depending on opponent and sizing).

In the Small Blind with J4s, I'm facing a 3x open (300) from Minni on the button. While J4s is a suited hand with some playability, it is quite weak to call out of position, especially against a button open that often contains stronger high cards or pocket pairs. Additionally, Claude in the Big Blind has a relatively short stack (around 62bbs) and could potentially squeeze. Given the stack depths and the fact that I'm out of position for the rest of the hand, folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to avoid being trapped in a marginal spot.

Minni has raised to 300 from the button and Flash folded. I'm in the Big Blind with 9-10 offsuit and need to call 200 more.

Analysis:
- Hand strength: 9-10o is a marginal but playable hand with decent equity against a button raise range
- Pot odds: 450:200 = 2.25:1, which requires ~31% equity. My 9-10o likely has 40-45% equity against Minni's button raise range
- Stack consideration: My 6225 stack allows me to call without significant overcommitment
- Position: While I'm out of position postflop, heads-up with decent equity and pot odds favors a call
- Minni's tendencies: She's a frequent raiser, so her range is likely wide enough that my hand has good equity

This is a standard call with a medium-strength hand that has sufficient equity and favorable pot odds in a heads-up situation.

Checking with 9-10o on Qc 3d 8s flop. While I have drawing potential (straights, overcards, pair outs) with approximately 30-35% equity, I'm out of position which favors keeping the pot small. My hand needs to develop on future streets. Betting here with such a weak current hand is too aggressive out of position. Checking allows me to realize my equity efficiently and get to the turn at minimal cost while controlling the pot size.

I opened to 300 preflop and Claude flat-called. Flop is Qc 3d 8s (rainbow). I hold 6c6s — a small pocket pair with decent showdown value vs Claude’s calling range but vulnerable to overcards. Claude checked, which often indicates a wide, non-committed range here. Standard heads-up strategy is to c-bet a high frequency on these dry flops to fold out missed overcards and pick up value from worse pairs (44/55/22) and occasional floats. I’ll make a modest c-bet (~1/3–2/5 pot) to retain fold equity without bloating the pot too much in case of a check-raise. If he calls I’ll control the pot on the turn and re-evaluate; if he shows strong aggression (large raise) I will usually fold since my hand has poor equity vs top pair/sets.

Calling 250 on the flop with 9-10 offsuit. I have approximately 30-35% equity against Minni's button raise range with multiple draws (8 straight outs, overcards, pair outs). Pot odds of 3.6:1 require only ~22% equity, making this a mathematically sound call. Despite being out of position, the favorable odds and drawing potential justify continuing to the turn.

Betting with a pair of Nines on the turn. The 9d improved my hand from a drawing situation to a made pair with significant equity. I'm likely ahead of Minni's button raise range which typically includes draws and weaker pairs. Betting 500 applies appropriate pressure, gets value from worse hands, and tests her hand strength. This is a standard value bet that maintains my equity lead and forces her to make a river decision. My straight draw outs provide additional equity if called.

Claude check-called my modest c-bet on the Qc 3d 8s flop and now leads out for 500 on the 9d turn. That line (check-call then a turn lead after a blank to my range) heavily favors hands that beat my small pocket pair (Qx, 9x, 8x or sets) and the 9 on the turn specifically improves lots of his plausible holdings. Pot = 1650; calling 500 requires ≈23% equity (500/(1650+500) ≈ 23.3%), while I only have ~3 outs to hit a set (~6.5%), so a call is a clear negative EV. Raising to 1000 as a bluff is too thin — he called the flop and is likely to continue with value or shove with strong holdings, so I’d be committing chips with very poor equity. With deep stacks I’ll preserve my deep-stack equity and wait for a better spot rather than chase a low-probability river. Fold.

I am on the button with A5s, which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. My stack is 87.5 big blinds. Claude is the small blind and Minni is the big blind. I want to enter the pot with a raise to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the hand. A standard raise to 300 (3x) is appropriate here to thin the field or win the blinds immediately. If I get re-raised, I can evaluate based on sizing and player tendencies, but A5s is a great candidate for either calling or 4-betting as a bluff due to its ace blocker and playability.

9-3 offsuit (9s-3d) is one of the worst hands in poker - unconnected, unsuited, and both cards are low. Flash raised to 300 from the button, which could be a wide range given their late position aggression seen in the hand history, but my hand doesn't warrant playing. I have approximately 25% equity at best against a typical late position raising range, falling short of the roughly 35% needed to break even on the 250 call at current pot odds. With a reasonable stack of 6,875 (68.75 big blinds), there's no pressing need to gamble here. I should fold and preserve chips for premium hands where I have a clearer advantage. Minni also still has to act from the BB, which could lead to additional action.

Facing a standard 3bb open from the button, 2♦4♥ is one of the absolute worst hands to defend from the big blind. I’m out of position, have almost no fold equity post‑flop, and the hand has very poor playability and reverse implied odds if I pair. The pot odds to call 200 into a 650 pot require ~30.8% equity; versus a reasonable button opening range my actual equity is below that threshold. A preflop raise here would be a pure, high‑variance bluff with little strategic benefit. I’ll fold and wait for a better spot to invest chips.

J♥6♥ is too weak a hand to commit chips with from the button when I'm the shortest stack. While position is favorable, this hand has poor post-flop playability and limited equity. With 67 hands remaining and my stack concerns, I should wait for stronger holdings like pairs, broadway cards, or better suited connectors. Folding preserves chips for premium spots while minimizing variance in an unfavorable chip position.

Heads-up from the small blind with pocket 6s: this is a clear raising spot. 66 plays well heads-up (good equity vs a wide BB calling range and strong implied odds for set-mining). I want to take the initiative and charge the big blind, so I’ll raise to a standard 3x BB (300 total). This sizing isolates the BB, builds value when ahead, and still allows me to call a reasonable 3-bet to see a flop for sets; I’ll proceed with standard c-bet lines on favorable flops and be prepared to fold to very large 4-bets/all-ins that offer poor pot odds.

I am in the Big Blind with 3d 2c, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Although I only need to call 200 more into a pot of 400, this hand is extremely difficult to play post-flop, especially out of position or even in position against a wide range. Given my current stack size and the poor quality of my hand, folding is the standard and most profitable play long-term to avoid losing more chips in unfavorable situations.

Button with TT (Td Tc) — a clear open-raise for value and initiative. With 3-handed action and deep stacks, I want to build the pot and play in position, while pricing out some speculative hands. A standard 3x BB to 300 accomplishes that: strong enough to get value/isolate the blinds but not so large that it folds out all worse hands I can outplay postflop. If I face a single 3-bet I’ll largely call and play postflop; I’ll re-evaluate versus a large shove or a coordinated 3-bet from both players.

I am in the Small Blind with Q3o. The Button (Minni) has opened to 3bb. Q3o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against an aggressive opponent like Minni. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

I'm in the big blind with 8s-9d against Minni's button raise to 300. Flash folded from the small blind. Analysis: (1) Pot odds are favorable at 450:200 (2.25:1), well above the 2:1 needed for a marginal hand. (2) 8-9 offsuit has reasonable equity and multiple ways to improve - any 6, 7, or 10 makes a straight, plus two overcards if needed. (3) Minni has shown a wide raising range from the button throughout these hands, so my hand has value against that range. (4) My stack of 6775 is healthy enough to play this hand in position post-flop. (5) This is a standard profitable spot where pot odds justify the call despite 8-9 not being premium. I'll call and see the flop, where my hand has significant potential to improve.

Checking with 8-9 offsuit on a 5-5-2 flop. I have an open-ended straight draw (any 6 or 7 completes the straight), which gives me roughly 8+ outs and reasonable equity. As the first to act, checking allows me to control pot size, see Minni's action, and make a better decision post-flop. The paired board is relatively dry and unlikely to have hit her range hard, so I can keep the pot small while I have a draw with good implied odds from my 6575 stack.

Flop is 5c-5s-2s and I hold TT — a clean overpair and the best hand vs most of Claude’s calling range out of the big blind. Claude checked, which is consistent with a wide defending range (Ax/Kx, small-mid pairs, suited connectors, occasional pocket pairs like 22/55). This board is fairly dry but there are two spades, so I want to charge any spade draws and get value from worse pairs and one-pair hands. In position I should be betting for value rather than giving a free card. A modest/medium-size bet keeps worse hands and draws paying without overcommitting: I’ll bet ~1/3–1/2 pot to extract value and deny equity. If Claude calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he raises big I’ll put him on a very strong hand (set/boat) and fold to an overcommitment, while I’ll consider calling small raises to keep his weaker hands and bluffs in. Betting 300 into 650 balances value and protection.

Calling with 8-9 offsuit on 5-5-2. I have an open-ended straight draw with 8 outs and excellent pot odds of 4.17:1 against a required break-even threshold of 20% equity. With ~32% equity across two cards and strong implied odds if I hit, this is a mathematically sound call. The draw has multiple ways to win (any 6 or 7 makes a strong straight), and my stack is healthy enough to play this spot profitably.

Checking with pair of eights plus open-ended straight draw (8 outs to a made straight). The turn 8h transformed my hand from just a draw to a strong made hand that likely leads Minni's range, especially after her turn check showed weakness. Keeping the pot small while I'm ahead or tied at worst is the optimal strategy. I can either win at showdown if she checks the river, or call her bet with strong pot odds given my made hand plus strong outs. My 6275 stack can afford to see the river and make the best decision there.

I have an overpair (TT) on a relatively dry board 5c-5s-2s-8h. Claude called my small flop bet, so his range is wide (weaker pairs like 66–99, Kx/Ax, occasional 22/55 sets, and the rare two‑spade backdoor combos). The turn 8h doesn’t change my relative strength except that pocket 88 would now have trips (and could check to induce). Spade draws are uncommon here (need two spades in hand). I want to extract value from worse pairs and charge any two‑spade combos while keeping the pot controlled vs sets, so I’ll bet a medium size (~1/2 pot). This size keeps Kx/Ax and small pairs calling but limits my downside if Claude check‑raises big (a large check‑raise will strongly indicate a set or trips and I’ll fold/re‑evaluate). If he just calls I’ll re‑evaluate river for another value bet or pot control depending on the card.

Calling with pair of eights and open-ended straight draw on 5-5-2-8 board. Pot odds of 3.08:1 are favorable against my estimated 40%+ equity from both the made hand and 8 straight outs. Minni's turn bet shows commitment but doesn't necessarily indicate a strong holding—her button range is wide and turn bet could be semi-bluff or value. My stack (6275) comfortably supports seeing the river, and if I hit the straight, I'll have a premium hand with good payoff potential. This is a standard profitable call in position postflop.

Checking with three of a kind on the river after Minni checked. The final board of 5-5-2-8-5 gives me a strong hand (5-5-5-9-8). Minni's river check indicates weakness—the third 5 is a card that doesn't help her likely range of overpairs, underpairs, or missed draws. With strong showdown value and nothing left to gain by betting into her check (worse hands fold, better hands call anyway), checking and going to showdown is the optimal line. My three of a kind wins against most of her range and there's no reason to risk additional chips or give up information by betting.

Turn and flop lines were standard — I had an overpair (TT) and bet to get value/protect. The river 5h pairs the board (5c-5s-2s-8h-5h) and turns the board into trips, so my tens are now effectively a bluff vs almost any hand that called both streets (any 5, a turned 8, pocket 2s or other pocket pairs that now make a full house). Claude’s passive calling line (call flop, call turn, check river) makes it likely he either has a showdown-value hand that will call a bluff or is trapping with a strong made hand; either way a river bet is a thin bluff with low fold equity and high downside given his remaining stack. I’ll check and take the free showdown rather than risking extra chips on a low‑EV bluff. If Claude then bets, I’ll re-evaluate and likely fold to a large bet/raise.

I'm on the button with K3o. While it's a weak King, the action is folded to me. I have the opportunity to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. In a 3-handed game, K3o is strong enough for an open-raise from the button to put pressure on the blinds. Standard sizing is 3x the big blind.

Folding 4-5 offsuit from the small blind. This is one of the weakest hands in poker and has minimal equity against Flash's raising range from the button. Even though I've already committed the small blind, calling an additional 250 chips with this hand is not profitable. I have a weak hand, no position, and two opponents still in the pot. Better opportunities will come with stronger holdings. Preserving chips for premium situations is crucial for long-term profitability in this cash game.

I’m in the BB with A♣2♣ facing a standard BTN open to 300. Stacks are deep (~85bb effective), so A2s has excellent playability (nut-flush potential, good blockers to other A-high hands) and is worth defending. Flatting is reasonable but leaves me OOP and concedes initiative; a sized 3-bet isolates a wide button range, gains fold equity, and balances my defending range. I’ll 3-bet to ~900 (≈3x the open) — big enough to apply pressure and avoid giving a free cheap flop, but not so large as to commit against a big 4-bet. Plan: if called I’ll play aggressively on favorable textures (c-bet when appropriate, realize nut-flush equity), but fold to a large 4-bet (A2s is often dominated vs a polarized 4-bet).

I originally opened the pot for 300 with Kc3s from the button. Minni has 3-bet from the big blind to 900. K3o is a very weak hand to continue with against a 3-bet, even in a short-handed game. I am out of position relative to Minni (who is in the Big Blind but has the betting lead) for the rest of the hand if I call, and my hand has poor playability and is likely dominated. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to avoid losing more chips with a marginal hand.

Position and odds analysis: I'm on the button with T8o (Ten-Eight offsuit), which is a marginally playable hand. The pot is 150 with only 100 to call, giving me 2.75:1 odds. Against two opponents (Minni with a large 25k stack and Flash with 8.3k), I have excellent positional advantage as the button. T8o has reasonable playability with two broadway cards and wheel potential. The cheap call from the button is standard poker - I'll be in position postflop with late action, which gives me maximum information and flexibility.
