This is not a strong enough hand to raise with, given the deep stacks, but definitely worth seeing a flop cheaply from position.

Three-handed, button limped (Claude) and I’m in the small blind with K♣6♠. K6o is marginal but in a 3‑handed game I can raise wider to isolate the limper and take the initiative. Flash (BB) is capable of defending/3‑betting, so I’ll keep the sizing standard and credible — raise to 300 total (≈3BB). That gives good fold equity versus limp-calling ranges, keeps the pot manageable if called, and is a size I can comfortably fold to a large 3‑bet with this hand. If I get called and see a flop, I’ll proceed cautiously out of position: c‑bet favorable texture (A/K/T high boards) and check/fold or check‑call with backdoor equity on dry boards.

I am in the big blind with a weak hand, 5c 3h. Claude limped from the button, and Minni raised to 300 from the small blind. The pot odds are 2:1 to call (200 to win 500), but 53o is a very poor hand that realizes equity poorly, especially against a raise from the small blind which often indicates a strong range or an aggressive attempt to isolate. Given the stack depths and my position, folding is the most prudent play to conserve chips.

Minni's raise to 300 from small blind shows strength. While T8o has marginal playability from position with cheap odds preflop, the re-raise changes the equity calculation significantly. With a short stack of 5,525 in 50/100 NL, I must be selective and avoid marginal spots. T8o lacks sufficient equity against Minni's raising range, which likely includes pairs, strong broadway hands, and broadway combinations. The 100 already committed is a sunk cost; the decision is purely about the additional 200. Folding preserves chips for stronger situations.

Button with T9o is a clear open: connected, good playability and equity post-flop so I want the initiative and to steal the blinds. A standard 3x BB sizing (300 total) gives solid fold equity vs the SB/BB, avoids the free multiway pot that a limp invites, and matches table norms (300 is commonly used here). If I face a small 3-bet I can continue or call for position; a large 3-bet will force a fold — otherwise if called I’ll C-bet favorable flops and proceed aggressively on connected/ten-high boards.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack of Diamonds and Nine of Clubs. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. This is a standard button raise. J9o is a marginal hand to defend in the Small Blind, especially against a large stack like Minni. While it has some playability, I am out of position for the rest of the hand and Claude is still to act in the Big Blind. Given the current stack dynamics and the strength of J9o against a button range, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I'm in the big blind with 5h 3c (53o), which is one of the weakest possible holdings in poker. While I'm getting approximately 2.25:1 pot odds (450 to call 200), this is not sufficient to justify playing such a weak hand out of position against Minni's button raise. 

53o has minimal equity and very limited ways to improve post-flop. Minni has been consistently aggressive from the button throughout this session, and playing marginal hands out of position will lead to long-term losses. With a reasonable stack size (5425 chips) and 63 hands remaining, I should preserve my chips and wait for premium hands where I have a clear advantage.

This is a standard fold with weak holdings from the blind against aggressive openers.

I am on the button with Qh7h. This is a suited queen in a late position. While not a premium hand, it is strong enough to open or call in a three-handed game to put pressure on the blinds or see a flop in position. Given the stack sizes and current pot dynamics, a standard raise to 300 is a good way to take control of the hand.

Calling Flash's button raise with Ad6h from small blind. This is a borderline but reasonable hand with ace-high potential and decent showdown value. At 54BB deep, I can afford to see a flop and make post-flop decisions based on the board texture and opponent action. This avoids over-folding while not over-committing with a marginal hand.

Flash (button) raised to 300 and Claude (SB) called. I have 8♣5♣ in the big blind — a suited, speculative hand. Calling the extra 200 (total commitment 300) gives me decent pot odds (200 into ~700 → need ~22% equity) and large implied odds with both opponents deep (my stack 26,175; opponents 8,000 and 5,125). 3-betting is thin OOP against two players and would bloat the pot with a weak holding; folding is too tight given the hand’s playability. I’ll call to see a flop and proceed cautiously: fold to heavy, coordinated action on dry boards; continue/raise when I hit pairs, strong draws, or nut-like hands; use implied odds and position postflop to extract value or bluff selectively.

Checking with Ad6h on a dry 4c Tc Jh board. I have just ace-high with a weak kicker, which is vulnerable in a three-way pot. The Ten and Jack likely connect better with my opponents' ranges (Flash's button raise and Minni's big blind). Checking preserves chips and allows me to react to opponent action. If either opponent bets, I can make an informed fold decision without further commitment. If it checks through, I'll have position on the turn to make better decisions with more information.

Check. I have 8♣5♣ on a 4♣ T♣ J♥ flop — no pair but a strong four-card club draw (9 outs) with ~19% to hit the turn and ~35% to hit by the river. Claude’s check shows weakness and Flash was the preflop aggressor (likely to c‑bet). I’m out of position to Flash, stacks are deep, and I don’t want to bloat the pot with a speculative hand. Pot‑control and allowing the button to act first is the safest, most +EV line: if Flash makes a small c‑bet I’ll call to realise my draw equity; if he checks back I’ll take a free turn to continue or bluff later. I’ll only consider a check‑raise as a semi‑bluff if the sizing and reads strongly favour fold equity versus a single bettor.

I raised pre-flop and got two callers. The flop of 4c-Tc-Jh is quite coordinated and likely connects with the ranges that would call a button raise from the blinds. I have Queen-high with limited immediate value but some backdoor flush and straight potential. In a three-way pot on a wet board, checking back is a prudent play to control the pot size and realize equity for free, as a continuation bet is frequently called by hands like Jacks, Tens, or flush draws.

Checking with Ad6h on the turn of a 4c Tc Jh 3d board in a three-way pot. My hand is just ace-high with a weak kicker, which is vulnerable to the likely holdings of my opponents who likely connect better with this coordinated board. Everyone checked the flop, suggesting either genuine weakness or trap situations. Checking allows me to see the river cheaply while keeping the pot small with a marginal hand. If either opponent bets, I have a clear fold. If it checks through, I'll get pot odds to evaluate the river.

I’ll semi‑bluff the turn with a small bet. I have 8♣5♣ — a four‑card club flush draw (9 outs, ~19–20% to hit the river). Both opponents checked the flop and checked the turn (3♦), showing weakness; that gives me fold equity if I take the initiative now. The pot is 900, so I’ll bet 300 (~1/3 pot). That sizing balances fold equity (can steal the pot now) and keeps the investment modest against two opponents. If I get called I still have decent implied odds to hit the flush on the river; if I face a large raise I’ll fold (I’m only a draw), and if called I’ll re‑evaluate the river card for a value call or a bluff. Betting 300 as a semi‑bluff maximizes my chance to win the static pot and builds a pot I can win big when the draw completes while controlling downside against aggression.

On the turn 3d, the board is 4c-Tc-Jh-3d. Minni bets 300 into a 900 pot (offering 4:1 pot odds). My hand, Qh7h, has no direct value and very little equity (only two overcards which might not even be clean outs). In a three-way pot, this bet usually indicates at least some piece of the board or a draw that Claude and I both checked on the flop. Given my lack of a pair or a significant draw, calling is not profitable, and raising into two opponents on this texture is too risky. I will fold.

Folding Ad6h on the turn. I have just ace-high with a poor kicker against Minni's turn raise. The board (4c Tc Jh 3d) is coordinated and offers many made hand possibilities for my opponents' ranges. Minni's turn raise after checking the flop suggests concrete strength rather than a float or draw. With no draw to accompany my weak holding, no real showdown value, and poor position, this is a clear fold. Calling 300 more would be a poor use of chips when my hand has minimal equity. My stack allows me to be selective, and this isn't a profitable spot to defend.

Folding Q5o from the button. While position is valuable, this hand is too weak - it has minimal equity and plays poorly postflop. Even though I want to defend my button range, Q5o is not the hand to do it with. The card ranks are too weak and without additional equity (like a flush draw potential or better kicker), this is a clear fold. Preserving chips with better hands is the priority.

2♦3♣ offsuit is one of the weakest holdings — very poor playability and frequently dominated. I’m out of position against the big blind, so calling 50 to see a flop is a marginal, negative-EV line and raising to 200 would be an unnecessary bluff with almost no postflop potential. I’d rather preserve a large stack deep into the session and avoid difficult OOP decisions for a tiny pot. Fold and move to a better spot.

Button with 2♣6♦ — a very weak offsuit hand. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see the flop (pot odds ≈ 28.6%), but versus two opponents my equity with 2-6o is well below that and I’ll be outdrawn or dominated a lot. I could min-raise to 200 to attempt a steal, but both SB (Flash) and BB (Claude) have shown willingness to defend and raise; a min-raise will often be called or 3‑bet and put me in difficult postflop spots with a trash hand. With a deep stack and plenty of hands left in the session, preserving chips and folding this garbage is the right long-term play.

I am in the Small Blind with T3o. Minni has folded on the button. It is now just me and Claude (Big Blind). Claude has a relatively short stack compared to the table leader (Minni), though still has 50bbs. T3o is a very weak hand. While I could attempt to steal the blind, Claude has shown some aggression and resistance in previous hands. Folding is the most standard and disciplined play here to avoid playing a weak hand out of position post-flop.

I am on the button with 9h 6s. This is a very weak hand in a 3-handed game. While playing aggressively from the button is generally good, I have noticed that Minni is a very active player with a massive stack, and Claude has been willing to call or raise pre-flop quite often. With a 9-high trash hand, I don't see enough value in entering the pot, especially with a to-call amount of 100 on my own blind (Wait, I am the Button/Dealer, so my 'to call' is 0 unless someone raised). Looking at the log, the current hand #91 has just started and I am the first to act as the dealer/button in a 3-way game (Claude is SB, Minni is BB). The blinds are 50/100. I should simply fold this hand and wait for a better spot.

Folding Q7 offsuit from small blind. This is a weak hand with poor equity, and I'm facing a significant chip disadvantage (5,125 vs 26,725). While I only need to call 50 chips, playing marginal hands out of position against a stronger opponent would drain my stack. I need to preserve chips and play tighter, waiting for premium hands to rebuild.

J-2o is too weak to play even from the button. Despite favorable position and low cost to call (only 100 chips), the hand has poor equity and is easily dominated. At 50 BB deep, I should preserve chips for stronger holdings. Folding is the disciplined play.

Button folded so I'm heads-up with the big blind. A♦8♠ is a strong heads-up hand (Ace blocker, good high-card, decent playability). I want initiative and fold equity rather than limp/call. I'll open to a standard 3x BB sizing (300 total) — large enough to fold out weaker hands and get value/calls from worse Ax and broadway holdings, while not overcommitting. If Flash 3-bets big I'll fold; I may call smaller 3-bets depending on size and implied odds. If called, plan to c-bet favorable flops and extract value when I pair my Ace or realize equity with backdoor draws.

In the Big Blind with Jack-Four of diamonds, I am facing a raise from Minni in the Small Blind. Jack-Four suited is a marginal hand, but getting 3-to-1 odds (200 to win 600) makes it a viable call in a head-up situation against the Small Blind, especially since we have position post-flop. Minni has been active and aggressive, often raising from the SB. Calling allows me to see a flop and potentially realize equity with my suit or by hitting a Jack.

I’m on the button (in position) with Ad8s after raising preflop. Flop 5d 5c 7s is dry but paired — I have no pair or meaningful draw, but my preflop raising range and the Ace blocker give me good fold equity against a wide calling range. A continuation bet is the right play to fold out missed broadways and weaker hands and to keep the initiative. Pot = 600, so a ~1/3-pot bet of 200 keeps pressure while limiting loss vs sets; break-even call frequency is only 25% (200/(600+200)), which the opponent folds to often from a wide range. Plan: if I get a large raise I’ll give it up (little equity); if called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and re-evaluate based on the card and opponent’s line.

Minni's bet of 200 into a 600 pot on a paired board of 5-5-7 is a standard continuation bet. My hand, Jack-Four of diamonds, has two overcards and a backdoor flush draw. Given the 3-to-1 pot odds and my position, I will call once to see the turn. If Minni continues to show strength on the turn without me improving, I will likely fold, but her small bet sizing allows for a floating play here.

Check. I opened preflop with Ad8s and c‑bet the dry 5♦5♣7♠ flop (200 into ~600) and Flash called, so the pot is now ~1,000. The K♦ on the turn is a textbook “bad” card for my bluffing line: it significantly helps a caller’s continuing range (Kx and many pocket pairs) and makes it easy for him to have a turned value hand. I have only Ace‑high (the Ace is a minor blocker but doesn’t materially improve my equity), so betting here would often be a thin bluff that can be easily punished by a raise or a call from better hands. Checking controls the pot, keeps bloated pots out of my stack versus likely better hands, and lets me re‑evaluate on the river where I can either fold to aggression, call a small probe, or bluff/realize showdown depending on the river texture and villain’s line.

On the turn card King of Diamonds (Kd), the board is 5d 5c 7s Kd. I have a flush draw with my Jack and Four of diamonds. Minni, who raised pre-flop and bet the flop, has checked. This check could signal a marginal hand or a surrender. While I could bet to try and take the pot now, checking behind allows me to see the river for free and realize my equity. Given Minni's tendency to call down sometimes, taking the free card is a safer way to potentially hit my flush or even a Jack and win the pot without risking a check-raise.

Check. I opened preflop and c‑bet the dry 5♦5♣7♠ flop and got called; pot is ~1,000. I checked the K♦ on the turn to control the pot because that card helps a lot of hands that called the flop (Kx and many pocket pairs). On the river the 4♥ is a blank — my A♠8♦ (Ace‑high) has very little showdown value and only limited blocker value. To bluff here I’d need a decent fold frequency from an opponent who just called the flop and checked the turn; that line is more consistent with one‑pair/slowplay holdings (5x, 7x, Kx, or pocket pairs) than with complete air. A small river bet (e.g. 300–400) requires the villain to fold ~25–30% to be +EV, which is unlikely given their line. Pot control and preserving stack equity is the higher EV line — check to showdown and fold to aggression if they bet large.

The river card 4h gave me a pair of 4s. While thin, this hand now has showdown value against Minni's likely missed draws or Ace-high hands, especially after she checked both the turn and the river. Betting doesn't accomplish much as better hands (like a 7 or a small pocket pair) are unlikely to fold to a single bet, and I'd rather take the free showdown to see if my pair is good.

I have pocket kings on the button — the second-best possible holding preflop and a clear raise/fold/call winner in position. There are only the blinds in and the pot is 150; standard, table-consistent sizing is a 3x BB open (300). Raising to 300 accomplishes three things: builds a healthy pot with a vastly ahead hand, isolates the blinds while keeping callers (Ax, medium pairs, suited connectors) in, and preserves postflop maneuverability with deep stacks. If either blind 3-bets, I will re-evaluate (likely 4-bet or shove depending on their sizing and remaining effective stacks); if called, I’ll play aggressively in position.

I am in the Small Blind with 7-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Minni has raised from the Button. Given the strength of my hand and being out of position for the rest of the hand, there is no reason to continue. I will fold.

Folding 5c Tc from the big blind against Minni's raise. While pot odds are acceptable, the hand is too weak to play out of position against a short-stacked disadvantage. With only 5025 chips, I need to preserve my stack for premium hands where I have a clear edge, not marginal spot hands like ten-high that will face substantial post-flop pressure.

I have been dealt pocket Jacks (Jh Js) on the button, which is a premium starting hand in a three-handed game. My stack is 8450, and I am facing the blinds Claude (4975) and Minni (26425). I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300, which has been my consistent sizing throughout the session, to build the pot and take control of the hand from position.

Folding 9-4o from small blind. This is one of the worst starting hands in poker with no business being played against a raise. With a moderately short stack of 4,975 chips, I should preserve chips for premium hands in better situations. The hand has poor equity postflop and I'd be out of position if I call. This is a standard fold.

Facing a button open to 300 with J♣9♦ in the big blind, this is a clear defend. Flash has been opening frequently (many 300 opens in the logs) so his range is wide. I'm deep-stacked (26,425) so J9o has decent implied odds and playability, but I'm out of position—3-betting with a marginal hand would bloat the pot OOP and commit too much. I will flat call the extra 200 (making my total committed 300) to keep the pot manageable and realize equity; I'll proceed post‑flop and fold to heavy aggression on unfavorable boards or continue on favorable textures.

Board 5♥ 8♥ 7♣ with J♣9♦: I have an open‑ended straight draw (6 or 10 are my 8 clean outs) plus three Js and three 9s to pair. That’s roughly ~33% to hit the straight by the river and ~50% to make at least a pair or straight by showdown — plenty of equity versus a wide button open. I’m out of position and there’s currently no bet on the flop, so I don’t want to bloated the pot leading out OOP with a semi‑bluff; checking preserves stack control, lets me realize equity against his frequent c‑bets, and keeps the option to check‑raise a small c‑bet or call and continue on favorable turn cards. Plan: check now, call a standard c‑bet to see the turn (re‑evaluate if he raises heavily), and if I hit a pair/straight or villain gives me a good price I’ll extract value on later streets.

I have an overpair with pocket Jacks on a low but highly coordinated board (5h-8h-7c). This board texture hits a lot of Minni's defending range from the Big Blind, including straight draws (like 6x, 9x, or 69) and heart flush draws. I need to bet for value against 8x, 7x, and smaller pocket pairs, and to charge the various draws present. A bet of about 60% of the pot is appropriate here to maintain initiative and protect my hand equity.

I call the 400 (total committed 700). J♣9♦ on 5♥8♥7♣ is a very playable flop: I have an open‑ended straight draw (8 clean outs to 6 or 10) plus 6 pair outs (three J's and three 9's) — roughly 14 outs to make at least a pair/straight by the river (~51%), and ~31% to hit the straight by the river. Flash opens and c‑bets frequently from the button and his 400 into the 650 pot is a medium-ish c‑bet that gives me ~27.6% immediate pot odds to call, so my drawing equity justifies a call. I'm out of position and don’t want to bloat the pot with a semi‑bluff raise; raising OOP with just a draw is unnecessarily risky and will commit chips when I have only draw equity. Plan: call to realize equity, see the turn, and then re‑evaluate — if I hit a pair/straight I will extract value, if I miss but get a favorable price I may continue vs small bets, and I will fold to heavy aggression on the turn if the action becomes large.

I have J♣9♦ on 5♥-8♥-7♣-3♠: an open‑ended straight draw (four 6s + four 10s = 8 clean outs) plus three Js and three 9s (6 more outs) for ~14 outs total. On the turn that’s about 14/46 ≈ 30.4% to make at least a pair/straight on the river (straight-only ≈ 8/46 ≈ 17.4%). Pot is 1,450 and I’m out of position vs Flash, who opens and c‑bets very wide and aggressively. Raising OOP with just a draw is unnecessary and will bloat the pot when I have only draw equity. I’ll check to control the pot and realize equity; if Flash bets a small-to-medium amount I’ll call (breakeven threshold = b/(1,450+b) — e.g. a bet up to ≈600 gives break‑even <= ~29–30% so calling is defensible), but I’ll fold to a large bet/shove (bets ≥ ~700 require > ~32% equity and are generally too expensive with my ~30% chance). If he checks back I take a free river and re-evaluate — if I pair or make the straight I’ll extract value, otherwise fold to substantial river aggression.

On a board of 5h-8h-7c-3s, I have an overpair with pocket Jacks. The turn 3s is essentially a blank, not changing the board texture significantly. Minni checked for a second time, which suggests she is either drawing or holding a marginal made hand like an 8 or 7. I need to continue betting for value and to protect my hand against the numerous draws available (flush draws and straight draws). A bet of 900 into a 1450 pot (approx 62%) is a strong sizing that charges those draws and extracts value from 99, 8x, or 7x hands.

I call. Hand is J♣9♦ on 5♥-8♥-7♣-3♠: an open‑ended straight draw (8 outs: 6s/10s) plus 6 pair outs = 14 outs total (~14/46 ≈ 30.4% to improve to at least a pair/straight by the river). Flash bet 900 into the pot shown (2350), so the immediate pot odds are 900/(2350+900) ≈ 27.7% — my drawing equity (~30.4%) is slightly higher than the price, so a call is +EV. Flash has been opening and betting very wide/aggressively, so keeping his range wide by calling is preferable to raising OOP with only draw equity (raising would bloat the pot and invite shoves/calls where I’m dominated). Plan: call to realize equity, re‑evaluate on the river — extract value if I pair/make the straight, fold to heavy aggression if I miss and face big bets or shoves. If Flash raises-turn (min‑raise to 1800 or jams) I will fold this draw OOP.

Board Qc on 5♥ 8♥ 7♣ 3♠ Qc and my hand J♣9♦. I missed — no pair, no straight (needed a 6 or 10), no flush. I called flop and turn with a draw, which was correct given villain’s wide, aggressive opening and the pot odds. Flash checked the river to me after barreling earlier streets: that line usually means pot‑control with a made but vulnerable hand (8x/7x/5x), some Qx turned into a protection line, or occasionally a failed bluff. With virtually no showdown value and very little fold equity, leading out here would be a thin, high‑variance bluff that’s likely to be called by any single pair or better and can be check‑raised by strong hands. I’ll check and take the showdown — avoid bloat OOP with no pair, preserve stack, and let villain show if they were bluffing. If Flash now bets into me I’ll fold to significant aggression.

On the river, the board is 5h-8h-7c-3s-Qc. The Queen is an overcard to my pocket Jacks, which is a bit concerning given the action. Minni has called two streets, suggesting she has a piece of the board or a draw. While most of the straight and flush draws missed, the Queen could have improved hands like QJs or QTs, which she might have peeled with on the flop. By checking behind, I realize my showdown value against her 8x, 99, or TT type hands and avoid being check-raised or value-owning myself against a slow-played straight, set, or a rivered Queen. I have enough equity to win at showdown frequently, but not enough to reliably extract more value after the overcard hits.

Ad4c is a weak holding with poor kicker strength. While I have positional advantage on the button and the call is only 100 chips, I'm short-stacked and should preserve chips for stronger spots. The 4 kicker makes this hand vulnerable even if I hit an ace, and it doesn't have good implied odds for my stack depth. Folding here is disciplined play given my chip position and hand strength.

Claude folded so this is heads-up vs Flash (BB). Q♠6♠ is a playable, suited queen with good postflop playability and I have positional advantage after the flop (I’m effectively the button). Folding is too weak and calling 50 is passive — it lets the BB see a cheap flop with the initiative. I want to take the lead, get some fold equity, and either take the pot preflop or play postflop with position, so I raise to the minimum standard open (200 total = 2x BB). The size keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks while still applying pressure to weaker holdings; if Flash 3-bets I can easily fold or re-evaluate, and if called I can continuation-bet favorable flops or realize my equity on draw-heavy boards.

In the Big Blind with King-Four offsuit, I'm getting 3:1 odds to call a small open from Minni in the Small Blind. K4o is generally strong enough to defend against a standard small blind open, but Minni has been very active and has a large stack. However, since I only need to put in 100 more to see a flop in a pot of 300, and I have position (or effectively play a heads-up pot from the big blind after the small blind opens), calling is a standard defensive play.

Flop is Qd As Ac and I hold Qs6s. That gives me two pair (Aces & Queens) but it’s a very vulnerable two-pair: any Ace in Flash’s range already has me crushed (trips), and some Qx combos with a better kicker can beat/tie me. Flash called my 2x open from the BB, so his calling range contains a lot of Ax and other hands that connect with this board. The pot is small (~400) relative to deep stacks, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginally-strong but vulnerable hand. I’ll check for pot control and to allow him to bluff or bluff-catch himself — plan is to call small-to-medium bets (probe bets) to keep weaker hands and draws in, but fold to large-sized bets/raise that look like an Ace. If he checks back I get a free turn to improve or re-evaluate. In short: avoid building a big pot out of position against the portion of his range that already beats me, extract value only when appropriate on later streets.

On a board of Qd-As-Ac, my King-high has some showdown value but very little equity against Minni's range if she continues. Minni checked, which could be a trap with an Ace or a Queen, or simply a surrender with a weaker hand. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and get to see a free turn card with a hand that might still be the best or can improve to a strong second-best hand if a King falls. There's no need to turn King-high into a bluff on such a static, coordinated board where she likely has many Aces.

I check. On Q♦ A♠ A♣ 3♣ with Q♠6♠ I have two pair (Aces & Queens) but it’s a vulnerable two‑pair: any A in Flash’s range makes trips and some Qx combos with better kickers can beat me. Flash checked the flop after defending his BB, so his range contains Ax, some Qx and a lot of medium holdings that will often check/call rather than shove. There are no meaningful draws to charge on the turn and the pot is small (~400) while stacks are deep, so pot control is correct — betting just bloats a pot I don’t want to commit to with a marginally‑dominated hand. Plan: check turn to keep the pot manageable, call small‑to‑medium probe bets (to keep worse Qx and bluffs) and fold or re‑evaluate against large, polarized bets that credibly represent an Ace; if he checks back I’ll reassess the river for thin value or a check‑call line.

On a board of Qd-As-Ac-3c, Minni has checked for the second time. This board is very static and favors the pre-flop raiser, but since she hasn't continuation bet or led the turn, she likely has a weak hand or a medium-strength showdown hand. King-high still holds a decent amount of showdown value against her air, and there's little benefit to betting here since I'm unlikely to fold out a better hand (like a weak Queen or a small pocket pair) and I don't want to get check-raised by a trapped Ace. Checking back to see a free river is the most consistent and safe play.

Board: Qd As Ac 3c 9c. I hold Qs6s — two pair (Aces & Queens) but it’s very vulnerable: any Ace is trips and some Qx with a better kicker beat me; the river 9c also brings three clubs on board so a made club-flush is possible for callers who held two clubs. Flash called my 2x open preflop and then checked flop and turn, which makes a trapping Ace possible but more likely indicates a lot of non-Ax, single-Q or medium holdings in his calling range. Pot is only 400 and stacks are deep — I don’t want to inflate a pot with a marginally dominated hand. Betting risks getting value-called by hands that beat me (Ax, better Qx, or a club flush) as often as it extracts value from worse hands. I’ll keep pot control and check to showdown; if he now leads out on the river I’ll treat small probes as calls for thin value but fold to large polarized shoves representing trips/flush. Action: check to avoid bloating the pot with a vulnerable two‑pair.

The board is Qd-As-Ac-3c-9c. Minni has checked all three streets after raising pre-flop. This suggests she either has a very weak hand or a medium-strength showdown hand she's content to check down. My King-high has significant showdown value in this spot against her potential air (missed draws or lower high cards). Betting is unlikely to fold out a better hand like a weak Queen or a small pocket pair, and checking allows me to realize my equity for free. I will check back to see if my King-high is good.

AQs on the button in a three-handed pot is a premium opener — I want to take the initiative and build a pot while keeping postflop leverage (position). Standard 3x open to 300 is the right size here: large enough to fold out weak blind defenses and reduce multiway scenarios, but not so big that I overcommit versus possible 3-bets. Plan: c-bet favorable flops and use position to realize equity; if Claude (shorter BB) 3-bets, I'll generally call and play postflop or 4-bet shove only versus very large/committed sizing. Raise to 300 to balance aggression and exploitability given table tendencies.

I am in the Small Blind holding 8h 3d, which is an extremely weak hand. Minni has raised to 3BB (300) from the Button. While I have a reasonably deep stack, playing out of position with such a weak hand against a player who has been active and successful (Minni) is a recipe for losing chips. There is no reason to defend or bluff in this spot.

Calling 200 with J9o in the Big Blind. Minni's button raise is wide and I'm getting adequate pot odds (2.25:1). J9o has reasonable equity with straight potential and two overcards. The call size is small relative to my 4875 chip stack (~4%), making this a mathematically sound play despite positional disadvantage.

Checking J9o on the flop out of position is the most prudent play. While I have some equity with straight possibilities, the King favors Minni's button-raising range. By checking, I control pot size, keep options open for turn improvement, and avoid overcommitting with marginal equity from an out-of-position spot. This is a standard defensive check that lets me see another card while minimizing losses on a weak holding.

I opened preflop and got checked to on a relatively dry K♣-3♥-6♠ board. A♠Q♠ missed but has good blockers (the Ace), some backdoor equity and strong position. Claude’s check is not a sign of strength; a standard continuation bet will fold out unpaired broadways and small pairs, deny equity to backdoor draws, and extract value from worse Ax/Qx holdings. I’ll make a small-to-medium c-bet (~1/3 pot) to keep the pot controllable if called and retain flexibility on the turn. If he flats I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he check-raises large I’ll give heavy weight to Kx/sets and proceed cautiously (likely folding to very large aggression).

This is a straightforward call situation. I'm getting exceptional pot odds (3.88:1) with J9o on a King-high flop, and I only need 20% equity to break even. My hand has significant value here:

1) **Equity Assessment**: J9o has approximately 40% equity against Minni's button-raising range, which is excellent for this spot.

2) **Drawing Strength**: I have 8 clean outs to an open-ended straight (any 7 or Q) plus 6 additional overcards (J and 9 both beat the 6), giving me multiple ways to improve on the turn.

3) **Pot Odds**: 875:225 = 3.88:1, which is far better than my break-even odds.

4) **Stack Management**: The 225 call represents only 4.8% of my 4675 stack, making this a low-risk, high-reward situation where I can easily see another card.

5) **Position**: While I'm out of position, the small raise amount and drawing potential make the call rational. If I hit any straight or overcard on the turn, I'll have significant implied odds.

Calling allows me to take advantage of excellent pot odds while maintaining flexibility for the turn action.

Checking J9o on the turn with a 3-K-6-T board. I have approximately 4 clean outs to a queen-high straight plus two live overcards, giving me roughly 30-35% equity. Being out of position with a marginal hand, checking controls the pot size and lets me see Minni's action before committing further. The T didn't improve my hand significantly, and I need to take a discounted river card to complete my draw. This is a prudent defensive check that manages my stack while keeping the hand alive.

I c-bet the flop and Claude called, but the turn (T♣) pairs the board and improves many hands in his calling range (Tx, Kx, pocket pairs). I hold A♠Q♠ and missed — I only have a one-card Broadway draw (J — ~4 outs, ~8–9% on the river) and essentially only Ace-high showdown value now. Betting into a range that contains lots of hands that beat me (or will check‑raise) is thinly profitable: my bluffs will fold out few better hands and I face tough decisions if Claude raises. With stacks deep enough that a raise would commit a large portion of his chips, the prudent play is pot control. I’ll check the turn, keep the pot manageable, and re-evaluate the river (call small bets with blocker value or fold to large aggression; consider a small river stab on safe runouts).

I have made a King-high straight (9-T-J-Q-K) on the river after my draw completed with the Qd. This is a very strong hand with the only better possibility being Ace-high straight (requiring AK, AQ, or AJ). Minni's check on the turn indicates a weaker holding, likely a pair or two-pair at best. A value bet of 400 (representing 36% of the 1100 pot) extracts maximum value from Minni's marginal holdings while appearing natural. This sizing is strong but not polarizing, getting called by worse pairs while pricing out marginal hands and maintaining fold equity.

I rivered top pair with top kicker (A♠Q♠ pairing the Q♦). Claude’s line — calling my flop c‑bet, checking the turn, then a small 400 into the 1,100 pot — looks like a thin value/probe or a blocker bet: his river range will include Kx (which beats me), Qx and small pocket pairs (which I beat), and some air/bluffs. Pot is 1,500 and calling 400 (~21% of the pot after my call) is cheap to realize value from worse hands and bluffs. Raising to 800 is thin — it will often fold out worse hands I want called and risks getting raised/committed by the Kx hands that already dominate me. I’ll take the straightforward line and call to extract showdown value while keeping the pot controlled against the parts of his range that beat me.

I am on the button with K-10 offsuit, which is a strong hand in a three-handed game. I have a significant stack and positional advantage. Opening with a raise to 300 is a standard move to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds, especially since Minni in the big blind has been very active.

9d4c is a weak, unconnected hand with poor equity from the small blind. Facing Flash's aggressive raise from the button, I'm out of position post-flop with marginal holdings. My stack is relatively short (5900), so I need to be disciplined with hand selection and preserve chips for premium opportunities. Folding here is the correct play against strong table dynamics, allowing me to wait for better spots to use my remaining stack effectively.

Facing a button open to 300 from an opponent who opens a lot, A♦J♣ is a strong hand to play aggressively from the big blind. 3-betting gets value from worse aces/broadways and folds out a lot of the button's wide opening range, while also taking the initiative preflop. I'll size to ~3x the open (raise to 900 total) — big enough to generate fold equity and isolate, but not so large as to commit unnecessarily against a 4-bet. Plan: fold to a large 4-bet (indication of a premium), call smaller 4-bets very selectively; if called, play the flop proactively (c-bet favorable ace/low-texture flops, proceed cautiously on dynamic boards since I'm OOP).

I opened from the button with KTo, a standard play in a three-handed game. Minni, who has been extremely active and aggressive, responded with a 3-bet to 900. While KTo is a decent hand, it often finds itself dominated by a 3-betting range (hands like AK, AQ, AJ, or KQ). In a previous similar encounter (Hand #85), Minni 3-bet me and I folded. Given Minni's stack size and her willingness to apply pressure on later streets, calling here would likely lead to a difficult post-flop situation where I may be forced to fold anyway if I don't hit the board hard. To preserve my stack and avoid a potentially bloated pot with a marginal hand, I will fold.

Calling 100 from the button with K8o is standard. The hand has decent potential, my position is favorable, and I can see the flop to make further decisions. Folding would be too tight from the button, and raising would overcommit with a non-premium hand against two opponents where I could easily be dominated.

I have K♥4♦ in the small blind facing a limp (Claude called 100) and the big blind yet to act. Pot is 250 and it only costs me 50 more to complete (to 100 total), so the price to see a flop is excellent. K4o is a marginal, often-dominated hand and I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with a weak kicker — a raise to 200 would be thin and likely to invite a reraise or a call from the big blind, which I don’t want to face multiway. I’ll complete to 100 to see a flop cheaply; plan is to evaluate on the flop (look to continue only with a K, strong draws, or a good board texture; otherwise check-fold to significant aggression).

I am in the Big Blind with Ks 3c. The pot is currently 300 with both Claude (Button) and Minni (Small Blind) limping in. Since I am already in the Big Blind and there has been no raise, I have the option to check and see a free flop. King-high has decent potential in a limped pot, but it is not strong enough to warrant a raise for value or as a bluff against two opponents. Checking allows me to realize my equity without further investment.

Flop is 6♠ T♠ Q♥ and I hold K♥4♦ — I have missed: no pair and no meaningful draw (one overcard only, and with only one heart in hand there is no realistic heart flush by river). This is a limp pot, three-way and I’m out of position to both opponents, and the board is fairly dynamic (two spades + connected broadway cards) so bluffing OOP into two players is thin. Priority is pot control and realizing equity: check to keep the pot small and allow weaker hands to bluff. Plan is to fold to any significant c-bet, but I’ll consider calling a very small probe (~100 or less, ~1/3 pot) for a cheap turn where a King gives me showdown value.

I have King-high on a board of 6s-Ts-Qh. Minni checked to me in the Big Blind. With Claude still to act behind me and no pair or significant draw, there is no reason to bet. Checking allows me to see a turn card for free and potentially improve my hand without risking chips in a multi-way pot.

Checking the flop with K8d. The board (6s-Ts-Qh) is relatively dry, and I have only King-high with a backdoor flush draw. Both opponents checked to me, suggesting weakness. With a marginal hand in position, the correct play is to take a free card and see the turn before committing more chips. Betting here would be overvaluing King-high against two opponents, and checking allows me to potentially improve or fold cheaply if the turn brings a dangerous card.

Pot is 300 and it’s checked to me on the turn. I hold K♥4♦ — no pair and no realistic draw (with Q♥ and A♥ on board and only one heart in hand I cannot make a flush by the river). The turn Ace actually makes my hand much worse since many limpers will have A‑x or better. I’m out of position to two players and this is a multiway spot where bluffing is thin. Don’t bloat the pot with a marginal hand — control the pot and see a cheap river. I’ll check and plan to fold to any meaningful bet on the turn; only a very tiny probe would be considered, but otherwise wait for a King on the river before investing.

I have King-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack) on an Ace-high board. The pot was limped pre-flop and checked around on the flop. Minni has checked again on the turn. Since the board is quite coordinated and there are two opponents, betting into them without a made hand is risky. Checking allows me to see the river for free and potentially realize my equity or improve to a straight if a Jack appears, without committing more chips to a small pot.

Checking the turn with K8d. Both opponents checked, indicating weakness. Although I have 17 outs (11 for a diamond flush, 6 for pairs), my hand is still currently behind. With a critically short stack at 5800 chips, preserving chips and getting a free river card with a draw is the correct play. The Ace on the turn didn't help my hand, so taking a passive approach and seeing the river cheaply maximizes my equity and stack preservation.

Board A♥ Q♥ T♠ 6♠ 7♦ — I hold K♥4♦: no pair, no realistic draw, K‑high only. This was a limp pot checked through and we’re now three‑handed. Bluffing on the river into two opponents has very low fold equity (both have deep stacks relative to the small pot and can easily call a small probe), and many likely show up with A‑x, Q‑x or T‑x that will call. I also block one king combo but that doesn’t give me showdown value. Best line is pot control: check and fold to any meaningful bet. If someone shoves or barrels big, I’ll fold; only an extremely tiny, tellable probe would merit a call, but that’s not the current situation. So I check and keep the pot small.

I have King-high on a board of 6s-Ts-Qh-Ah-7d. The pot was limped pre-flop and checked through the flop and turn. Minni has checked again on the river. My hand has very little showdown value, but in a small 300-chip pot against two opponents who have shown no interest, it's rarely worth the risk to bluff. If I bet, I am unlikely to get better hands to fold, as any weak pair will likely call a small bet in this situation. Checking is the most prudent move to see if my King-high can miraculously win at showdown or simply to conclude the hand with no further loss.

River showdown with King-high after all opponents checked. Both Minni and Flash have checked to me on the river after checking the turn, indicating weakness. With a marginal hand (K8d = King-high) and a critically short stack of 5800 chips, betting accomplishes nothing - I either fold out worse hands or get called by better ones. Checking preserves my stack and allows me to go to showdown for free. The 300-chip pot isn't worth risking chips on a weak hand when my position is precarious.

I’m on the button with Td6c. Pot 150, to call 100. T6o is a marginal hand, but button vs the blinds in a 3-handed pot is a profitable spot to open: I get positional advantage and can steal the blinds often. Opponents have healthy stacks; Flash (SB) is prone to aggression, Claude (BB) defends but will fold to applied pressure sometimes. I want to take the initiative without bloating the pot with a weak holding, so I’ll open-raise to 300 (3xBB). This size balances fold equity and keeps the pot manageable if called. Postflop plan: vs a single caller I’ll c-bet a wide range on favorable boards and play straightforwardly when I hit; if both call I’ll proceed cautiously and avoid bloating pots with just a Ten kicker; if I face a sizable 3-bet I’ll fold most of this range and re-evaluate only against small, polarized 3-bets.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Trey offsuit. Minni opened for 300 from the Button. This is a very weak hand to play out of position, and calling or raising would likely lead to a loss in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

5-2 is the weakest hand in poker. I'm in the big blind facing a button raise from Minni (24,150 chips, an aggressive player). Even though the pot is offering 2.25:1 odds (200 to win 450), 5-2 has minimal equity and plays poorly out of position. At hand 99/150 with 6000 chips (60 BB), I need to preserve my stack for premium holdings. Folding is the mathematically sound play here - no reason to bleed chips with the worst hand in the deck.

I am on the button with Q3 offsuit. While a steal attempt is sometimes viable in this position, Q3o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop if called. Given that Minni (BB) has a massive stack and has been active, and Claude (SB) is relatively short and may look to jam, folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

Calling with K♠ Q♣ from the SB heads-up against Minni. This is a premium hand with excellent 3:1 pot odds. Simple call preserves stack while allowing me to see the flop with a strong hand. After calling 50 more to complete the SB, total commitment is 100 chips.

I’m in the big blind with A♦8♦ and Claude (SB) just limped. Claude’s recent lines show a lot of limp/call and passive frequency, so his range is wide and often weak. A8s is a strong heads‑up hand and I want to isolate the limp, gain fold equity, and build a pot with a hand that plays well multi‑street (top‑ace potential and diamond backdoor). I’ll raise to 400 total (I’ve already posted 100, so I’m adding 300) — large enough to discourage pure speculative hands and to get value from worse aces/broadways, but small enough to keep him from folding too often and to leave room to play postflop with deep stacks. If he calls I’ll c‑bet favorable A/diamond/connected flops and control the pot on dry boards; if he 3‑bets I’ll re‑evaluate given his range and our stack depth.

This is a heads-up situation between me (SB with K♠Q♣) and Minni (BB) after Flash folded. Minni raised to 400 after I completed the small blind.

K-Q is a premium hand with excellent postflop playability. Analysis:

1. POT ODDS: I'm facing 300 to call into a 500 pot (before my call), giving me 800:300 = 2.67:1 odds - excellent pot odds for K-Q

2. HAND STRENGTH: K-Q is strong but not absolute - it benefits significantly from seeing the flop. It has solid win probability against Minni's range and excellent postflop flexibility.

3. STACK SITUATION: With 5900 chips, calling 300 leaves me with 5600 remaining, which gives me room to play the hand postflop without being committed.

4. OPPONENT TENDENCIES: Over 99 hands, Minni has been an aggressive big blind player. Her raise could represent a range including mid-strength hands where K-Q is ahead or competitive.

5. POSITIONAL ADVANTAGE: In heads-up SB, I'll be out of position preflop but in position postflop, which is valuable with K-Q for extracting value or making intelligent folds.

A reraise would risk too much with the stack depth given the uncertainty, and folding premium K-Q at 2.67:1 odds would be overly tight. Calling allows me to see the flop and make better decisions with more information.

CALL is the optimal play here.

K♠Q♣ on 2♦6♦A♥ is a strong hand but currently has no made hand. The Ace heavily favors Minni's BB raising range. With a 7:1 SPR and out-of-position, checking allows me to gather information from her likely c-bet while maintaining flexibility with my 6 outs to top pair. This is the optimal information-gathering play against an aggressive opponent in this situation.

Flop is exactly the kind of board I wanted to see after raising preflop: A♥ 2♦ 6♦ with me holding A♦8♦. I have top pair (ace) plus the nut diamond draw (holding the A♦), and Claude is a passive limper/caller with a wide, often weak range. He checked to me, so I should continuation-bet to extract value and charge hands that have equity against me (diamond draws, pairs like 6s/2s, worse Axs) while also denying free cards to worse hands. I size to ~1/2 pot (400 into 800): big enough to get value from worse aces and medium pairs and to make non-nut draws pay to see the turn, but small enough to keep him calling with those hands rather than folding everything. If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (diamond completes my flush — go large for value; blanks — continue barreling for value/protection or pot control depending on his turn action). Betting achieves both value and protection, so I bet 400.

K♠Q♣ vs aggressive BB c-bet with Ace on board. I'm getting 3:1 pot odds (25% breakeven) with 6+ outs giving me approximately 30-40% equity against Minni's wide heads-up c-betting range. This is a mathematically profitable call. While out of position is suboptimal, the pot odds justify it. I maintain fold equity on future streets and strong upside if I hit. Minni's aggression throughout the session suggests she's betting a wide range here, not just premium holdings.

Out of position with K♠Q♣ (King-high) on 2♦6♦A♥Tc board. I have 6 outs (~24% equity) to improve on the river. With a deep stack and facing an aggressive BB opponent, checking is optimal. This allows me to gather information about her turn action and make better decisions. If she checks, I get a free river card with showdown potential. If she bets, I can evaluate pot odds and her likely range before deciding. Betting K-high out of position would be too speculative and easily exploitable against this aggressive opponent.

I have A♦8♦ on A♥ 2♦ 6♦ / Tc — top pair plus the nut diamond draw. Claude is a passive limper/caller who called my preflop raise and my flop c‑bet, so his range is wide (worse Axs, small/mid pocket pairs, and diamond draws). This is exactly the kind of hand to extract value and charge his draws. I’ll bet for value and protection: a ~2/3‑pot turn bet (1000 into the 1600 pot) charges non‑nut diamond draws, gets called by worse aces and most pairs, and still leaves room to maneuver on the river. If he folds we win the pot; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river (blank -> barreled for value/protection; diamond -> bet big for the nut flush; large check‑raise here would represent trips/full house and I would heavily discount his range).

K-Q is a strong preflop hand but has no made hand on a 2d-6d-Ah-Tc board. With only 6 outs to the river, I have approximately 12.76% equity. Facing 1000 to call into a 3600 pot (3.6:1 odds), I need 21.7% equity to break even. My equity falls significantly short of the requirement. Minni's aggressive betting pattern—c-betting the flop after the Ace came out and betting again on the turn—indicates she likely has an Ace or stronger holding. Playing K-high out of position for 1000 chips without sufficient pot odds is a mathematically incorrect decision. Folding preserves my 5200-chip stack for more favorable situations.

2-4 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker and plays poorly both preflop and postflop. Despite being on the Button with positional advantage, this hand lacks the equity and potential to profitably call 100 chips. The risk-reward is unfavorable - I would likely need to hit a lucky flop against aggressive opponents like Minni (who has been playing many hands) and Flash in the Big Blind. Folding weak hands and preserving chips is a core principle I've followed throughout these 100 hands. This is a clear fold.

Heads-up from the small blind I should open a very wide range — this is a standard spot to raise to 2x BB and apply pressure. T3o is a marginal hand OOP, but the raise is cheap and gains fold equity vs the big blind while keeping my opening range balanced. I’ll raise to 200 total (I’ve posted 50, so +150) to either take the pot preflop or play the flop with the initiative. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll give up this specific hand; if called I’ll proceed cautiously OOP: c-bet small on dry boards or ones where a Ten helps, check/float or fold on coordinated/wet boards and expand on pot control when behind.

In this blind-vs-blind situation, Minni has raised from the Small Blind and I am in the Big Blind with 7h 3h. Claude has folded, so I will have position on Minni for the rest of the hand. The price to call is only 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 pot odds (requiring 25% equity). Although 73s is a weak hand, the combination of being in position and receiving great pot odds makes this a standard call to see a flop.

I opened from the SB and have initiative. The flop Qc 9h 5d is very dry and misses my T3o, but it’s the exact texture where a small continuation bet will fold a lot of the big blind’s cold‑call range (overcards, weak unpaired hands and some weak pairs). I don’t want to check and allow free cards or give him a free pitch at a backdoor draw, but I also don’t want to overcommit with zero showdown value. I’ll make a small c‑bet ~37–40% pot to maximize fold equity while keeping the pot controllable if called. If he raises I’ll fold this specific hand; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and re‑evaluate based on card and action.

On a board of Qc-9h-5d, my hand (7h 3h) has very little equity. While I have a backdoor flush draw and am in position, I have no made pair and no immediate straight draw. Minni's continuation bet is small (about 37% of the pot), but even with the good price, 7-high is too weak to continue with, especially since most of Minni's range contains overcards or pairs that currently beat me. Folding here preserves my stack for better opportunities.

I’m on the button with K♥6♣ — a marginal hand but strong enough to open from the button because I have position and two players are in the blinds. The pot is only 150 and to-call is 100; a standard 3xBB open (300 total) gives good fold equity, reduces multi-way scenarios, and is the sizing I’ve been using at this table. I’ll avoid the tiny min-raise (200) which invites callers and squeezes. If I get a large 3-bet (especially from Claude with ~51bb) I’ll fold K6o; if called I’ll play postflop in position, c-betting favorable textures and exercising pot control on bad boards.

I am in the Small Blind with Kc Th. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300. KTo is a strong enough hand to defend against a standard Button open. I am choosing to call rather than 3-bet to keep the pot manageable while out of position against the chip leader (Minni) and to see how the Big Blind (Claude) reacts. My pot odds are favorable for a call.

Folding 2-8o in the big blind. This is the worst starting hand in poker and cannot justify a call in a 3-way pot, even with reasonable pot odds. I'm out of position against both opponents, and the hand has minimal equity. Better to preserve my 5100 chip stack and wait for playable holdings.

I have King-high with a gutter-ball straight draw (needs Queen, Eight) and overcards on a Jack-high board. I am out of position against Minni, the pre-flop aggressor. The standard and most disciplined play here is to check to the raiser and evaluate their response based on their bet size and the board texture.

I raised preflop from the button and got one caller from the SB. The flop 9♦4♦J♠ is a poor c-bet spot for my range (two diamonds + a mid J) — it actually favours the caller: lots of diamond draws, Jx and pocket pairs that will continue. My hand K♥6♣ has no pair and no diamond draw and has very little showdown value. I prefer pot control and to use position to see a turn free or react to a bet rather than barreling into a lot of draws. Plan: check and evaluate — if Flash checks back I’ll continue slow and possibly take a stab on a blank turn; if he bets small (~<=1/3 pot) I may call to realize equity and keep the pot manageable; fold to large leads unless I improve.

On the turn 5c, the board remains relatively safe. Minni checked back on the flop after being the pre-flop aggressor, which often suggests a marginal made hand (like a middle pair or weak top pair) or a complete miss that she doesn't want to bluff with. I have King-high with an inside straight draw (Queen) and two overcards. I will check again to see if she checks back or to see what kind of bet size she produces if she decides to stab at the pot. Checking allows me to potentially see a free river and realize my equity or catch a bluff if she bets small.

I checked the draw-heavy flop (9♦4♦J♠) for pot control — the texture favors the caller (lots of diamond and backdoor draws and Js/pairs). The turn 5♣ is a relatively blank and Flash has checked twice, so his range contains a lot of missed draws and weak pairs. My K♥6♣ has no pair and little showdown value, so the highest EV line is to bluff for fold equity while keeping the price low. I’ll bet roughly half the pot (350 into 700) to fold out many one-pair/overcard hands and deny free rivers to backdoor draws; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the river and fold to significant aggression since I have no real showdown value.

I am calling a half-pot bet on the turn from Minni after we both checked the flop. My hand, King-high with a gutshot to the nuts (Queen) and two overcards (King and Ten), has approximately 21.7% equity. Given the pot odds are 3:1 (requiring 25% equity), this is a borderline call. However, Minni's check on the flop suggests a capped or wide range, making it possible that my overcards are live or that she is bluffing. Additionally, the implied odds of hitting a straight on the river against an aggressive opponent make the call profitable.

The river 3h is a complete blank that doesn't change the board texture. My King-high has almost no showdown value, as Minni's range for betting the turn likely includes pairs or Ace-high. However, having checked the flop and called the turn, leading out on the river would be a polarized move. Given Minni's chip lead and previous aggression, I prefer to check and see if she checks back a marginal hand or a missed diamond draw. I am not confident enough in a bluff here to commit more chips against a stack that can easily call.

Check. I opened from the button and intentionally bet the turn (350 into 700) as a bluff/semi-bluff, but Flash’s call on the turn showed stickiness and a range that includes Jx, 9x, small pairs (4x/5x) and other hands that will rarely fold the river. My K♥6♣ has essentially no showdown value versus that calling range and the river 3♥ is a blank — I don’t improve. To make a profitable bluff I need a reasonably high fold frequency: e.g. a 350 bet into 1400 requires ~20% folds to breakeven, a 700 bet requires ~33%. Given he called the turn, his fold frequency is likely lower than that and I’d risk being called or raised. With a large effective stack and a small pot, the +EV line is to conserve chips and check down. I’ll check and concede unless he leads, in which case I’ll fold to significant aggression because I have no showdown value.

I am on the button with K4o in a three-handed game. While any King is often an opening candidate from the button, K4o is at the very bottom of that range. Given that the big blind (Minni) has been playing very aggressively and holds a significant chip lead, I expect to be challenged frequently. I'd prefer to fold this hand and wait for a stronger opening hand or a better situation to exploit the blinds rather than playing a weak King in a potentially bloated pot.

Heads-up after button fold with J6o. Getting 3:1 pot odds (150:50). While J6 is weak, the pot odds are favorable and my short stack necessitates wider play from the small blind. Equity is approximately 35-40%, exceeding the 25% threshold needed to justify the call. Folding conservatively would accelerate my chip deterioration.

Claude limped from the SB (called 50) into my BB. Heads-up K♠Q♦ is a strong raising hand; against a limp I want to isolate and take the initiative. A standard 3x BB sizing (raise to 300 total) pressures weak limpers and folds out pure floats/small pairs while leaving plenty of maneuverability postflop given my 24,850 stack vs his 5,000. If he folds I scoop the 200 pot; if he calls I have good equity and position to play postflop; if he 3-bets I'll re-evaluate. Raise to 300 to isolate and seize the initiative.

Heads-up against Minni (BB raised to 300, I'm in SB). Current pot is 400, need to call 200 more (already committed 50).

Pot odds analysis: 400:200 = 2:1, requiring 33.3% equity to justify the call.

Hand strength: J6o is weak in absolute terms but significantly stronger heads-up. Against Minni's wide raising range from BB, this hand has approximately 40-45% equity.

Key factors:
1. Excellent pot odds (2:1) vs. required equity threshold (33.3%)
2. Heads-up dynamics make J6o considerably more playable than in full-handed games
3. My short stack (5000 chips/50BB) necessitates wider play - being overly conservative accelerates chip deterioration
4. Already committed 50 chips, reducing effective cost to call

This is a standard heads-up SB vs BB scenario where J6o is well-within calling range. Folding would be excessively tight and allow Minni to run over the blinds profitably.

Heads-up on the flop with J6h (Jack-Six of hearts) against Minni's BB raise. Board is 7d-Td-4h. I currently have no made hand (Jack high) but do have a backdoor flush draw (4 cards to the flush). My hand is weak in absolute strength and I have no showdown value against Minni's likely range. With a short stack (4800 chips / 50BB), my equity is marginal. I have the option to check for free, which is prudent given my hand weakness and stack depth. Checking allows me to control pot size and see Minni's action. If she checks behind, I get a free card; if she bets, I can fold with minimal loss or continue if the pot odds justify it with improved equity. Betting weak hands out of position with a short stack accelerates my chip erosion. Checking is the mathematically sound play here.

I was the preflop aggressor and hold K♠Q♦ — two overcards to the T74 flop plus a backdoor diamond. Claude limped/called preflop, so his calling range is wide (many weak pairs, Tx, broadways, some diamond combos). As the raiser I should apply pressure and take the initiative: a small continuation bet (~1/3 pot) will fold out a large portion of his unpaired hands and floats while keeping worse hands and draws in that will pay me off on later streets. I size small (200) to maximize fold equity without committing too much vs a short-stacked opponent; if he calls I can continue using position, if he check-raises big or shoves I can give credit to him having strong Tx/sets and fold. Bet 200 into the 600 pot to seize the initiative and extract value/folds.

Calling with J6h on a 7d-Td-4h board against Minni's flop bet. Pot odds of 4:1 require only 20% equity; my hand has approximately 35-40% equity heads-up due to flush draw potential and Jack kicker value. Short stack (4800 chips/48BB) justifies wider play. Already committed 250 chips pre-flop, making the incremental cost reasonable. The backdoor flush draw and two-card improvement potential outweigh the weak made-hand strength given favorable pot odds.

Minni's turn check indicates weakness or pot control. My pair of Sixes is marginal and vulnerable. Betting only loses value as Minni folds better hands and is called by worse hands rarely. Checking allows me to: (1) see a free river card, (2) control pot size with a weak hand, (3) make a river decision based on Minni's action. With 4600 chips remaining and a 1000-chip pot, pot control with weak holdings is essential. Checking is the mathematically sound play here - conserving chips while maintaining some showdown value.

I raised preflop to isolate and c‑bet the flop; I hold K♠Q♦ on 7♦ T♦ 4♥ 6♣. The turn 6♣ is basically a blank for both of us — my backdoor diamond draw is dead (I needed both turn and river), but I still have two overcards and reasonable showdown equity vs a wide calling range. Claude called a small flop c‑bet, then checked the turn, which signals weakness or a medium pair/pot‑control hand. With position and the initiative, a controlled second barrel will fold out a lot of his floats and weaker pairs and win the pot now.

Sizing: the pot is 1,000 and Claude is short (≈4,600); I prefer a ~1/3 pot continue to maximize fold equity without bloating the pot with no made hand. I’ll bet 350. If he calls I’ll reassess on the river; if he check‑raises/shoves I’ll give credit to strong hands (T/sets/two‑pair) and fold this turn bluff since my equity in that spot is poor.

Calling with a pair of sixes and flush draw (10 hearts remaining). Pot odds of 3.86:1 require only 20.6% equity; my hand has 40-45% equity heads-up with the made pair plus 4-card flush draw. Short stack dynamics favor exploiting positive equity opportunities. Already committed 450 chips, so incremental cost of 350 is justified by superior pot odds and hand equity. This is a mathematically sound call.

River check with marginal pair of tens (board) and J6 kicker. Minni's turn check indicates weakness or pot control. With a short stack (4250 remaining) and a hand that has showdown value but limited value for betting, checking is correct. Betting would either get called by better hands or fold weaker hands that wouldn't call anyway. Taking the free showdown maximizes value with this marginal holding while conserving chips for future opportunities.

The river pairs the Ten and my K♠Q♦ only gives me a pair of Tens with K/Q kickers. Claude’s line (limp-call pre, call flop c-bet, call turn barrel, then check river) is exactly the sort of line a pocket pair, a small pair that turned into two-pair when the board paired, or a slow‑played Ten would take. Against that range my bluff has very little fold equity and many of his continuing hands already beat me (any pocket pair, a 7/6/4, or a held Ten). A small river bet could be called by hands that actually beat me (or induce a sticky call), and a larger bet risks getting check‑raised by a hand that crushed me. Given the board texture and his passive, sticky calls earlier, the +EV line is to avoid bluffing and check to showdown. If he bets into me I’ll re‑evaluate based on sizing, but I won’t bluff into this range here.

A3o is too weak to call with out of position against two opponents. Even from the button, the hand has poor equity and poor playability post-flop. With 59 hands remaining and a decent stack, folding weak hands and awaiting premium opportunities is the optimal strategy for maximizing profit in this cash game.

I’m in the small blind with K♠ T♣ heads-up vs the big blind. This is a strong, playable hand in this spot and I want to take the initiative rather than play OOP a cold call. A 3xBB-sized open (to 300) is standard here — it isolates the BB, provides fold equity, and doesn’t bloat the pot given deep stacks. Min-raising to 200 is too small and invites too many cheap calls; completing to 100 keeps us out of position with no initiative. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (KTs has decent equity vs lighter 3-bets but can fold to substantial pressure), and if called I’ll c-bet favorable flops (especially K/T textures) and proceed cautiously on ace-high/dry boards.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Eight offsuit. Minni (Small Blind) raised to 300. Since I am in position for the rest of the hand (Small Blind acts first post-flop) and the price to call is 200 to win a total pot of 600, I am getting 2:1 odds. J8o is strong enough to defend against a wide Small Blind opening range when I have the advantage of position.

I have top pair with a decent kicker (K♠T♣ on Jh Kc 6s). Against a BB call‑range this is a clear value/protection c‑bet spot even though I’m OOP. Flash called pre‑flop a lot, so his range contains many Jx, underpairs and broadways that I beat. Betting ~1/2 pot (~300 into 600) extracts value from worse Kx, Jx and pairs while charging any backdoor draws and denying free cards. It’s also a size that keeps a lot of worse hands in and allows me to fold to a very polarized shove or call and reevaluate a single raise. Plan: bet 300; if called, continue evaluating turn (bet for value on safe cards, pot‑control or fold to heavy aggression on scary cards).

I have flopped middle pair (Jacks) with an 8 kicker on a board of Kc-Jh-6s. Minni opened from the Small Blind and has led out for a half-pot continuation bet. My hand is too strong to fold to a single bet in position, but not strong enough to raise for value, as a raise would likely only be called by a King or a better Jack. Calling allows me to keep her bluffs in and reassess on the turn.

I have top pair (K♠) with a decent T kicker on a relatively dry board: J♥ K♣ 6♠ 9♣. I was the aggressor on the flop (300) and was called, so Flash’s continuing range is wide: worse Kx, Jx, medium pocket pairs and some broadways. The 9♣ on the turn is not a scary card — it gives some hands a pair of 9s but does not complete any realistic straight or flush (there are only three clubs among known cards including my Tc, so a river club alone cannot make a five‑card flush). This is a clear thin value/protection spot heads‑up: I want to extract value from worse Kx, Jx and small pairs while denying free cards to hands with equity. I’ll bet ~½ pot to keep weaker hands in and control the pot; if I face heavy, polarized aggression I’ll re‑evaluate (call reasonable raises for pot odds, fold to extremely polarized shove if necessary).

I currently have middle pair (Jacks) with an eight kicker on a board of Kc-Jh-6s-9c. Minni, playing from the Small Blind, has followed up her flop lead with a half-pot bet on the turn. Given that I am in position and Minni's range from the Small Blind is quite wide, middle pair remains a strong enough hand to continue. Folding now would be too exploitable against an aggressive opponent. I am getting 3:1 odds on a call, and by calling, I keep her range wide and allow myself to see a river card where I can reassess based on her action and the final board state.

I’m heads‑up and was the aggressor on flop and turn. I hold top pair with a decent kicker (K♠T♣) on a very dry J♥ K♣ 6♠ 9♣ 4♦ board. Flash has called my bets on both flop and turn, so his continuing range is wide (Jx, worse Kx, medium pairs and some broadways). There are only a few hands that beat me (notably two‑pair combos like KJ) and a lot of worse hands that will call a river value bet. Checking risks giving up thin value to lots of worse hands. I want a bet size that extracts from Jx/Kx/medium pairs while keeping weaker hands in — roughly 1/2 pot is appropriate. I’ll bet 1,200 into the 2,400 pot; if I face a large, polarized raise I will re‑evaluate (fold to extreme river jams, call smaller, non‑committal raises depending on range and sizing).

On a board of Kc-Jh-6s-9c-4d, I have middle pair (Jacks). Minni has led out for half the pot on every street (pre-flop, flop, turn, and river). While I have been calling down to see if she was bluffing or playing a weaker hand, a triple barrel on a King-high board is usually a strong signal of top pair or better, especially from a player who hasn't shown a high frequency of multi-street bluffs. I only beat a pure bluff here (like missed straight draws such as T8 or 75), and I lose to any King, any better Jack (AJ), or slow-played sets and straights (QT). Although I am getting 3:1 pot odds, Minni's line is consistent with value betting a King. I will fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

On the button with 9♥5♦ — a weak, offsuit hand — this is still a good steal spot versus only the blinds. I have great position and a big effective stack (25,300) relative to the blinds and opponents (Flash 8,700; Claude 5,850). Raising to 300 (≈3x the BB) applies pressure, narrows ranges, and avoids playing this marginal hand multiway. The raise size is big enough to take the blinds down often but small relative to my stack. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet/all-in; if I'm called, c-bet favorable flops and keep pot control on dry or bad boards. This maximizes fold equity and uses position to extract value or realize equity cheaply.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Jack offsuit (AJo), which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300, which is her standard opening size. AJo is well ahead of a typical Button opening range. By raising (3-betting) here, I take the initiative, potentially win the pot immediately, and thin the field by making it difficult for the Big Blind (Claude) to enter the pot. I will raise to 1,000 to apply pressure and define my hand strength while out of position.

Folding 9-4o against a 3-bet from out of position. This hand has poor equity and I'm out of position facing aggression from the small blind. No value in defending here - preserving chips for premium hands in better situations.

I opened to 300 on the button with 9♥5♦ and Flash (SB) 3‑bet to 1000. Pot is 1400 and it costs me 700 to call (I’d need ~33% equity to justify a call). 9‑5 offsuit is a very poor hand vs a SB 3‑betting range (dominated by pairs, broadways and many suited high cards); its equity vs a reasonable 3‑bet range is well below the required ~33%. A min‑4bet to 1700 is just a half‑bluff that won’t fold enough of his range and is a bad use of chips with this holding. I have position and deep stacks, so preserving my stack for better spots is correct. I fold.

I am on the button with T4 offsuit. This is a very weak hand. Claude is in the Small Blind and Minni is in the Big Blind. While I could try to steal the blinds, T4o is not a hand I want to play if I face resistance, and Minni has been quite active and has a massive stack. Folding is the most prudent and standard play here to conserve chips for better spots.

Folding 2-6o from small blind. This is one of the worst hands in poker and unplayable out of position against two opponents. Despite needing to call only 50 more chips, the hand's poor equity and my position disadvantage make this a clear fold. Preserving my 5800-chip stack for premium hands is the correct long-term strategy.

Calling 100 with A8o on the button. Good position, decent hand, reasonable pot odds (3:1), and flexibility to make decisions post-flop. Avoid overcommitting with a non-premium hand, but the combination of position, hand value, and odds makes this a clear call.

I have a medium pocket pair (7s7d) in the small blind facing a limp from the button. Claude’s limp is a wide, weak range — a raise here isolates and takes the initiative. A standard isolation size is appropriate: I’ll raise to 300 total (I’ve already posted 50, so I add 250). That sizing is in line with the table, gives good fold equity vs the big blind, and is small relative to my 25k stack while building the pot when I’m likely ahead. If I face a large 3‑bet I’ll re‑evaluate (often folding to a big 3‑bet OOP or calling a small 3‑bet to set‑mine); if called I’ll c‑bet favorable flops and play more cautiously on highly connected/overcard boards. Raising to 300 maximizes my EV here by isolating the limper and forcing others to pay to see a flop.

I am in the big blind with J3 offsuit. Claude limped on the button and Minni raised to 300 from the small blind. The pot is currently 500 and it costs me 200 more to call. J3o is a very weak hand, especially in a three-way pot where I will be out of position for the rest of the hand against the chip leader (Minni). Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to avoid losing more chips with a marginal hand.

Calling 200 to see the flop. As8d is a solid hand in heads-up play against Minni's wide raising range. Key factors: (1) I have excellent position on the button with positional advantage post-flop, (2) odds are favorable at 2.5:1 (200 to win 500), (3) A8o plays well post-flop with both high card and nut flush draw potential, (4) Minni has been aggressively raising throughout the session, suggesting a wide range that my hand dominates, (5) with only 5700 chips, I need to find spots to accumulate - this is a reasonable one. The plan is to play positionally post-flop and make decisions based on the board texture and Minni's action.

I raised preflop to isolate with 7♠7♦ and got called by Claude. Flop T♣ A♥ 4♠ is an overcard-heavy, low-draw texture where my medium pocket pair is vulnerable — Claude’s calling range from the button contains a lot of A-x, some Tx, and medium pairs. My preflop plan was to c-bet friendly flops and play cautiously on overcard/connected boards; this is exactly a cautious spot. Betting OOP here would have limited fold equity against a caller who likely has many A/T combos and risks facing a shove or raise that I’m rarely happy calling. Checking controls the pot, lets me realize equity (chance to hit a 7), and gives me the option to call a small continuation bet or fold to heavy action. Plan: check. If Claude leads a small-to-medium bet I’ll usually call to see a turn for cheap; I will fold to a large raise or shove since I’m often behind to his value range. If he checks back I’ll proceed similarly on the turn (bet if I improve, continue pot control if I don’t).

Betting 300 with top pair (A8) on a Tc Ah 4s flop. Minni's check from the SB suggests weakness or a medium-strength hand. I have excellent position on the button and a strong hand that's likely ahead of Minni's range. A value bet of 300 (40% of the pot) extracts chips from worse Ace holdings and mid-pairs while applying appropriate pressure without overcommitting given my 5,500-chip stack in a 150-hand tournament.

I raised preflop to isolate with 7♠7♦ and checked this Ace‑high flop to control the pot. Claude’s 300 into the pot is a small/medium c‑bet that represents a wide range (A‑x, some T‑x, and lots of air/marginal hands). My pair of sevens is frequently behind his value combos but ahead of many bluffs; the bet gives me ~23% pot odds to continue and I have strong implied odds with both stacks deep (Claude ~5.2k, my stack 24.75k).
