Raising OOP here would be risky and unnecessary; folding surrenders too much equity for a cheap price. I’ll call to see a turn and reevaluate: if I hit a 7 I’ll get aggressive; if an A/T or heavy action arrives on the turn I’ll fold to large aggression; if the turn is blank and he barrels small I’ll usually fold or decide based on sizing.

I have 7♠7♦ on Tc-Ah-4s-Kd. The turn is a second overcard that meaningfully reduces the relative strength of a medium pocket pair — Claude’s range after limping/calling and a small flop c-bet contains a lot of Ax, some Tx and Kx, and a fair amount of air/marginal hands. Betting OOP here would be a thin bluff: I have limited fold equity against his likely Ax/Kx/Tx combos and risk getting raised or called by hands that dominate me. Pot control is the priority. I’ll check to keep the pot manageable, realize equity (I can still river a set), and force Claude to show strength if he wants to build the pot. Plan if checked to me: if he bets a small-to-medium sizing I’ll usually call to see a river; I will fold to large turn raises or shoves since I’m rarely ahead. If he checks back I’ll re-evaluate on the river and only get aggressive if I improve.

Checking on the turn with As8d on a Tc Ah 4s Kd board. The King significantly reduces my hand strength—I was likely ahead on the flop with top pair, but now I'm vulnerable to AK, KK, and broadway combinations. Minni's turn check is ambiguous (could be weakness or a trap). With a short stack of 5200 chips, I should prioritize pot control and information gathering. Checking allows me to see the river card for free and make a better-informed decision with full board information. If Minni bets the river, I can then evaluate her range more accurately. This is a prudent play that preserves chips and avoids committing to marginal equity situations.

Board Tc Ah 4s Kd 6s, I hold 7s7d. I isolated preflop and called Claude’s small flop bet, which puts him on a wide range heavy with A-x, some T-x and K-x; his turn check is a pot-control line rather than definite weakness. I missed a set and the river is a blank — betting here would be a very thin bluff because many hands that beat me (A, T, K) will call a small bet, and few worse hands will fold. Pot is only 1,300 while my stack is deep relative to the pot; there’s no reason to risk chips on a low-EV bluff. Optimal play is to check and take showdown (and fold to large river raises if presented).

River bet with As8d (top pair) after Minni checks through the flop, turn, and river. Her checking pattern strongly indicates a weak or marginal hand rather than a premium holding like AK or KK. A 1/3 pot bet of 450 extracts value from worse Ace holdings and missed hands while remaining appropriately sized relative to my 5200-chip stack. This is a profitable spot to value bet given the opponent's passive action and board texture.

Claude limp-called preflop, c‑bet a small amount on the A♠T♣4♠ flop, checked the K♦ on the turn and now makes a small river stab of 450 into a 1,750 pot. That line represents a wide range: many A‑x, some T‑x and K‑x value hands plus a fair number of missed hands/bluffs. I hold 7♠7♦ — behind most A/K/T combos but ahead of lots of his marginal hands and bluffs. Calling 450 here gives me roughly ~20% pot odds (and the cost is tiny relative to my 24k stack), and my pair has sufficient showdown value versus his mixed river range. Raising would be a thin, high-variance play with little fold equity against his value combos, and folding surrenders too much equity. I’ll call and take it to showdown.

2♣8♦ is one of the weakest possible holdings — very poor equity and terrible post‑flop playability even though I'm on the button. I could attempt a light steal, but both blinds are deep (Flash 9k, Claude 6.85k) and have shown they will defend and raise; a min‑raise to 200 will be called or 3‑bet enough to put me in bad spots. Calling 100 to see a flop is cheap but I am unlikely to realize enough equity or extract value from worse hands. Best long‑term play is to conserve my large stack and fold this trash hand preflop.

I am in the Small Blind with Jd9d, a solid suited connector hand in a 3-handed game. Minni, who has been aggressive pre-flop, has folded from the button. This leaves only Claude in the Big Blind. Given the folded button, I want to take control of the pot and apply pressure on the Big Blind. J9 suited plays well post-flop and has a significant equity advantage over a random range. I will raise to 300 to attempt to win the blinds immediately or play a pot in position if called.

This is 6-3 offsuit, the weakest hand in poker. While Flash's raise from the small blind could be relatively wide in a heads-up scenario, my hand has virtually no equity against any reasonable range. 6-3o is a clear fold from the big blind regardless of pot odds - it's simply too weak to play profitably. I maintain my disciplined approach of only playing hands with reasonable equity or strong positional leverage. With 6850 chips and 150 hands remaining, there's no need to take -EV spots with trash hands.

I am on the Button with Trey of Hearts and Four of Diamonds, which is a very weak starting hand. While the Button is an advantageous position for stealing blinds in a three-handed game, 3-4 offsuit has extremely poor playability and equity. Given Minni's large stack and active defending style (frequent 3-bets), attempting a steal with this specific hand is unnecessary. I will fold and wait for a more suitable hand to exploit my position.

Heads-up small blind with QJ - a solid hand. Only 50 to call into a 150 pot provides excellent pot odds (3:1). QJ is well above the threshold to defend the small blind heads-up. Will see the flop and evaluate from there.

Holding 4♥6♣ in the big blind vs a limp from the small blind is a clear fold-to-raise, check-to-see-flop spot. The hand has very little showdown value and plays poorly out of position, so I don’t want to inflate the pot preflop or try to bluff-raise. I’ll check and see a cheap flop. If Claude bets a normal continuation bet I’ll fold unless I pick up a pair or strong draw or the price is very favorable; if he checks back I’ll re-evaluate the flop for any real equity or a safe fold. Preserve stack and wait for better opportunities.

Heads-up flop with QJ on 8h-5h-9h. I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs) plus Q-high. Minni checked pre-flop suggesting a weak hand. I should take the initiative with a standard flop bet of 100, roughly 50% of the 200 pot. This bet accomplishes three goals: wins the pot if Minni folds, builds value when I have equity with my draw, and maintains control. The hand has reasonable equity (~30-35%) and betting is more profitable than checking in this position.

Claude limped preflop and now made a small c-bet (100 into a 300 pot). With 4♥6♣ I have one heart on a three-hearts flop — that's a 9-card flush draw — plus a few straight outs (the four 7s, with 7♥ overlap). Unique outs ≈ 12, so my equity to the river is roughly ~45%. Calling 100 to contest a ~400 pot (~25% break-even) is +EV. I'm out of position, so I don't want to inflate the pot with a semi-bluff raise; raising risks getting called by better hearts or pairs and makes future decisions harder. Plan: call cheaply to realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn — continue aggressively if I hit, fold to heavy action if I miss and face large pressure.

Missed my straight draw on the turn with 2c. Currently holding just Q-high with no additional outs. Checking controls pot size and gives me the option to see the river cheaply while maintaining the ability to evaluate river action. Aggressive betting would build a pot with a marginal hand that's unlikely to be best at showdown.

Claude limped preflop, c‑bet a tiny 100 into 300 on the flop and I called with 4♥6♣ to realize my draw. Turn (2♣) bricks — I still have 12 clean outs (9 hearts + 3 non‑heart 7s). With one card to come that’s 12/46 ≈ 26% to hit the river. Pot is 400 and Claude checked to me; minimum bet here is 100. Betting now would get called a lot and, while a 100 bet called is not catastrophically unprofitable in isolation, it inflates the pot when I only have ~26% equity and exposes me to a check‑raise or difficult river decisions OOP. I prefer pot control and to realize equity for free. Plan: check the turn, see the river card and then decide — call reasonable river bluffs/small bets if I hit my heart, fold to large polarized pressure if I miss. This minimizes risk and keeps future play exploitable given my stack advantage.

On the river with QJ on a board of 8h-5h-9h-2c-3s, I hold Queen-high. Minni checked, indicating weakness. This is a classic situation for a river check-down. My hand is too weak to bet for value - Queen-high only wins against hands like 7x, 6x, or complete air. Minni's check-turn check-river line suggests she likely has at best a marginal hand (possibly 8x, 9x, or a missed draw). Betting Queen-high on the river is generally unprofitable - either she folds hands worse than mine (making it a wasted bet) or she calls with better. By checking, I reach showdown efficiently and don't bloat the pot with a marginal hand. This allows me to see her cards at minimal cost. Against her likely weak hand and given the heads-up nature of the matchup, checking is the most profitable play.

I missed my river — 3♠ is a brick. On the flop I had a flush/straight draw (9 hearts + 3 non-heart 7s = 12 unique outs ≈ 26% to the river) and I correctly called to realize equity. Now on the river I have no pair and essentially zero showdown value. The pot is 400 and the minimum bet is 100; a bluff requires >100/(400+100)=20% fold equity to break even. Claude limped preflop, made a tiny flop bet and then checked both turn and river — a line consistent with either weak made hands or missed draws, and it also suggests he is willing to call small bets. Given that, my fold equity is likely thin and a 100 bluff would be called too often to be +EV. I’ll preserve my stack and avoid a thin bluff: check and fold to any river bet.

Folding J-6o from the button. While position is favorable, the hand is too weak to play profitably, especially out of position postflop against two opponents. Stack management and hand selection are prioritized over positional advantage with marginal holdings.

I’m in the small blind with Kc2c — a suited King but a very weak kicker. Pot is 150 and I need 50 to complete to 100 (min-raise to 200 available). Versus a single big blind I can play this hand, but raising OOP with K2s is suboptimal: it often gets called and creates a bigger pot where my hand plays poorly postflop. Calling 50 gives excellent pot odds (~4:1) to see a flop, preserves my deep stack, and keeps my range wide. If I hit the flop I can apply pressure; if I miss I can fold to heavy action. Against Flash, who defends fairly widely, a small raise will usually be called — so I prefer to complete and evaluate on the flop.

I am in the big blind with Qh 7h. Claude folded and Minni completed the small blind. The pot is 200 and it is checked to me. My hand is suited and has decent high-card value for a 3-way/blind-vs-blind battle. I will check to see a flop for free, as my hand is strong enough to play post-flop but not necessarily strong enough to justify a raise here and potentially face a 3-bet from Minni, who has a massive stack.

Flop: 3c Jd 5h. Holding Kc2c I have only king-high with a weak kicker and a backdoor club flush possibility. I missed the flop — my equity to hit a king by the river is ~12.5% and the two-card club backdoor is ~4%. I was the preflop caller, so my range is weak and OOP I should control the pot size. Flash checked preflop which indicates a fairly wide range that will often call a c-bet, so betting here as a bluff is thin and will frequently leave me in tough spots if called. Checking preserves chips, lets me realize my modest equity, and keeps options to call small bets on the turn or concede to large aggression. Plan: check and reevaluate on the turn (call small bets, fold to large ones; look to take the pot if I pick up a king or club).

In a limped pot blind-vs-blind, ranges are wide. The flop of Jd 5h 3c is relatively dry. Minni checked, and while I have two overcards to the lower cards, I only have Queen-high. Checking behind allows me to realize my equity and potentially catch a pair on the turn without risking a check-raise from a large-stacked opponent.

Turn card 7s leaves me with nothing — Kc2c is still just king-high with only a remote backdoor flush (~4%). Flash checked both flop and turn, which can indicate weakness but Flash has a history of both aggression and passive checking; many of his lines are calling with Jx, small pairs, or broadway overcards that will call a bluff. I’m out of position and don’t want to build a bigger pot with a very marginal hand where I’ll be difficult to play if called or raised. Pot is only 200 and my stack is deep, so the lowest-variance, highest-expected-value line is to check and keep the pot small, see the river for free, and decide based on the river card and any action — call small river bets, fold to large ones, or consider a targeted bluff on a favorable river. For these reasons I check.

I have turned a pair of sevens in a limped pot against the small blind. The board is relatively dry, and Minni has checked twice. My seven is likely the best hand currently, as most Jacks would have bet the flop or turn to protect against draws. I will place a small value bet of 100 to get called by Ace-high, smaller pairs, or gutshot draws, and to protect my hand from overcards on the river.

Flop and turn missed — Kc2c has no made hand and the turn 7s kills the backdoor club possibility. Flash checked the flop then made a small turn probe (100 into a ~300 pot). I have 6 outs (three Ks, three 2s) to pair by the river (~13% equity). Calling 100 to win ~400 after my call (~4:1 pot odds) is slightly short of the ~20% break-even if I were drawing strictly, but Flash’s tiny turn bet line often contains weak pairs or probes and not just strong value hands. Raising OOP as a pure bluff is thin and will open me to larger, more expensive decisions; folding throws away cheap equity. I stick to my earlier plan to “call small bets, fold to large ones”: call the turn to realize equity and see the river, then fold to a large river bet if I miss or proceed only if I pair/upside on the river.

Board (3c Jd 5h 7s Ts) and my Kc2c: I have no pair and my flush draw never completed. I checked flop and called a small 100 probe on the turn, so my line isn’t representing strong value — bluffing OOP here would be thin and likely to be called by any Ten, Jack, or pair. Pot is only 400 and I want to avoid bloating the pot with a worthless hand; preserving stack equity for better spots is the higher EV play. I’ll check to see if Flash checks back (saving chips) and plan to fold to any river bet — calling is a losing proposition with just K-high.

On the river, the board is Jd 5h 3c 7s Ts. I have a pair of sevens (Qh 7h). In a limped pot against the Small Blind (Minni), my hand has significant showdown value. I bet the turn for value when the 7s arrived, and Minni called. The river Ts is a relatively safe card, though it completes some gutshots (like 89, though unlikely to have called the turn). Minni checked the river. While I could bet for thin value, checking back ensures I reach showdown with a hand that likely wins against her range of missed draws or smaller pairs, without risking a check-raise from the chip leader.

I'm on the button with A♣4♦ facing only the blinds (pot 150, to call 100). This is a standard spot to open: good positional advantage and an ace-blocker makes A4o raisable. I want to steal the blinds / isolate and avoid a cheap three-way flop, so I'll open to a standard 3x BB sizing. If I face a large 3-bet I'll fold A4o; against a small 3-bet I may call and use position post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Nine offsuit. Minni has opened for 300 (3bb) from the Button. While J9o is a decent hand in short-handed play, it performs poorly when played out of position against a standard 3x raise. Calling would put me in a difficult spot for the rest of the hand, and 3-betting it as a bluff is unnecessary given my stack size and the aggressive tendencies of my opponents. Therefore, the most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

K9o in BB vs. button raise deserves a call with favorable pot odds (~31% needed, ~35-40% equity). Stack depth is manageable, and seeing the flop is reasonable here. Minni's aggressive tendencies suggest a wider raising range.

K9o on a 6-8-4 flop is marginal. I have a gutshot (any 7) and an overcard, but no made hand. Out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, checking is prudent. This allows me to keep pot size manageable while maintaining flexibility for the turn card. The board is dry and unlikely to have favored Minni's typical button range heavily. I'll see what Minni does and evaluate on the turn.

I raised preflop and have A♣4♦ on a 6♠-8♣-4♠ flop. I’ve hit a vulnerable but real pair (middle/bottom pair with the ace kicker) and I have positional advantage. Claude’s check from the big blind is consistent with a wide calling range (overcards, small pairs, suited connectors, some spade combos). The board is relatively dry aside from two spades (so only hands with two spades have immediate flush draws), so a continuation bet will fold many unpaired hands, get value from worse pairs/draws, and protect my hand. I’ll make a standard c-bet around half the pot to balance value and fold equity (keeps weaker hands honest and charges the few draws). If Claude flats I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if he raises big I’ll likely fold as my pair is vulnerable.

K9o on a dry 6-8-4 board versus Minni's flop raise. I need approximately 26% equity to call (350 into 1,000 pot), and my K9 has stronger equity than that against Minni's typical button-raise range (~35-40%). I have multiple outs: any King (3 outs), any 9 (3 outs), any 7 for a gutshot straight (3 outs), plus backdoor possibilities. The board texture is dry and not particularly heavy in Minni's favor. Calling is mathematically sound here with favorable pot odds. Out-of-position, I want to keep the pot manageable while maintaining flexibility on the turn. If I hit my outs or catch a favorable turn card, I'll have more information for future decisions. Folding would give up too much equity in a favorable spot.

Check on the turn with K9o against a three-spade board. Without a spade in my hand, the 5♠ turn is problematic and weakens my equity significantly. I'm out of position against an aggressive button raiser, so checking allows me to see Minni's turn action before committing more chips. My K and 9 have outs (top pair, bottom pair, or straight draws), but the spade-heavy texture makes them vulnerable. Checking preserves my stack, controls pot growth, and allows me to evaluate Minni's betting patterns before making a decision on the river.

Check. I have A♣4♦ — a vulnerable bottom pair with the ace kicker after c-betting the flop and getting called. The turn (5♠) brings a third spade (board: 6♠‑8♣‑4♠‑5♠), completing many of Claude’s flop‑calling combos (spade flushes) and significantly reducing my equity. Betting here buys little fold equity (spade combos won't fold and a bet can get raised off), so the prudent, EV‑maximizing play is pot control: check and keep the pot manageable. Plan: if Claude checks back I’ll take a free river and likely check again; if he bets the river I’ll call small value/bluff bets as a bluff‑catcher but fold to large, polarized value bets (i.e. heavy sizing consistent with a made flush).

K9 on a spade-heavy board (6-8-4-5-Q with 4 spades showing) is extremely weak - just King-high. Minni's check on the turn suggests they also missed equity or don't want to commit further. With no made hand and poor showdown value, checking to showdown is the only sound option. If Minni bets, I would fold, but checking preserves the small chance I win at showdown if Minni also has air.

Board pairing and turn brought heavy spade texture: 6♠‑8♣‑4♠ then 5♠ and Q♠ on the river (four spades). I hold A♣4♦ — bottom pair with a good kicker but no spade. I c‑bet the flop, got a call, checked the turn for pot control when the spade draw completed, and Claude checked the river. With four spades on board my hand is very vulnerable to any single‑spade holding; Claude’s flop‑call line contains many hands that now beat me (6x/8x, Qx, and spade flushes). Betting the river would get called or raised by most of the hands that beat me and only fold out marginal holdings — too thin and too risky. The prudent, +EV play is to check and take showdown. If Claude had bet a small sizing I’d call as a bluff‑catcher; I’d fold to a large, polarized river shove.

I have Ace-Jack offsuit (AdJs) on the Button. This is a very strong hand for three-handed play. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand. Minni (Big Blind) has been aggressive throughout the game, and Claude (Small Blind) has been more selective. Raising here is the standard play with a premium hand in position.

Raising with ATs from small blind after Flash's button raise. This hand is too strong to just call - I have broadway cards with flush draw potential in spades. A raise to 800 applies pressure, takes initiative, and builds the pot with a premium hand. Flash's shorter stack (9,000) makes them vulnerable to aggression, and my hand plays well against their likely wide button raising range. This also allows me to define my hand strength and avoid playing out of position on later streets.

Facing a small‑blind 3‑bet to 800 after a 300 button open. Pot is 1200 and I must call 700 (total 800) or 4‑bet to at least 1300. I hold K♦7♣ — a weak, frequently dominated hand with poor playability out of position against a 3‑bettor and the original opener. Calling requires ~36% equity on a relatively small investment but likely leaves me OOP vs a polarized 3‑bet range and possibly multi‑way. 4‑betting as a bluff is too loose here given Claude’s sizing and ~52bb effective stack; I don’t have enough equity or fold equity to justify building a pot. Preserve the deep stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am holding AdJs on the Button. I opened to 300, and Claude (Small Blind) 3-bet to 800. This is a relatively small 3-bet, requiring only 500 more to call into a pot that will be 1700. In a three-handed game, AJ is well ahead of a standard 3-betting range from the Small Blind, but since Claude has been somewhat selective and conservative (see Hands #46 and #55 where he 3-bet or 4-bet and won pre-flop), I don't want to 4-bet and potentially blow him off worse hands or get jammed on by better ones. I will call to see a flop in position, utilizing my positional advantage and the strength of my hand against his range.

On the flop (2d 4d Kc), I'm holding ATs with a strong spade flush draw and ace-high. This is a premium draw with significant equity. I should continue the aggression I established pre-flop by betting. Key factors: (1) I was the aggressor pre-flop, so a bet maintains my aggressive narrative and makes it harder for Flash to play their hand profitably; (2) Flash is short-stacked (8,500), making them vulnerable to pressure; (3) My hand has multiple ways to win - the flush draw itself is strong, and any spade improves me significantly; (4) A bet of 1,100 (about 65% of the current pot) applies reasonable pressure while keeping my stack management healthy and avoiding getting overcommitted. This sizing allows Flash to either fold weaker hands or call with marginal holdings where I have strong equity. Checking would cede control and give Flash a chance to improve or take the pot cheaply with a bet.

On a King-high board (Kc 4d 2d) following a pre-flop 3-bet from Claude, my Ace-Jack offsuit is in a difficult position. Claude has led out with a large continuation bet of 1100 into a 1700 pot (approximately 65% of the pot). Given Claude's selective and aggressive history in 3-bet pots (Hands #46, #47, #55, and #65), his range is likely weighted towards strong hands like AK, KQ, or overpairs like AA/QQ. While I hold the Ace of Diamonds, which provides a backdoor nut flush draw and potentially three outs to an Ace, an Ace on the turn might still leave me behind AK. With Claude having a relatively short stack remaining (4100), calling here likely commits me to a very large pot with only Jack-high. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack against a strong range.

I'm holding pocket sevens on the button, which is a solid hand for raising from this position. With blinds at 50/100, a standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate. 

Position analysis: I'm in the best position at the table (button), and both opponents still have chips to work with. From the hand history, I can see that Minni is highly aggressive with a large stack (24550), while Flash has a medium stack similar to mine (8400).

Hand strength: Pocket sevens is a made hand that beats most unpaired hands and has decent equity. It's strong enough to apply pressure preflop from the button, and I can extract value or win the pot immediately with a well-timed raise.

This raise accomplishes several goals:
1. Takes down the 150 chips in the pot without immediate resistance
2. Puts pressure on the blinds to fold weaker holdings
3. Demonstrates strength and helps balance my button range
4. Gives me fold equity, which is important given my medium stack size

My stack of 6900 is healthy enough to make this play profitable.

I’m in the small blind with T9o facing a button open to 300 (pot 450, I must call 250 more = 300 total). T9o is a playable defender but not a 3-bet hand out of position — the min-raise to 500 is too small to generate fold equity and a larger 3-bet would risk too much with an offsuit connector. With deep stacks and a wide button opening range, calling keeps the pot controlled, realizes equity (straights/two-pair/backdoor) and preserves maneuverability postflop. Plan: call to see a flop; continue with pairs, strong draws or favorable textures, fold to heavy, symmetrical aggression on unhelpful boards and avoid bloating the pot OOP with a marginal hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 7s. Claude (Button) raised to 300 and Minni (Small Blind) called. While I am getting decent pot odds (200 to win a total pot of 900), 97-offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly multi-way and out of position. I would be playing the rest of the hand out of position against Claude, and the hand lacks the connectivity or high-card strength to justify a call. I will preserve my stack for a better spot.

I have top pair (T♠9♣) on a T-5-3 (two diamonds) board and I'm out of position to the preflop raiser. Pot ~700, effective stacks ~6600 -> SPR ≈ 9. This is a decent but non-nutted holding: many hands in Claude's button range have better kickers or will continue aggressively. The board is relatively dry (only backdoor diamond/straight possibilities), so my priority is pot control and avoiding getting bloated OOP with a vulnerable kicker. I’ll check to keep worse tens and bluffs in, and to see how Claude chooses to continue. Plan after checking: call a standard c-bet (~1/3–1/2 pot, ~200–350) and re-evaluate on the turn; fold to strong flop raises or heavy pressure that commit a large portion of stacks; if Claude checks back I can take a stab or value-bet safe turn cards. Checking preserves maneuverability and maximizes expected value with this medium-strength hand.

Continuation bet on the flop with pocket sevens. Minni's check indicates weakness, and my position advantage allows me to apply pressure. The bet sizing of 450 is appropriate for the pot size (700), representing about 64% of the pot - a standard aggression level for a button raiser on a dry flop. This bet accomplishes multiple goals: wins the pot immediately against weak holdings, extracts value from draws, and maintains my range credibility as a button aggressor. My remaining stack of 6150 is sufficient to follow through on future streets if needed.

I have top pair (T♠9♣) on T‑5‑3 with two diamonds on board. I checked as planned to keep the pot under control and allow worse tens and bluffs to continue. Claude (BTN) bets 450 into the ~700 pot (pot now 1,150) — a somewhat sizable c‑bet but not polarizing. Against his wide button opening/c-bet range this is a clear call: my hand is good enough to realize equity and extract value from worse tens, JTx type hands and some weaker one‑pair/semi‑bluff hands, while folding to very large turn aggression if he shows heavy strength. Raising OOP with T9o here is thin and will often bloat the pot or get called by better tens/draws; folding is too weak given top pair and the likelihood he has many worse hands and draws. Plan: call 450, re-evaluate on the turn — continue vs small/standard bets, consider check‑back or small value bet if he checks, and fold to large commits or shove without improvement.

Board: T♠ 5♥ 3♦ 3♣; I hold T♠9♣ — top pair with a medium kicker, out of position. Pot is 1,600 and Claude's opening/c-bet range is wide; the turn paired the board making sets/full-house combos possible but still rare. My priority is pot control: betting OOP with a vulnerable kicker bloats the pot and often gets called by better tens or folds out worse (reducing value). I’ll check to keep worse tens and bluffs in, see how Claude reacts, and call small-to-medium bets but fold to large commit-sized aggression. If he checks back I can re-evaluate a small river value-bet on safe runouts.

Continuing my aggressive line with a turn bet maintains pressure on Minni's passive range. She's checked twice, suggesting she either has a weak/marginal hand or is checking down with hands that don't connect with this board. My 77 has decent equity against her likely holding distribution. The bet sizing of 500 into the 1600 pot (31% pot bet) is appropriately sized to extract value while not overcommitting. This is consistent with my established aggressive line from preflop and flop, and helps me win the pot without going to showdown or giving free cards to potential drawing hands.

Call 500. I have top pair (T9) with a medium kicker out of position. Claude’s line (button open, a sizable flop c-bet then a small turn continuation of 500 into ~1,600) is wide and non‑polar — that small turn bet is consistent with worse tens, floats and bluffs. Raising OOP here is thin (will bloat the pot and get called by better tens/draws) and folding is too weak. Calling preserves pot control, extracts value from worse tens and many semi‑bluffs, and lets me re‑evaluate on the river: call small/medium river bets, consider a small value probe if he checks, but be ready to fold to large commit‑sized aggression or a shove showing clear strength.

Board is 5♥ 3♦ T♦ 3♣ 2♣ and I hold T♠9♣ — top pair with a medium kicker, out of position. Claude opened wide from the button, c-bet the flop and barreled a small turn; those sizes and the river check point to a fairly wide, non-polarized range (missed diamonds, worse tens, single pairs, small pockets) rather than a lot of slow-played monsters. A thin value bet here will get called by worse tens, underpairs (55, 44, etc.), and many busted draws while keeping the size small vs the few combos that beat me (sets/full houses). I’ll probe for value with a ~1/3–1/2 pot bet to extract from these hands while preserving most of my stack and keeping fold equity. If Claude check-raises I’ll give up the hand (he’s showing strong value). If he calls I’m happy to show down; if he folds I’ve earned extra EV without bloating the pot.

Calling the 900 river raise. Pot odds are 3500:900 (3.89:1), requiring ~20% equity. My pocket sevens has roughly 30-35% equity against Minni's likely range, which includes many bluffs and weak pairs given her passive checking line through flop and turn. The math is favorable and folding would sacrifice too much value. My hand beats most bluffs and many mediocre holdings while still losing to the strongest hands, making a call optimally balanced.

8♠3♠ is a very weak holding but I'm on the button with both blinds to act — high positional value and good opportunity to steal. Calling 100 gives good pot odds but often leads to a multiway pot where this hand has poor playability and little showdown equity. The table standard open-size is ~300; a 3x open preserves fold equity and keeps the initiative while not bloating the pot with a marginal hand. Flash (SB) and Claude (BB) can call, but Claude is relatively short-stacked which reduces his light-call/3-bet frequency. Plan: open to 300 to pick up the blinds or play postflop in position; fold to strong 3-bets unless getting good odds or facing a short-stack shove.
With 9d 6h in the Small Blind against a Button raise from an aggressive player like Minni, this hand is too weak to call or 3-bet. Folding is the standard play to preserve the stack for better spots.

Calling A7o from Big Blind in heads-up play against Minni's button raise. Getting 3.25:1 pot odds with a hand that has decent playability - ace-high, wheel straight potential, and good 2-card combo value. Minni has been playing wide from late position throughout the session, making my call justified. Stack size (4,650) allows flexibility while preserving chips for quality spots. Position is out, but heads-up dynamics and pot odds make this a solid call to see a flop.

Strong flush draw (9 outs) and ace-high on 3h-8h-Qc. Betting takes initiative against aggressive Minni, wins the pot immediately with fold equity, and keeps the hand in good shape if called. With a significant chip disadvantage, I should play aggressively with my draw strength to accumulate chips when possible. Flop texture favors aggressive play.

I have 8♠3♠ on a 3♥8♥Qc flop — two pair (eights and threes). Claude called preflop and now makes a small flop bet (350 into a 1000 pot), which signals a wide, often draw-heavy or marginal-value range. I’m ahead of most of that range (single-pair hands, heart draws, floats) while only losing to sets, Q8/Q3 type two-pair combos, or rare trips — so my goal is to extract value and deny free cards to heart draws. Raising to the minimum available (to 700) charges draws and gets value from worse pairs/overcards without bloating the pot unnecessarily against hands that currently beat me. For reference, a raise to 700 forces Claude to call an extra 350 to win roughly a 2050 chip pot (break-even equity ≈17%), which is a tough price for many marginal holdings. If he jams or 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (he’s representing a very strong hand); if he calls I’ll proceed to extract value on later streets while remaining cautious of completed hearts or full-house possibilities.

Calling with a premium draw (flush + overcards + wheel straight potential) that has approximately 45-50% equity. Pot odds of 4.86:1 strongly favor calling with this equity. Short-stacked situation requires building chips with strong equity hands. Preserving remaining 3,750 chips maintains tournament flexibility while chasing best odds to win the pot.

Turn: Betting with a strong flush draw (9 outs) and ace-high overcards. Board runout shows Minni checked the turn, suggesting weakness or a cautious approach. With 3,750 chip stack, I need to play aggressively with strong equity hands. A 1,100 chip bet (roughly half-pot) applies pressure, has solid fold equity, and builds the pot when I'm in good shape. This is a semi-bluff that gains value from better hands folding and from equity when called. Short-stack strategy demands playing strong draws actively rather than giving free cards.

I have two pair (8s and 3s) on 3♥ 8♥ Q♣ T♦ — a very strong hand versus a typical continuing range. Claude’s line (flop c-bet 350, called my raise to 700, then a small turn bet of 1,100 into a ~3,150 pot) looks like a fairly wide/marginal value/protection range (single pairs, heart draws, small pockets) rather than a huge locked-in monster. Calling 1,100 here gives me ~26% break-even equity (1,100 to win ~4,250) — my two pair has well better equity than that vs his likely range and also denies free cards to heart draws. Raising to the minimum (2,200) would often fold out the worse hands I want value from and risks inducing an all‑in decision from a short-stacked Claude; folding is too nitty with two pair. Plan: call 1,100, see the river, and re-evaluate — if he jams I’ll call given I’m ahead of most of his range; if river is a scary heart/Q/8 I’ll proceed cautiously, otherwise extract value.

All-in with the nuts (ace-high flush). The river brought K♥, completing my flush draw from the flop. This is the absolute best possible hand on this board - no straight flush is possible. Minni has shown aggressive calling tendencies throughout (called flop raise and turn bet). With only 2,650 chips left and the nuts, going all-in for value maximizes EV. Expected to get called by weaker flushes, pairs, two pair, or potential bluffs from Minni.

I have 8♠3♠ for two pair (8s & 3s) on 3♥ 8♥ Q♣ T♦ K♥. Pot is 6,900 and Claude has shoved all‑in for 2,650 (he’s now at 0), so calling 2,650 wins a final pot of 9,550 — break‑even equity ≈ 2,650/9,550 ≈ 27.7%. Claude’s line (call preflop, small flop c‑bet called my raise, small turn bet, river shove) is consistent with a wide continuing range: single pairs, missed heart draws, some medium pockets, and a few strong hands that beat me (straights like J9, KQ turning two‑pair on the river, or rare sets). The number of combos that actually beat my two pair is limited, while many of his hands are worse or bluffs. Given the cheap call relative to pot and my hand strength vs his range, this is +EV — raising isn’t an option (he’s all‑in), folding gives up too much value. Call 2,650.

I am on the button with Pocket Tens (TT), a premium starting hand in a three-handed game. My goal is to raise for value and to take control of the pot early. Standard opening size at this table has been 300, which builds the pot effectively while putting pressure on the blinds.

Folding J9o from the small blind. While the pot odds are decent at 1.8:1, the hand is marginal and I'll be out of position post-flop. Additionally, Minni in the big blind hasn't acted yet and could re-raise, making the situation worse. With a solid 9500 chip stack and 65 hands remaining, I should be selective with weak holdings from early position. Better spots will come.

Fold. Q4o is a very weak, easily‑dominated hand and I’m out of position to the button. The pot is 450 and calling 200 would make the pot 650, so I’d need ~31% equity to break even — Q4o has poor equity vs a BTN open and little post‑flop playability or blocking value to justify a 3‑bet bluff. With deep effective stacks I should preserve chips for better spots and defend wider from the BB with suited/connective or stronger Qx hands. Fold and wait for position or a stronger hand.

7-2 is the worst hand in poker with minimal equity. Although I have positional advantage on the button, this doesn't justify playing trash. Position leverage works best with better hands. Folding preserves my stack and maintains disciplined play toward the later stages of the tournament.

I’m in the small blind with 5♥8♠. The pot is 150 and calling only costs 50, but I prefer to take the initiative heads-up vs the BB. A standard 2x BB open to 200 gives fold equity to pick up the blinds and lets me c-bet favorable flops; 5-8o has some backdoor equity and is acceptable as part of a SB opening range. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold this marginal holding; I’ll continue aggressively on safe flops and fold to heavy resistance. Raise to 200 to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 5c. Minni, who is in the Small Blind and has a massive stack, has raised to 200 (a min-raise). My hand is very weak and offsuit. While I only need to call 100 more to see a flop, 9-5 offsuit plays very poorly post-flop, especially against a player as active as Minni. There is no need to defend this hand and get into a potentially difficult situation out of position. I will fold.

3-handed I should be opening a wide range from the button. 6♣7♠ is a playable, connected hand with decent postflop playability and good equity vs blind ranges. Both blinds have shown frequent preflop opens/calls, so I want to take the initiative and apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable. A standard 3xBB open (to 300 total) is the right balance — it gives fold equity, avoids conceding free flops, and keeps SPR reasonable if I get called. If I face a sizable 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate (fold to large 3-bets, consider calling smaller squeezes in position), but preflop the +EV play is to raise to 300.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s 5h, which is a very weak starting hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. While I am getting a decent price to call (250 more to win a total pot of 450), this hand plays extremely poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Additionally, Claude is still to act in the Big Blind and could potentially squeeze. There is no strategic reason to defend such a weak hand in this spot; folding is the standard and most profitable play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Folding 2♥8♥ from the big blind. This is one of the weakest hands in poker with virtually no playability. While the pot odds appear marginal (450:200 = 2.25:1), hand equity with 2-8 is extremely poor, especially out of position against Minni's button raise. Additionally, even hitting the flop would likely leave me in a dominated situation with terrible kicker problems. It's not worth depleting my stack with such a weak hand, particularly when I'm out of position post-flop. Better to fold and preserve chips for stronger opportunities.

I am on the button with Jack-Nine suited (Jd 9d), which is a very strong hand to open in a 3-handed game. My goal is to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds. A standard raise to 300 is appropriate here to build the pot while maintaining position.

Folding Q8 offsuit from the small blind facing a button raise. The hand is marginal with only ~36% equity against Flash's wide range, and the out-of-position postflop disadvantage makes this unprofitable. Position is the worst in poker, and Q8o doesn't justify playing 250 chips to 450 in pot when I'll be out of position and unable to leverage any advantage. Preserving chips for better spots is optimal.

Facing a BTN open to 300 from Flash with me in the big blind holding 8♦5♠ (offsuit). Pot is 450 and it costs 200 to call (break-even equity ≈ 200/(450+200)=30.8%). 8‑5o is a very weak, poorly-connected offsuit hand that will be dominated by most of the button’s continuing range and has poor playability out of position. With effective stacks (~80BB) a 3‑bet bluff would require better blockers/playability — this hand is not a good candidate. Flat‑calling OOP will put me in tough spots facing c‑bets and multi-street pressure. I’ll preserve my stack and fold to this button open.

Premium hand (KK) from button position. Standard aggressive play is to raise 3x the big blind to 300 total. This builds the pot, establishes position advantage, and extracts value from weaker hands. Pocket Kings is one of the strongest starting hands and deserves aggression in this favorable spot.

I have Q♠T♥ in the small blind facing a standard 3BB button open to 300. This hand is too good to fold but marginal for an isolation 3‑bet out of position — 3‑betting here would bloat the pot OOP or invite a cold 4‑bet/call from the BB. Calling 250 (300 total) keeps the pot controlled, gets favorable immediate pot odds to see a flop, and preserves stack flexibility with deep effective stacks. Plan: call to see the flop, play straightforwardly — c‑bet for value/initiative heads‑up if I connect, check and pot‑control or fold to heavy action if I miss and face aggression in a multiway pot.

I am in the big blind with T2o. Claude has raised to 300 from the button and Minni called from the small blind. The pot is currently 700 and it costs me 200 more to call. T2o is a very weak hand, even with favorable pot odds in a multi-way situation. Looking at the previous hands, I've been playing relatively tight and selective. Calling here puts me out of position against two opponents with a hand that rarely dominates and often finds itself in difficult spots post-flop. Given the stack depths and the hand strength, folding is the most prudent play to conserve chips for better spots.

I missed the flop with Q♠T♥ on A♥ 8♣ 6♦ — no pair and no meaningful draw. Claude opened preflop, so his continuation range contains many A‑x and strong pairs; bluffing OOP here has limited fold equity and just inflates a pot I don't want to play big out of position with a weak holding. With deep stacks I prefer pot control: check to see the turn and reassess. If Claude bets very small (≈≤1/3 pot) I may call to float, but I will fold to a sizable c‑bet given my poor equity.

I'm in excellent position on the button with pocket kings after Minni checked the flop. While the Ace on the board is concerning, Minni's check indicates weakness. I should make a continuation bet to build the pot and extract value from her weaker hands (pairs, weak Aces, missed hands). A bet of 450 (approximately 2/3 of the 700 pot) is standard here - aggressive enough to charge drawing hands but not so large that I commit too heavily if she raises and indicates an Ace. This maintains my aggression, capitalizes on position, and allows me to reassess on the turn if she raises.

I called preflop to keep the pot controlled and preserve stack flexibility. The flop (A♥ 8♣ 6♦) misses Q♠T♥, so I checked as planned. Claude's c-bet of 450 into the ~1150 pot is relatively small (about 39% of the pre‑bet pot; I only need ~28% equity to continue), and with deep stacks the 450 is a cheap price to try a turn float. Raising OOP would bloat the pot with a weak hand and get called or 4‑bet by worse/equal hands, so it's a poor play here. I'll call the 450 to see a turn, look to take the pot away on a blank or a heart/Queen that helps my story, and fold to sizable turn/river aggression if I don’t improve.

Check. I missed — Q♠T♥ on A♥ 8♣ 6♦ 7♣ gives me no pair and no realistic draw (a flush is impossible from here). I called Claude's small flop c-bet as a cheap float, but the turn is a blank for my hand and actually brings a second club (more potential draws I don't have). I'm out of position vs a button opener whose range contains many A-x, pairs and strong broadways, so leading/raising here would bloat the pot with a weak holding and be poor risk/reward. I’ll pot-control: check and see how Claude reacts. If he fires a smallish turn bet (thin barrel ≤ ~1/3 pot) I may call to float to the river; I will fold to sizeable turn aggression (commitment-sized bet ≳1/2 pot) since my showdown equity is low. If he checks back I get to a cheap river and can decide whether a Q/T or a bluff on a favorable river is profitable.

I hold pocket Kings on the button, a premium hand that is very strong on this Ah-8c-6d-7c board. Minni has shown significant weakness by checking both the flop and turn, indicating she likely doesn't have a strong Ace (AK, AQ, AJ) or a premium pair. Her range is heavily weighted toward weaker Aces, small pairs, or complete missed hands.

The turn brought a 7, which is a favorable card for my range and doesn't improve most of her likely holdings. With two checks from Minni, I'm in an excellent spot to extract value with another bet. Being in position on the button with a hand this strong gives me significant equity advantage.

A turn bet of 800 (roughly 50% of the 1600 pot) accomplishes several goals: it charges drawing hands, extracts value from weaker holdings, applies reasonable pressure, and is proportional to my continuing aggression. This is standard value-betting with a strong made hand after Minni's weak checking line.

My stack is 8600, so betting 800 leaves me 7800 for potential river decisions - good stack management for a hand of this strength.

Out of position with Q♠T♥ on A♥ 8♣ 6♦ 7♣ — I have no pair and only a gutshot to a 9 (4 outs). Claude is the button opener who c‑bet the flop and now fires a small/medium turn bet of 800 into a 2400 pot (~33%). A pure pot‑odds call would need ~20% equity while my raw chance to hit the straight on the river is only ~9% — so this isn’t a pure draw call. However the turn sizing is thin enough to justify a float: calling keeps the pot controlled, lets me see a cheap river, and gives me opportunities to realize fold equity or extract value if I pair up (Q/T) or hit my 9. I’ll avoid raising OOP with a marginal hand and will fold to any sizable raise or large river bet if I don’t improve. Plan: call the 800 to see the river, re‑evaluate on the river (value bet/push if I improve, fold to heavy action if I don’t).

I called preflop to keep the pot small and floated Claude’s small flop c‑bet. The turn (7♣) was a blank for Q♠T♥ and I called his thin turn bet to see a cheap river. The river (2♦) doesn’t improve me — I have no pair and virtually no showdown value. Claude opened pre, c‑bet the flop and barreled the turn; that line is heavily weighted to A‑x and medium pairs that beat Q‑T. With little fold equity out of position and a modest pot (3,200) versus deep stacks, bluffing here is low EV — he’s shown willingness to fire two streets and is unlikely to fold most of his value/medium hands. I’ll protect my stack and keep the pot controlled: check and fold to any meaningful river bet. If Claude checks back I get a free showdown (and can re‑evaluate if he shows down a weak hand).

River value bet with pocket Kings on Ah-8c-6d-7c-2d. Minni has checked all three streets (flop, turn, river), indicating a weak holding or missed hand. Her triple-check line combined with the blank 2d on the river makes Kings very likely the strongest hand. Betting 600 (about 1/5 of the 3200 pot) extracts value from weaker pairs (88, 66, 77) or weak Aces while staying reasonable with stack management. This continues my aggressive value-betting narrative while being sized appropriately for the strength of my hand and her weak checking line. Her consistent passivity suggests she won't have a strong hand to check-raise with, making this a solid value extraction spot.

Board: A♥ 8♣ 6♦ 7♣ 2♦. I hold Q♠T♥. Claude opened BTN, c-bet flop and barreled the turn — that line is weighted to A-x and medium pairs, but his river sizing is very small (600 into the ~3,200 pot pre‑river). After his 600 the pot is 3,800, so I’m getting ~16% pot‑odds (600/(3,800)) to call. I have no pair and only Q‑high, so this is purely a bluff‑catch; however the price is cheap and his tiny river sizing can include thin value and some missed broadway bluffs. Raising OOP with nothing is a clear leak — it bloats the pot and will be called by worse or folded to by better. Given the small cost, the opponent’s line, and my deep stack, a call to see showdown is the lowest‑risk way to realize any fold equity or catch a river bluff. If Claude raises I will fold. Action: call 600.

Pot is 150 and I face only the blinds (to call 100). Td3h is a marginal hand, but on the button I should be aggressive to pick up the blinds and seize initiative. A standard open to 300 (my usual sizing) gives good fold equity against Flash (SB) and Claude (BB) while keeping the sizing small enough to fold profitably to a meaningful 3-bet. If either player 3-bets big I will release Td3h; if called I can leverage position to c-bet favorable flops or check back dry boards. Limping is passive and lowers my EV with this holding, so I raise to steal.

I am in the Small Blind with 6d7d, a strong suited connector. Minni has raised to 3 levels (300) from the Button. While suited connectors play better when deep-stacked and against multiple opponents, this is a hand with good playability and potential to flop draws or sneaky two-pairs/trips. I need to call 250 more to see a flop. Claude is in the Big Blind and might squeeze, but calling here is generally profitable with this hand's realized equity post-flop, especially given my stack size of 8350.

Pocket fives with 3.5:1 pot odds and position advantage as Big Blind. Three-way pot gives me decent equity. Calling 200 more (total 300 from BB position) is mathematically sound given the pot odds and positional advantage post-flop. Strong fold equity and set-mining potential make this a clear call.

I have flopped bottom pair (Sixes) on a coordinated board of Th-Jd-6h. I am first to act against two opponents, one of whom was the pre-flop aggressor. This board likely connects well with a standard Button opening range (broadway cards, pairs). Betting here wouldn't accomplish much as I'm unlikely to get better hands to fold or worse hands to call for value. I will check to see how the other players react and evaluate if a call is profitable depending on the bet size.

Pocket fives on T-J-6 is a weak holding in a 3-way pot against Minni's pre-flop raise. Checking allows me to set-mine at reasonable odds while controlling the pot size. Flash's check indicates weakness. As Big Blind out of position, checking lets the action develop naturally - Minni can reveal strength with a bet or I can see the turn cheaply. My pair needs improvement to win, and checking provides the best risk/reward profile here without overcommitting chips to a marginal hand.

Board T♥ J♦ 6♥ — I hold T♦3♥: top pair with a very weak kicker. Both opponents checked to me and I was the preflop aggressor, so a continuation bet is the default to take the initiative, get value from worse tens/small pairs/Jx, and charge the few turn/river draw combos. Against two callers I don’t want to overcommit with a marginal kicker, but I need a size that still folds overcards and charges straight/heart backdoors. A half-pot c‑bet (≈450 into 900) is the right balance: it extracts value from worse hands, provides protection, and keeps my options open. Plan: if I take it down I win the pot; if called I’ll reassess on the turn and play cautiously (check more often on scary cards, fold to large raises since my kicker is weak).

I have bottom pair (sixes) with a 7 kicker on a very coordinated board (Th-Jd-6h). Minni, the pre-flop aggressor on the button, has made a half-pot continuation bet. This board texture hits a button opening range very hard, containing many straight draws (KQ, Q9, 98, 97), flush draws, and higher pairs (Jacks, Tens). Being out of position against Minni and having Claude still to act behind me makes this a difficult spot to continue. My hand is extremely vulnerable to almost any turn card (overcards, straight completers, or hearts). Given the multi-way nature of the pot and the high likelihood that I am already behind or will be outdrawn, I will choose to fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Folding is the correct play here despite my previous thoughts about set-mining. My pocket 5s have very limited equity against Minni's flop raise. With no draws (no flush draw, no straight draw), I'm essentially playing for set value only. 

On a T♥-J♦-6♥ flop, my hand is extremely vulnerable. Minni's raise to 450 after both Flash and I checked suggests strength. Even accounting for possible continuation bet bluffs, my equity is likely only around 15-20% against a reasonable pre-flop raiser's range.

The pot odds are 3:1 (needing 25% equity), but I don't have sufficient equity. More critically, I'm out of position facing future betting, which eliminates much of the implied odds benefit from set-mining. The 450 call would be a losing play mathematically. 

With only 5 outs to make a set (at roughly 10% to hit by river), and no way to extract significant value from my opponent if I miss, this is a clear fold. Preserving chips in marginal spots is critical for long-term profitability.

Q9o is a standard opening hand from the button in a 3-handed game. My goal is to steal the blinds or play a pot in position against the small or big blind. Raising to 300 (3x BB) is consistent with my betting patterns in previous hands and applies appropriate pressure.

Fold J7o from Small Blind against Button raise. This is a weak, unconnected hand that plays poorly out of position. Despite the modest 250 chips to call, J7o lacks the equity and positional advantage needed for profitability. Additionally, Big Blind (Minni) still needs to act, creating potential for a 3-bet. Disciplined fold of a marginal hand.

I’m in the big blind with Ah6h facing a button open to 300. Pot is 450 and I need to add 200 (total 300) to call. A6s is a clear defend from the BB: it has nut‑ace potential, backdoor heart/fold equity and good postflop playability heads‑up. 3‑betting here is marginal — this hand doesn’t play well if I face a 4‑bet and a tiny min‑3bet (500) just bloats the pot out of position. I’ll call to see a flop, keep the pot controlled, and proceed aggressively only with top pair/strong hearts or fold to heavy turn/river aggression when I miss.

I have A♥6♥ on a K♣ J♣ 2♦ flop — I only have ace‑high with backdoor heart equity but no pair. The preflop raiser’s range is heavily weighted toward Kx/Jx/broadways and many of those connect with this flop, so barreling out of position with just ace‑high is a losing line. I’ll check to control the pot, gather info, and fold to meaningful pressure. If the button makes a very small c‑bet (~≤1/3 pot) I can consider a call as a float with backdoor hearts and showdown value vs weaker parts of his range; I’ll fold to larger bets and re‑evaluate if I improve (heart or pair) on the turn.

As the pre-flop aggressor, this King-Jack-Deuce board is very favorable for my perceived range, which includes many strong Kings and Jacks. I currently hold Queen-high with a gutshot straight draw (any Ten). Placing a continuation bet here allows me to maintain the initiative and likely fold out many of Minni's hands that missed the flop or are weak draws. A bet of roughly half the pot should be sufficient to apply pressure.

I’ll call 350. I have A♥6♥ on K♣ J♣ 2♦ — only ace‑high (no hearts on board), so this is a defensive float rather than a value raise. Flash’s c‑bet is small (~350 into 1000 ≈ 1/3 pot), so the price is right: I’m getting ~26% pot‑odds to see a turn and his BTN range contains lots of missed broadways and bluffs I can exploit later. Raising out of position with just A‑high is too thin and invites 4‑bet/commitment problems; folding is too passive given the odds and my Ace blocker to a lot of Ax combos. Plan: call to see the turn, look to continue only if I improve (A or a heart) or if he shows clear weakness; fold to significant turn aggression when still unpaired.

I have A♥6♥ on 2♦ K♣ J♣ 5♦ and I missed — no pair and the backdoor heart route is dead after the turn. Flash was the preflop raiser and c‑bet the flop, so his range contains a lot of Kx/Jx and broadway combos that connect with this board. I only have ~6 outs (3 Aces + 3 Sixes) ≈ 13% to improve on the river, so I’m a weak bluff‑catcher. Betting out of position with just ace‑high is a losing line; the right line is to control pot size and fold to real pressure.

Plan: check and give up to significant turn aggression. Mathematically, with the pot ≈1,350 I need a very small bet (roughly ≤~225–250) to justify a call with my ~13% chance to improve; anything larger is +EV to fold. If Flash checks back I’ll re‑evaluate on the river and only continue if I pair the Ace or he shows clear weakness. I will not bluff‑raise or barrel this street without improvement.

I currently hold Queen-high with a gutshot straight draw (a Ten would complete a King-high straight). Minni called my pre-flop raise and my flop continuation bet, indicating she likely has a piece of this board, such as a Jack or a King, or a drawing hand. Given the texture and Minni's tendency to call down in previous hands, a second barrel on the turn is unlikely to force a fold from a better hand. Checking allows me to control the pot size and realize my equity for free, hoping to hit my straight or a favorable card on the river.

I never improved — A♥6♥ is just ace‑high on 2♦ K♣ J♣ 5♦ 9♠ and my backdoor heart route is dead. Flash opened and c‑bet the flop (I called), then checked the turn, which looks like pot‑control with Kx/Jx or he gave up with air. Most of his value combos (Kx, Jx, pairs) will call a river bluff, so my fold equity is thin despite blocking some A‑combos. Pot is only 1,350 and I don’t want to invest more with a pure bluff that’s unlikely to work — this fits my earlier plan to concede without improvement. Action: check and take it to showdown (fold to a raise).

On the river, I have improved to a pair of Nines. The board contains both a King and a Jack, which are very much in Minni's range after she called my pre-flop raise and flop continuation bet. Since Minni checked for a second time on the river, I have some showdown value. However, betting is risky because she is unlikely to call with a hand weaker than a Nine, and she likely would have bet her strongest hands earlier or will call with any King or Jack. Checking back is the most prudent play to see the showdown without risking more chips.

Folding A2o despite button position. This is a fundamentally weak hand that doesn't justify calling 100 chips. Position helps marginal hands, but A2o is below the threshold for profitable play even on the button. Need to preserve chips and wait for better spots in the final 28 hands remaining.

Heads-up from the small blind (button) I have Q♣5♦ (Q5o). This is a marginal hand but very playable in position against a single big blind. Limping (calling 50) is too passive — it gives the BB cheap odds and forfeits initiative. Folding concedes my already-small blind. With deep stacks and positional advantage postflop, I want to open a wide stealing range and apply pressure. A standard 2x BB open to 200 maximizes fold equity, builds a pot when I'm ahead of the BB's calling range, and keeps weaker hands in that I can outplay. If Flash 3-bets big I can fold (hand is often dominated); if he calls I’ll continuation-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously on ace- or queen-high boards. Action: raise to the min-open 200.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Jack offsuit (AhJs). Minni, in the Small Blind, has raised to 200 (a 2x open). AJo is a very strong hand in a heads-up situation against a Small Blind range. I have the advantage of acting last on every street after the flop. To capitalize on this strength and take control of the hand, I will put in a 3-bet. Raising to 600 is a standard sizing to build the pot and potentially end the hand immediately, while still being ahead of much of the range Minni would use to call.

I opened to 200 as a standard SB/button steal, but Flash 3-bet to 600. Q♥5♦ is a weak, easily-dominated hand vs a 3-bet range (Ax, Kx, broadway pairs). The pot is 800 and calling 400 requires ~33% equity — Q5o rarely has that vs a 3-bettor. A 4-bet bluff to 1000 is too risky here against deep stacks and a likely stronger range. I’ll preserve my stack for better spots and throw this away rather than invest more with a dominated hand.

On the button with Jc8d and two players to act (SB Flash and BB Claude). This is a marginal but playable hand in position — I want to take the initiative, apply pressure to the blinds and avoid a limp-heavy multiway pot. Standard open to ~3x the big blind gives good fold equity and keeps the sizing consistent with the table (min raise is 200 but that’s too small). I’ll raise to 300 total. If I face a sizable 3-bet I’ll fold (hand is frequently dominated); if I get called I’ll use position to c-bet or control pot size depending on the flop. Stack depths are deep enough to play postflop but there’s no reason to overcommit preflop with J8o.

I am in the Small Blind with 5d 2c, which is one of the weakest starting hands. Minni has opened to 3BB from the Button. Calling out of position with a hand this weak is not profitable, and there is no reason to bluff here. Folding is the most straightforward and correct play.

This is a straightforward fold decision. I hold Q5o (Queen-Five offsuit), which is a weak hand with poor connectivity and weak kicker. Even though I'm getting reasonable pot odds (450:200), the hand quality is insufficient. Minni is raising from the button with an aggressive style (evident from her frequent raises throughout the hand history). Flash folded from the small blind, which is standard. Q5o plays poorly out of position post-flop and has minimal win equity. This is a clear fold - no need to invest additional chips with such a marginal holding. Preserving my stack for premium hands is the correct strategy with 28 hands remaining in the session.

I am on the button with 6s 7c, which is a very weak offsuit connector. In a three-handed game against Claude and Minni, both of whom have shown aggressive tendencies and significant stacks, there is little merit in playing this hand. Attempting a steal with such low equity is risky, as both opponents have been willing to call or re-raise pre-flop. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Folding 4d 2h from small blind. While only 50 chips to call, this is one of the weakest hands in poker with virtually no value. Will be out of position post-flop against Minni. The attractive pot odds don't justify playing trash hands as a regular pattern. Looking to play disciplined poker and wait for better opportunities.

KQ from the button is a premium hand with excellent positional advantage. Raising to 300 (3x BB) applies appropriate pressure to the blinds, allows me to build the pot with a strong hand, and establishes control. This is a standard aggressive line from the button.

Holding 4♦7♥ in the small blind facing a button open to 300. I’ve already posted 50; calling would cost 250 more to play out of position with a very weak, often-dominated hand. Calling gives poor equity versus a typical BTN raising range (need ~26% equity to justify the call vs the immediate pot odds) and if the BB defends I’ll be OOP against multiple opponents. 3-betting as a bluff is too thin with this holding. Preserve the stack and fold this marginal spot.

Claude has raised to 300 from the button. My hand, Jack-Six offsuit, is quite weak to defend from the big blind, even in a three-handed game. While the price to call is 200 more into a pot of 450, the hand plays poorly post-flop and is likely dominated by Claude's opening range. Given I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have 9♠3♦ on the button — a garbage hand, but in three-handed play the button has significant steal value against the blinds. The pot is 150 and a cold call is 100; standard table open sizing here has been ~3x the BB. I’ll open-raise to 300 to apply pressure and be consistent with my previous opens (good fold equity vs both blinds). If I face a sizable 3-bet I will fold this hand; if I'm called I'll proceed very cautiously postflop and look to fold to sustained aggression unless I improve or get a clear read. Keeping the raise at 300 keeps the pot manageable while maximizing immediate fold equity.

In Hand #126, I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Six offsuit (Qs 6d). Minni, on the button, has opened with a raise to 300. While Q-6 is a marginal hand, I am facing a button open which is often wide. However, being out of position against Minni, who has a very large stack and has shown aggression throughout the game, makes playing this hand difficult. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to avoid getting into a potentially losing situation with a weak hand out of position.

Td5d is a weak hand with only ~33% equity against Minni's button raising range. While pot odds are 2:1, this is a marginal break-even spot at best. With only 24 hands remaining out of 150, folding to preserve chips and waiting for stronger spots is the prudent play. The hand has minimal equity and no made hand value.

I am on the button with Q7o. The pot is 150 and it is 100 to call as the small blind and big blind have posted their blinds (50 and 100 respectively). Q7o is a weak hand to open from the button, especially with two aggressive opponents like Claude and Minni who frequently 3-bet or call raises. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to avoid getting into a difficult post-flop situation with a marginal hand.

Folding Qs-4s from small blind vs big blind. Queen-four offsuit is a weak hand with a poor kicker, and I'll be out of position post-flop. Against an aggressive opponent like Minni, it's not profitable to defend with marginal hands. Maintaining a solid folding range in the small blind preserves equity and avoids difficult situations.

With 6c4c on the button, I'm facing a 100 chip call to see a flop in a 3-handed game. Key considerations: (1) Pot odds are favorable - I need roughly 15% equity to break even, and with position and two suited cards I likely have that; (2) The cost is minimal relative to my 11,100 stack; (3) Button position gives me significant advantage postflop; (4) In 3-handed play, standards loosen considerably; (5) Even weak hands have decent equity in multiway pots. This is a clear call - I should see the flop with the last-to-act advantage and let the hand develop from there.

Situation: 3-handed, I’m the small blind with 5♥4♠. Pot is 250 and it’s 50 to complete. Claude limped and the big blind is behind him — I’ll be out of position postflop and both opponents will have wide calling ranges. 5‑4 offsuit has very low equity and poor playability OOP; completing invites a multiway pot where I’ll be forced to fold most flops, and squeezing with this hand is a clear leak (it’ll often get called or reshoved). Preserve stack and fold — the 50 already posted is a dead blind, but folding here maximizes long‑term EV.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit. Claude has limped from the button and the small blind folded. While I could raise to try and take the pot immediately, checking to see a free flop is the most prudent play with a marginal hand like A2o, especially against a player like Claude who can be unpredictable. This keeps the pot small and allows me to play based on how the board develops.

I am in the big blind and checked pre-flop after Claude limped from the button. The flop is 6h-4h-3s, which is a low, somewhat coordinated board. I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5). In a limped pot out of position, checking is the standard play to see how the button reacts and to keep the pot small with a hand that has some equity but is currently weak. Claude has shown a tendency to overplay hands, so I want to avoid building a pot until I improve or have a better sense of his intentions.

I have two pair (6s and 4s) on a 6h4h3s board in a heads-up pot after Minni folded pre-flop. Flash checked from the big blind, indicating weakness. I should bet for value to extract chips from weaker hands, protect against flush draws (there are two hearts on board), and build the pot with my strong holding. A bet of around 160 chips (roughly 2/3 of the 250 pot) is proportional and appropriate for this spot.

I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw to the wheel on a low, coordinated board. Claude limped on the button and is now betting about 2/3 of the pot. Given the pot odds and the potential for my Ace to be the best hand or improve on the turn, I will call the 160 to see another card. This keeps the pot manageable while I have significant equity to improve.

The turn card is the Queen of Clubs, which is an overcard to the board. I am out of position and still hold Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5). Given that I checked and called on the flop, checking the turn is the most logical step to see how Claude reacts to the new overcard. If Claude checks back, I get a free card. If he bets, I will have to evaluate based on the bet size and my remaining equity.

Flash checked the turn after calling my flop bet, indicating weakness. With two pair (6-4), I have a strong made hand that's vulnerable to flush draws. I should bet for value to extract chips from weaker holdings (like Ax, low pairs, or pure draws) and charge Flash's likely hearts draw. A bet of 240 (approximately 42% of the 570 pot) is proportional and consistent with my flop sizing, getting thin value while keeping the bet size reasonable.

I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5). Claude limped on the button and has followed up with bets on the flop and turn. His turn bet of 240 into a 570 pot is less than half-pot, giving me decent odds to see the river. While my Ace might be the best hand if Claude is on a draw himself, I am mainly looking to hit my straight or hope for a check-down on the river if I miss. Given Claude's frequent betting, I will call this marginal amount to see the final card.

I have Ace-high and have missed my gutshot straight draw. The board runout ended with a Queen and a Ten, and the heart flush draw missed as well. Throughout the hand, Claude has been betting moderately, which could indicate a marginal made hand like a pair of sixes or a draw that he was semi-bluffing. Since I have no showdown value and am out of position, checking is the best play. If Claude checks behind, I might occasionally win against a missed draw, but I expect to lose to almost any pair. If he bets the river, I will fold, as my hand is too weak to call a third barrel.

Betting 300 chips on the river for value. Flash's check indicates weakness, and my two pair beats most of their likely holdings (ace-high, weak pairs, missed draws). This bet size (~1/3 pot) is a thin value bet that extracts chips from weaker holdings while respecting that Flash showed weakness with a check. Two pair is strong enough to warrant extraction on this blank river runout.

I have Ace-high with the lowest possible kicker (Deuce) and have missed my gutshot straight draw. The board runout (6h-4h-3s-Qc-Td) has seen Claude bet on every street after limping from the button. While Claude can be aggressive, a triple barrel usually represents at least a pair or a better Ace-high than mine. Given that most of his potential missed draws (like 7-5 or 5-2) actually beat my Deuce-kicker or have made a pair, and even missed heart flushes might contain a better Ace, I don't have enough showdown value to justify a call here, even with the decent pot odds.

Button with pocket 7s vs only the blinds — good spot to take initiative. I want to isolate and use position while preserving set-mining implied odds, so open to a standard 3x (300) rather than limp/call. 300 gives better fold equity vs the blinds, is consistent with table raising patterns, and keeps postflop play straightforward: c-bet if called, re-evaluate against a large 3-bet (fold to an overly large 3-bet, call smaller 3-bets to set-mine).

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Deuce suited. The button, Minni, has opened for a standard raise to 300. Ten-Deuce suited is too weak to defend out of position against an aggressive button open. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop without the initiative or a strong range, and raising as a bluff is not ideal with this hand. Folding is the best choice to preserve my stack.

5-8 offsuit is a weak hand with poor equity against Minni's wide button-raising range. Being out of position with this holding makes post-flop play difficult and unprofitable. I'm getting 2.25:1 pot odds, but the hand lacks sufficient equity to justify a call. Folding preserves my stack for premium situations with 21 hands remaining.

I am on the button with Seven of Spades and Deuce of Spades (72s). While being suited provides some playability, 72 is statistically the weakest hand in poker. In a three-handed game, standard opening ranges are wider, but 72s is still below the threshold for a profitable open, especially with Minni in the Big Blind, who has been quite active and aggressive throughout the session. Conserving chips for better spots is the priority as we approach the final hands of the game.

Folding marginal connector in small blind heads-up vs aggressive opponent. 7-8o plays better multi-way and with position. Minni's large stack and aggressive tendencies make this a poor risk/reward scenario. Conserving chips for stronger opportunities.

I'm holding 2s7s, which is the worst hand in poker - even with both cards being spades, this combination has minimal equity. While I'm in the button position (typically favorable for late-position play), 2-7 is simply too weak to justify entering the pot, even for the small 100-chip call. The hand ranks below virtually all other starting hands and will struggle significantly if called. With a healthy stack of 11,500 and 59 hands remaining, I should preserve chips for premium situations rather than speculating with this bottom-tier hand. Folding is the standard play here.

We’re heads-up from the small blind with Q♦7♣ — a hand worth playing aggressively in heads-up play. Folding is too tight and calling 50 leaves us out of position on the flop with little fold equity. I’ll open to a standard 3x the big blind (300 total) to take the initiative, apply pressure to the big blind, and avoid playing a marginal hand passively OOP. Flash defends reasonably wide, but with 20k+ stacks we have postflop room to maneuver and can fold to a large 3-bet if necessary. Total committed: 300 chips (we’ve posted 50, so +250).

I am in the Big Blind with King-Nine offsuit. Minni, in the Small Blind, has raised to 3 times the big blind. King-Nine offsuit is a strong enough hand to defend my blind, especially when I will have position for the rest of the hand against the Small Blind. The pot odds are 3:1, making a call profitable against a wide stealing range. I will call to see a flop.

We raised preflop and took the initiative, so a high-frequency continuation bet is standard here. The flop K♠ 9♥ 6♥ misses our Q♦7♣ completely (no heart, no pair, no backdoor straight), but Flash’s BB calling range is wide — many hands (overcards, weak pairs, floats) will fold to pressure. I’ll make a half-pot c‑bet (300 into the 600) to apply pressure and fold out those hands while keeping the size manageable if I get resistance. If Flash check‑raises heavily I’ll fold (I have very little showdown equity and am OOP). If he calls, I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously — rarely barreling without improvement.

I have flopped top two pair (Kings and Nines) on a board that is somewhat coordinated with two hearts. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor in the Small Blind, has led out with a continuation bet of 300 into a 600 pot. This is a very strong hand for me, and while I could raise now to protect against heart draws and get value from hands like Ace-King or Queen-King, I want to keep her range wide and allow her to continue bluffing or value-betting worse hands on future streets. I will call the flop bet to remain deceptive and maintain position.

I took the initiative preflop and barreled the flop, but the turn (8♥) bricks my range and makes the board much scarier — there are now three hearts on board. I have Q♦7♣ with no hearts, no pair and only 6 outs to improve on the river, so I have very little showdown equity versus a calling range that includes Kx, 9x, pocket pairs and possible two‑heart combos (made flushes). Per my earlier plan I should rarely barrel the turn without improvement.
