Checking controls the pot (current pot = 1,200), avoids getting stacked by a sizable turn raise, and lets me reevaluate the river: I can fold to meaningful aggression or call very small probing bets (only where pot odds justify chasing my 6 outs). Betting here would be a thin bluff into a range that frequently beats me, so I check.

On this turn, the Eight of Hearts completes a potential flush and several straights (like 5-7 or 7-10). While I have a very strong hand with top two pair (Kings and Nines), the board has become extremely coordinated and dangerous. Minni has checked, which could indicate she is pot-controlling a King, giving up on a bluff, or trapping with a completed flush. Given that I hold the King of Hearts, I have a blocker to the second-nut flush, but I still lose to any other flush. Checking back here allows me to control the size of the pot, see a river card for free, and potentially induce a bluff from Minni on the river if she was drawing or has a weaker made hand.

Board: 6h 9h Ks 8h 3d. I hold Qd7c — no heart, no pair (queen-high). Pot is 1,200 and action is checked to me on the river. Flash called my flop c‑bet and then checked turn and river, a passive line consistent with Kx/9x/medium pairs or some missed draws; many of those hands will call a river bluff or already beat me. Betting here has very limited fold equity and little value — I’m risking chips with virtually no showdown equity. I’ll check and take the showdown (and fold to any river raise), preserving my stack for better spots.

On this river, the board remains extremely dangerous with three hearts potentially completing a flush and several cards (7, 5, 10) completing various straights. Although I have a very strong hand with top two pair (Kings and Nines), Minni's decision to check for a second time suggests she is either giving up on a bluff, pot-controlling a weaker King, or potentially trapping with a monster hand. Given the coordinated nature of the board, betting for value is risky; if I am check-raised, I would be forced to fold a very strong hand. By checking back, I guarantee a showdown and maximize my chances of winning the 1,200 chip pot without risking further loss on a 'wet' board where many draws have arrived.

AK offsuit on the button is a premium hand — big equity and huge positional advantage. With only the SB and BB left and everyone deep-stacked, I want initiative and to build the pot while keeping the size reasonable. The table’s standard open size has been ~300, so I’ll raise to 3x the BB (300) to isolate and take control of the hand. If I get called I’ll play post‑flop aggressively in position; if I face a 3‑bet I’ll evaluate sizing and opponent before deciding to call or 4‑bet.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s 5s. Minni, who has been raising frequently from the button, has opened to 300. While 85s has some playability as a suited one-gapper, playing it out of position against a wide but aggressive range from Minni is difficult. With a stack of 88 big blinds, I prefer to fold and wait for a more advantageous situation rather than defending a marginal hand from the worst position at the table.

Calling with 6♥J♦ from big blind. Receiving 2.25:1 pot odds (450 to call 200) makes this a reasonable call. J6 has flop playability and I have position advantage postflop. Minni's button raise is likely a wide range. With 19 hands left and healthy stacks, seeing the flop to determine further action is profitable given the odds and hand potential.

Check with J6 on a three-heart flop (8♥ J♥ T♥). While I have a pair of jacks, the board is extremely flush-heavy and dangerous. My weak kicker (6) makes this hand vulnerable to domination. Checking allows me to see Minni's betting patterns before committing more chips. With 19 hands left and good chip position, there's no need to force this marginal hand. I can evaluate her strength and make better decisions on future streets with more information. This also protects my range by not always leading with top pair from the BB.

Flop is extremely wet: 8h Jh Th — three hearts. I raised preflop with A♦K♣ (actually A♠K♣) which is fine, but I completely missed this monotone flop and hold no hearts or pair. Claude (BB) checking here can be weakness or a trap, but his calling range preflop contains a lot of heart-suited combos that have already made the nut/near-nut flush and also many hands with decent equity. With ~19k behind vs a 11.2k caller and only a 650 pot, I don’t want to build a big pot with two overcards and poor blockers. Pot-control is best: check back, realize equity, and re-evaluate on the turn. If Claude leads small on the turn I can call to see a river; if he barrels big I’ll give up unless I pick up a pair or a very favorable card. If checked to me on later streets I can take a stab on a safe non-heart turn or river, but not here on this coordinated texture.

Betting 450 on the turn with a pair of jacks. The flop check from both players suggests Minni likely doesn't have a premium hand, making my pair of jacks a strong holding. The dangerous three-heart board means I should extract value from draws while building the pot when ahead. Betting protects my hand against free river cards and gives me information about Minni's strength. The bet sizing of 450 (70% of pot) is balanced - firm enough to discourage weak calls while not overcommitting if Minni shows strength. With 19 hands remaining and a healthy stack, this is a profitable spot to play my hand aggressively when likely ahead.

Call 450. I have top pair with the best kicker (A♠ K♣) after the King on the turn, but this is a very wet texture — flop was 8♥ J♥ T♥ so made two‑card heart hands (double‑suited hearts) already had the flush on the flop and single‑heart hands have big backdoor equity. Claude checked the flop and now fires a small 450 into a 1,100 pot, which looks like a probing/weak‑value size that includes thin value (Kx, Q9, Jx) and bluffs/semi‑bluffs. Raising into this texture is too risky — it bloats the pot against potential made flushes/straights and I don’t block many of those combos — while calling gives me good pot odds (≈22% equity needed) to see a river and keeps the pot controlled in position. Plan: call to see the river; on a non‑heart safe river I’ll extract value or shove depending on action and sizing; on a heart or a heavy bet I’ll strongly consider folding to large pressure.

River 9♠ with my J♦6♥ pair of jacks on an extremely flush-heavy board (8♥ J♥ 10♥ K♠). This is a check situation for several reasons: (1) My pair of jacks has a weak 6 kicker and is vulnerable to Minni's likely holdings—she called my turn bet on a three-heart flop, suggesting she either had a pair, was on a flush draw, or had a straight draw. (2) With no additional flush development on the turn and river, if she was on a flush draw it didn't complete, but she could still have K♥ (which would make trips), a straight with Q, or a better pair. (3) By checking, I allow her to bluff if she's on a missed draw, gain information if she bets (suggesting strength), or check behind if she has a weak hand. (4) With 19 hands remaining and holding a marginal hand on a dangerous board, chip preservation and information gathering are more valuable than forcing value. Checking keeps the pot manageable and allows me to make an informed decision on her river action.

The river (9♠) completes a five‑card straight on the board (8♥ J♥ T♥ K♠ 9♠). With K♣ A♠ my best five cards are just the board straight — I have no extra value to beat Qx/AQ straights or a heart flush. Claude’s line (check flop, a small turn bet, then check river) can be both bluffs/marginal hands and hands that actually beat the board; a river bet from me would be a thin bluff that will mostly be called by worse (if any) or — worse — by hands that beat me. I block some AQ combos with the A♠, but not enough to make a bluff profitable. With deep stacks and little fold equity, the correct, lowest‑variance play is to check and take the showdown.

I am on the button with Ace-Eight offsuit in a three-handed game. This is a very standard opening hand to play from the button to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot in position. I've been using a consistent 3x open (300 chips) throughout the game, and I will continue with that sizing here.

Folding 4-10 offsuit from small blind. This is a weak hand that doesn't merit getting involved against Flash's button raise, especially with the big blind still to act behind me. Preserving chips for better spots is critical at this late stage of the tournament (hand 133/150). The risk/reward is unfavorable - I'm out of position and vulnerable to a 3-bet or poor post-flop scenarios. Standard fold with weak hand from weak position.

I’m on the big blind with 5♦2♣ — the absolute worst playable hand. Flash opened to 300 from the button (pot 450) so calling costs 200 for a total of 300 committed; that gives me roughly 31% pot odds, but my real equity vs a button opening range is considerably lower and the hand plays terribly postflop (very few clean wins, lots of domination). A 3-bet as a bluff is possible in some spots, but with this hand I have no blockers and little fold equity vs an aggressive opener; it’s a pure leak to continue. I’ll preserve my 20k+ stack for better spots and fold preflop.

I'm on the button with J9o, a reasonable hand given my position advantage. The pot currently stands at 150 with only 100 more to call. 

Looking at opponent tendencies:
- Minni (SB) has been playing aggressively with frequent raises, but also folding to pressure
- Flash (BB) has 8,850 chips - the shortest stack at the table, likely to defend wider

J9 offsuit is a solid hand in late position. It has decent flop equity against a wide range and plays well post-flop with two overcards and potential straight possibilities. The math is favorable: I'm investing 100 into a 150 pot (3:1 odds). 

My 10,700 stack gives me room to play small pairs/speculative hands. Given the button position and reasonable pot odds, calling is the optimal play here. I can use my positional advantage post-flop to either win the pot or fold if I don't connect.

I'm in the small blind with J♦6♣ facing a limp from the button. The pot is 250 and it only costs me 50 more to complete to 100 total, giving ~6:1 pot odds (≈16.7% break-even). J6o is too weak and poorly positioned to raise profitably (risking a 3-bet and multiway action), but cheap to see a flop — it has enough equity vs limp/call ranges to justify the call. I'll call to see a flop and then play cautiously OOP: check/fold to significant aggression unless I hit a meaningful pair/draw. If I face a large raise preflop I’ll fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c-5d. Both Claude (Button) and Minni (Small Blind) have limped in, and I have the option to check. With a weak hand like 7-5 offsuit, there is no reason to raise and build a pot. I will take the free flop and see if I can hit something significant.

I missed this flop. With J♦6♣ on T♦ 9♠ 2♣ I have no pair, no clean draw (only a weak backdoor club), and very little equity. This is a three-way pot and I'm out of position to both opponents, so a bluff c‑bet is low EV — they call often and I have limited fold equity. I will check to control the pot and see a free turn. Plan: fold to any sizable c‑bet, but consider calling a very small bet (cheap to see the turn); if I pair or pick up a backdoor draw on the turn I can continue, otherwise let it go.

I have a very weak hand (7-high) on a T-9-2 board with no direct draws. Minni has checked from the Small Blind. There is no reason to bluff here into two opponents in a limped pot. I will check and hope to see a free turn card, but I am prepared to fold if there is significant betting action.

I flopped middle pair (9s) with a gutshot on a dry board (Td 9s 2c). Both opponents checked, indicating weakness. My pair of 9s is likely the strongest hand here. Position allows me to bet confidently and either win the pot now or extract value when ahead. Betting 150 (half-pot) is a standard aggressive flop bet that balances value and pot building.

I checked the flop as planned with J♦6♣ and Claude now bets 150 into the ~300 pot (pot is 450 including his bet). Calling 150 gives me ~3:1 pot odds (≈25% break-even). I have only 6 clean outs (3 jacks + 3 sixes), which is ~24.1% to hit a pair by the river — slightly below the break-even threshold. This is a three‑way spot, any pair I make is often second‑best, there are no realistic backdoor draws (a flush is impossible), and a bluff/raise OOP here is low EV. Given the math and my earlier plan to fold to significant c‑bets, I’ll fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

I have 7-high on a Ten-Nine-Deuce board. Claude, the Button, limped pre-flop and is now betting 150 into a 300 pot. Minni has already folded. I have no pair, no draws, and no equity in this pot. Calling 150 to win 450 with 7-high is a losing play in the long run, and there is no reason to bluff in a small limped pot given the stage of the tournament. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

On the button with T♥4♥ in a 3-way pot and deep stacks (202bb), I want to leverage position and fold equity. A standard 3x open (to 300 total) is a balanced play: it’s big enough to discourage loose callers and small blind steals, but small enough to keep SPR large if I'm called. Plan: raise to 300 to try to take down the blinds preflop. If I face a sizable 3-bet I’ll fold this marginal hand; if I get called, I’ll play aggressively in position — c-bet favorable heart/ten/paired flops and control the pot on dry boards, continue with strong draws or pairs. Winning the blinds + antes (150) outright or seeing a flop in position gives more EV than a straightforward limp/call here.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit. The Button (Minni) has raised to 3BB (300). Ace-Seven offsuit is generally strong enough to defend in a 3-handed game against a button open, but it doesn't play exceptionally well post-flop when out of position. However, with the current stack sizes and the tournament approaching its final hands (135 of 150), folding a hand as strong as A7o to a single raise would be too tight. I have two main options: call to see a flop or 3-bet to try and take the pot down immediately. Minni has been quite active with button raises. A 3-bet would be a strong play, but given Minni's large stack, she is likely to call. Calling allows me to keep the pot smaller while out of position. I will call the 250 more to see the flop.

With 8♠4♠ in the big blind, this is a clear fold. 8-4 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker - it has poor connectivity, is unsuited, and offers minimal playability post-flop. While I only need to call 200 more chips into a 700 chip pot (3.5:1 odds), the hand quality is too poor to justify calling out of position against two opponents already in the pot. Minni is an aggressive raiser from the button, and Flash called from the small blind, suggesting reasonable holdings. Folding preserves my stack and allows me to wait for better opportunities. With 15 hands remaining and a reasonable 10,800 chip stack, there's no value in speculating with marginal holdings.

On this board of 4c Jc Ah, I have flopped top pair with an Ace, though my Seven kicker is relatively weak. In this 3-handed dynamic, top pair is a very strong hand. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni), the most standard and effective play is to check to her. This allows her to continue her aggression with a wide range of hands, including bluffs, draws, or weaker pairs like Jacks. I am prepared to call a bet on this street to see how the turn develops, but I do not want to bloat the pot immediately given my vulnerable kicker.

I was the preflop aggressor and have position. Flop A♥ J♣ 4♣ with T♥4♥ in hand: I’ve hit a vulnerable bottom pair (4s) with a weak kicker but this is a paired flop that favors a BTN c-bet — it folds out many overcards, charges club draws, and extracts value from worse jacks and small pairs. I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal pair, so I’ll make a standard-sized, controlling c-bet (≈50% pot). If I face a sizable check-raise I’ll fold; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and control the pot.

I have flopped top pair on an Ace-high board in a three-handed game. While my seven kicker is relatively weak, top pair is a very strong hand in this short-handed dynamic, especially against a button open from an active player like Minni. I checked to the pre-flop aggressor as planned. Minni's continuation bet of 350 into a 700 pot is standard. Folding here would be far too tight, as I am likely ahead of her bluffs and many of her semi-bluffs (like club draws or straight draws). Raising would likely force out her bluffs and isolate me against better Aces (AK, AQ, AJ) or strong draws. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable while out of position and keeps her wider range of bluffs in the hand.

The King of Hearts on the turn is a significant card that interacts well with Minni's button-opening range, potentially giving her two pair (AK, AJ, KJ) or completing a straight with QT. However, I still hold top pair in a three-handed game, which remains a relatively strong hand. My strategy continues to be one of pot control and checking to the aggressor. By checking, I avoid bloating the pot out of position with a marginal kicker and give Minni the opportunity to check behind or continue bluffing with her draws. I will decide my next move based on the sizing of her bet if she chooses to lead.

I have T♥4♥ — a weak made hand (pair of 4s with a Ten kicker) but I now have a 4-card heart setup (Ah, Kh on board + my Th4h), so I need one heart on the river (~9 outs, ≈20%) to make a flush. Flash’s line (call pre, call a 350 flop c‑bet) is polarized toward Ax, Jx, medium pairs and club draws — many of those beat my pair or are happy to call another bet. The turn K♥ actually helps his range (Kx/two-pair combos) and makes barreling less profitable: betting here will bloat the pot with a marginal hand that has limited fold equity and will often be called by better hands. I’ll check to control the pot, preserve my river options, and realize my ~20% equity. If Flash bets a small/medium amount on the river I can call to see if I hit; if he shows weakness or the river is a heart I can transition to value/bluff accordingly.

On a board of 4c Jc Ah Kh Kd, I hold As 7d. My best five-card hand is a Two Pair, Aces and Kings, with a Jack kicker (As Ah Kd Kh Jc). In this 3-handed dynamic, this is a very strong hand, but there are several hands in Minni's button-opening range that now beat me or split with me. Specifically, I lose to any King (Trips), any Queen-Ten (Straight), or Ace-Queen (a better kicker, as the Queen would replace the Jack). I split with any other Ace (A2-AT) because the board's Jack acts as the kicker for both of us. I beat any hand that doesn't contain an Ace or a King, such as a Jack or a missed club draw. Minni's check on the turn suggests she might not have a King or a very strong Ace, but it doesn't rule them out. Betting into this board doesn't accomplish much, as better hands won't fold and weaker hands (like Jacks) might not call. Checking allows me to see a showdown or decide whether to call a potential bluff or value bet from Minni, while keeping the pot size manageable in a situation where a split is highly probable.

Check. On the river the board pairs kings (4c Jc Ah Kh Kd) and my T♥4♥ only has a single pair of 4s — a very weak made hand vs Flash’s line. He called my flop bet and then checked turn and river, which is consistent with pot-control with Ax, Jx or medium pairs, or with missed club draws; most of those hands beat my pair and will call a river bluff. Betting has low fold equity and little value (very few worse hands will call), so bluffing is thin and bloats a pot I’m usually behind in. With healthy stacks and no showdown cost, the lowest-variance, highest-EV play is to check and show down rather than risk a bluff that will be called.

I am on the button with 5h 6h, which is a strong speculative hand. Raising to 300 is a standard play to take control of the pot and leverage my position against the blinds. This hand has good post-flop playability if I am called.

Calling with 9♦J♦ from small blind against Flash's button raise. This is a strong hand with good multiway potential (straight and flush possibilities). Calling is better than folding a playable hand or 3-betting with a non-premium holding. The odds are favorable (1.8:1) and the hand plays well postflop where I can exercise positional skill after seeing community cards. I can take down the pot with aggression on favorable flops.

I have Ah8h in the big blind facing a BTN raise to 300 and a CO/SB call. The pot is 700 and I must put in 200 more (so my total commitment will be 300). Ah8h is worth defending: it’s a suited Ace (blocks some strong Ax hands) with decent nut-flush/backdoor potential. I don’t want to inflate a multiway pot with a marginal 3‑bet — a min 3‑bet to 500 is thin value and likely called by at least one opponent. Calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves postflop playability, and lets me evaluate the flop (I’ll play cautiously OOP vs the button but can be more aggressive vs the small blind when appropriate). Therefore I will call the remaining 200 (total commit 300).

Betting top pair with strong draw potential. I hold 9♦J♦ on a Jh-5d-6s flop, giving me top pair, a 4-card flush draw, and straight draw potential. With three players and 900 in the pot, a standard c-bet of 350 (roughly 1/3 pot) builds value, takes the initiative, and gains information. I'm first to act and should apply aggression with this hand. The bet sizes work well against both opponents - Minni will likely call with decent holdings, and Flash's shorter stack makes fold equity favorable.

I have A♥8♥ (nut-heart draw + ace blocker) and already committed 300 preflop. On this J♥5♦6♠ flop Claude has put in 350 (after the button’s preflop open), so I must call 350 more. Calling is the correct, low-variance play here: I’m getting ~22% immediate pot odds (350 into ~1,600) while my equity to make the flush by the river is ~38% (10 hearts left), plus I can pick up additional equity with an A or if opponents miss. Raising OOP in a 3‑way pot with a draw is thin — a raise would bloat the pot and commit chips without much chance to fold out two players. Folding throws away a very playable nut draw and blocker to strong Ax hands. Plan: call 350 more (total commit 300+350 = 650), see the turn, and proceed — barrel/raise if I hit the nut flush or top pair; fold to heavy action if I miss and face strong pressure.

I have flopped bottom two pair (5s and 6s) on a board of J-5-6. While bottom two pair is vulnerable, it is currently a very strong hand in a three-way pot. Claude led out with a donk bet and Minni called, suggesting they both likely have pieces of the board, most probably a Jack or a straight draw. I want to raise here for two reasons: value and protection. I want to extract value from Jack-x hands and charge straight draws (like 78 or 47) to see the next card. I will raise to 1100 to put pressure on my opponents while I have the best hand and position.

Calling with J♦9♦ on a Jh-5d-6s flop against Flash's aggressive raise. I have top pair with a strong 4-card flush draw (~12 outs total). The pot offers 3.5:1 immediate odds, and I have adequate equity against Flash's range. My top pair plus flush draw gives me roughly 40-50% equity in most scenarios, well above the break-even point. Minni is still in the hand, maintaining pot size and fold equity. Calling allows me to evaluate turn action while getting proper odds for my draw equity. This hand plays well postflop where I can assess final outcomes.

I have A♥8♥ on J♥5♦6♠ — that’s the nut (Ace‑high) heart flush right now. Two opponents remain and Flash (BTN) just raised the flop to 1,100 and Claude called, so both have shown willingness to commit. With the nut flush in a multiway pot I want to extract value (from sets, Jx, mid pairs, other heart combos) and charge any runner draws rather than passively checking and letting them see free cards. The min‑raise (1,850) is too small to get real value; I’ll make a larger, pot‑sized raise to ~3,500 on the flop to build the pot while still keeping worse hands and greedy callers in. Pot before my action is 3,450; raising the flop to 3,500 will meaningfully charge calls and protect my equity. That raise means my total commitment this hand will be 300 (preflop) + 3,500 (flop) = 3,800 chips. If I face a big 3‑bet/reraise I’ll re‑evaluate (sets/full houses are possible but rare); otherwise I plan to continue extracting value on later streets.

I have flopped bottom two pair (6-5) on a J-5-6 board in a three-way pot. While this is a very strong hand, the action on this flop is extremely concerning. Claude led into two players, and after I raised to 1100, Claude called my raise. Minni then back-raised (3-bet) to 3800. This line from Minni—calling a bet then 3-betting over a raise and a call—is incredibly strong, typically representing a set (JJ, 66, or 55) or at the very least a superior two pair like J-6 or J-5. Given that I hold two of the cards needed for the lower sets, her holding JJ, J-6, or J-5 becomes even more mathematically likely. In a high-stakes tournament setting, a 3-bet on the flop in a multi-way pot is almost never a bluff. I am likely way behind, and calling would commit a significant portion of my stack (nearly half) to a hand where I am potentially drawing to only four outs (to make a full house). With only 14 hands remaining in the tournament, I choose to fold and preserve my 71 big blinds for a more favorable situation.

Calling with J♦9♦ on Jh-5d-6s against Minni's aggressive re-raise. I have top pair with an exceptional draw package - 4-card flush draw plus straight possibilities providing ~14 outs. The pot offers 3.5:1 odds and I need 28% equity to break even. My realistic equity against Minni's likely range (sets, two pair, or broadway draws) is 30-40%, making this a profitable call. Even against a set, my draw outs provide sufficient equity. The flop action has built the pot meaningfully, and with strong draw potential, I'm in a position where I can improve significantly on the turn and river. Calling allows me to realize my equity while maintaining pot control awareness.

Checking on the turn with J♦9♦ against Minni's aggressive line. I have top pair with a powerful draw (flush + straight possibilities = 8+ outs). The turn card (Th) was neutral and didn't change the dynamic. By checking, I preserve chips while maintaining adequate equity - my top pair and draw give me roughly 40-50% equity, and a free river card is valuable. With my stack at 6,700 and pot at 9,600, I'm approaching commitment territory. Checking allows me to evaluate the river action while keeping fold equity intact. If she bets weakly on river, I can evaluate based on the final card. If I miss the draw, I can fold without excessive loss.

Heads‑up vs Claude after Flash folded. I have A♥8♥ — the nut heart flush on the turn (J♥ 5♦ 6♠ T♥). Very few hands beat me here (only full houses/quads), and Claude’s line (calling my big flop raise then checking the turn) looks like medium strength: Jx, Tx, single pairs or maybe a draw. I want to extract max value and deny free cards — Claude will call large with many worse hands. His effective stack is 6,700, so I’ll size to commit him: bet enough to put him all‑in and get called by Jx/Tx/paired hands and lesser heart combos. Pot is 9,600; betting the remaining 6,700 of his stack makes sense. That means my total commitment this hand becomes 3,800 (already in) + 6,700 = 10,500. If he shoves/calls I’ll likely be favorite; if he folds I scoop the pot.

Calling all-in with 9♦J♦ on Jh-5d-6s-Th. I have top pair with two strong draw options (straight + flush draws = 8-12 outs). The pot odds are 3.8:1, requiring only 25% equity to break even, and my realistic equity against Minni's range is 35-40%. I'm pot-committed at this stack depth (6,700 against 20,100+ pot), and folding top pair with this much equity is mathematically incorrect. The aggressive turn bet could indicate strength or a semi-bluff with draws, but either way, my equity justifies the call. This is a profitable all-in situation given the odds and my hand strength.

Heads-up on the button with J8s (both hearts) — a playable, suited jack that plays well in position. I want to take the initiative and steal/isolate the BB rather than limp; a standard 3x open (300 total) is a table-consistent size that gives fold equity, discourages cheap 3-bets, and keeps the pot manageable if called. I have a large stack (31750) vs Flash's 8100, so I can apply pressure later if appropriate, but I won't overcommit preflop with a marginal hand. Plan: raise to 300; if he 3-bets large I'll fold or carefully assess based on sizing/reads, if called I'll use position to c-bet selectively and control pot size.

We are now heads-up against Minni, who has a significant chip lead. In Hand #136, Claude was eliminated, leaving just two active players. With 8100 chips (81 big blinds), I have plenty of room to play and don't need to defend with marginal hands like 63s against a 3x open raise from the button. While 63s is sometimes a defend in heads-up play, Minni has shown high aggression and a large stack, making post-flop play difficult with a weak hand. I will fold and wait for a stronger starting hand to confront the chip leader.

I am holding Ace-Nine suited on the button in a heads-up situation against Minni. This is a very strong starting hand. I have a stack of approximately 80 big blinds. I want to raise for value and to take initiative in the hand, especially since I will be in position for the rest of the hand. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here.

Flash opened to 300 from the SB in a heads-up spot — a very wide range. I hold J♥7♥: suited, decent postflop playability and good enough to defend the BB. The pot is 400 and I need to put in 200 more (total 300), giving me ~2:1 pot odds to see a flop. With deep effective stacks and the button's wide opening range, calling is the standard, keeping pots manageable and allowing me to play position postflop. I'll call to 300 and evaluate on the flop — continue aggressively if I hit a pair/heart draw or if the board favors a bluff, fold or check-protect against heavy pressure when unimproved.

Flop: 3♥ T♦ 9♥ — I hold J♥7♥. I have a very strong drawing hand: a four‑card heart flush draw plus an overcard (J) and a backdoor/runner straight possibility. Raw outs are large (9 hearts + the remaining J/7/8 outs ≈ 15–18 unique outs depending on overlaps), so my equity to improve by the river is roughly in the ~55–65% neighborhood (raw equity; actual equity vs made Tens will be lower because some pair outs are still second‑best). Flash opened very wide from the BTN/SB, so his flop range contains a lot of overcards and missed hands that will fold to pressure.

Given I’m out of position and the pot is only 600, the correct line is to apply semi‑bluff pressure now to (a) take the pot down immediately against missed hands and (b) build a pot when I have good equity. A half‑pot size gives solid fold equity while keeping weaker hands in to be outdrawn. If he calls I still have strong draw equity; if he makes a small raise I’ll usually continue (good equity), but I’ll respect a large reraise and fold to avoid committing with only a draw.

Action: bet about half the pot (300). Plan: if called, evaluate turn — barrel/raise when I improve or get a free card when appropriate; fold or concede vs significant aggression if I miss turn and show weakness.

I have middle pair (Nines) with the top kicker (Ace) on a coordinated board of 3h-Td-9h. Minni has led out with a donk bet of 300 into a 600 pot. While she could have a Ten or a draw, my hand is too strong to fold at this stage, especially in position. Calling allows me to keep the pot controlled, maintain position, and see how the board develops on the turn without overcommitting against a potentially better hand like a Ten or a slow-played monster.

The turn 8♣ completes a 7-8-9-10-J straight for my J♥7♥ — I now have a very strong, made hand that beats almost everything except rare QJ combinations that make a higher straight. Flash opened wide pre and then called my flop half‑pot, so his calling range has lots of one‑pair hands (T, 9), Jx and broadways that will happily call a value bet. Flushes are not possible with only two hearts on board, so I should be extracting value rather than protecting. I’ll bet for value: ~2/3 pot (800 into 1200) to charge top‑pair/second‑pair and worse Jx hands while keeping the pot manageable against the few hands that can beat me. If he raises big I’ll re‑evaluate (likely fold to an overbet shove but call smaller raises), if he calls I’ll look to get more value on the river when appropriate.

I am heads-up with Ace-Nine suited and currently have middle pair (nines) with an Ace kicker. The board is quite coordinated with potential straights (Q-J, 7-6) and a flush draw. Minni led the flop and is now firing a second barrel of 800 into a 1200 pot. While the turn 8c is a card that connects well with many drawing hands, folding a pair of nines with the best kicker in position would be too exploitable in a heads-up match. Calling is the most prudent action to keep the pot size manageable, maintain position, and see how the river develops before committing more chips.

Turn put me on a made straight (7-8-9-10-J). The river Jd pairs the board but doesn’t change that — the only hands that now realistically beat me are holdings containing a Q (they make the higher Q‑J‑10‑9‑8 straight). Flash opened wide from the SB and then called both my flop (300) and turn (800) bets; that calling line is most consistent with one‑pair hands (T♥/T♦, 9x), Jx floats, some 7‑8/8x combos and only a subset of Qx. He checks the river, which more often signals weakness than a slow‑played higher straight. I want to extract value from the many worse hands in his range while keeping the pot manageable vs the few Q combos that beat me, so I’ll bet for value ~2/3 pot. Bet 2000 into 2800 to charge top/second pairs and weaker Jx; if he simply calls I likely win a decent pot, and if he raises big I’ll re‑evaluate (large raise is a strong indicator of a Q‑based straight).

The board has become extremely coordinated with the river Jack completing several straight possibilities (any Queen or Seven). Minni has led out on every street, showing significant strength. My pair of Nines, which was middle pair on the flop, has now been downgraded by the overcards and the straight potential on the board. Given that Minni's triple-barrel line often represents a very strong hand in this configuration and that many of her semi-bluffs would have improved to a better hand or a straight, calling a large river bet with only a pair of Nines is too risky. I will preserve my remaining stack for better opportunities in the final hands of the tournament.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with Ah7c — a very playable hand in heads-up. I want to seize initiative, use the Ace as a blocker to strong Ax combos, and apply pressure to the shorter-stacked BB (6600). The table standard open sizing is around 300, so I’ll raise to 300 (total) to build the pot, gain fold equity, and keep postflop play easy with position. Calling 50 is too passive and gives him good odds to see a flop; folding is unnecessary.

I am in the Big Blind with 52s, a speculative suited connector type hand. The pot is currently 400 and it costs me 200 more to call, giving me 2:1 pot odds. Minni has a significant chip lead and has been very active with pre-flop raises. While 52s is a weak hand, in a heads-up or short-handed scenario, getting 2:1 odds on a suited hand can be a marginal call to see a flop. However, given I only have 6600 chips (66 BB) and Minni is aggressive, I should avoid playing weak hands out of position. Nevertheless, for only 200 more chips and with a hand that can flop well (flushes, straights, two pairs), I will make the call to see if I can catch a piece of the flop and exploit any potential post-flop passivity if Minni misses.

I have bottom pair (Deuces) on an Ace-high board with some backdoor potential (spade flush draw and a gutter to the wheel). As the Big Blind, I should check to the pre-flop raiser, Minni, to see how she reacts to this board. Leading out here would likely only be called or raised by better hands, so checking allows me to control the pot and potentially see a turn card cheaply.

Flop: As 3d 2h — I hold Ah7c: top pair with a decent kicker in position. Flash (BB) called my preflop raise from a wide range and checked the flop, which makes his continuing range weighted toward worse Ax, small pairs, and speculative combos (4-5 straight combos are very rare). I want to extract value from worse aces and medium pairs and charge the few straight combos, while keeping the pot controlled vs the small number of two‑pair (A2/A3) combos that beat me. A half‑pot bet is the balanced line: gets called by worse hands, folds useless air, and leaves room to maneuver on the turn. Bet 300 into the 600 pot to build value and deny free cards.

I have bottom pair (Deuces) on an Ace-high board with a backdoor spade flush draw and a backdoor wheel straight draw (any 4 gives me a gutshot). Minni has a range advantage as the pre-flop raiser on an Ace-high board, but her bet of 300 into a 600 pot (1/2 pot) provides me with 3:1 pot odds. At this price, folding bottom pair is too tight, especially heads-up. I will call to see if I can improve on the turn or if Minni checks back, allowing me to potentially take the pot down later or get to a cheap showdown.

I have improved to three of a kind (trips) on the turn with the deuce of diamonds. This is a significant improvement to my hand. Since I am out of position against Minni, who has been aggressive as the pre-flop raiser and flop bettor, checking is the best play to trap and induce further bluffs or value bets from her Ace-high hands. Leading here would likely fold out her air and only get action from hands that have me beat or have high equity. I will check to see her reaction to the board pairing.

Board pairs the deuce on the turn (3d 2h As → 2d). I have top pair (Ah7c) but the turn creates several hands that now beat me (A2 two‑pair combos, A3 two‑pair combos, sets/quads with 3x or 2x). Flash checked the turn after calling my flop bet, which is a fairly neutral line — he can be wide, but there’s a non‑trivial portion of his continuing range that now has me crushed. Betting the turn would bloat the pot and invite value raises/all‑ins from those strong hands while only extracting modest value from worse A‑x hands; it also makes it easier to get exploited by a shove. Checking controls the pot, keeps worse Ax and some bluffs in his range, and preserves flexibility for the river (call a small bet, fold to a huge shove if necessary, or small value bet if he checks). With all that, I’ll check back and re‑evaluate on the river.

I have Deuces full of Threes (2-2-2-3-3), which is an extremely strong hand on this board (As-3d-2h-2d-3h). My hand beats any Ace-high hand (which makes Aces and Threes) and any pocket pair. I am only losing to a Three (which makes Threes full of Deuces), A-3, A-2, or A-A. Minni's turn check makes those monster hands less likely, as she would typically want to bet for value or protection on a paired board with an Ace. I will bet 600 (half the pot) to extract value from an Ace-high hand or a mid-to-high pocket pair that might suspect a bluff. If she has a Three, she will likely raise, but checking misses too much value against her likely range.

Board: 3d 2h As 2d 3h. I hold Ah7c — top pair on the flop but the turn/river paired both deuce and trey, so any hand containing a 2 or 3 now beats me (full house/quads). Pot before the river bet was 1800; Flash bets 600, making the pot 2400 and giving me pot odds that require ~25% equity to call (600/(2400)). Flash’s line (call pre, call my flop bet, check-turn, small bet on the river) is consistent with either a slow-played hand that turned into a full house (2x/3x) or a small value/targeted bet with weaker holdings — his range will include full houses but also many hands I beat or tie. Rough range work: a meaningful share of his flop-calling range has 2s/3s and now crushes me, but there are still plenty of non‑made hands and Ax combos (many of which will tie me) that I beat; my estimated showdown equity versus his calling range comfortably exceeds the 25% break‑even. Raising is thin and risks a large portion of his stack when he very well may have a full house; folding concedes too much given the pot odds and my equity. Therefore I will call the 600 to reach showdown.

I am holding 3d 2c, which is the weakest possible starting hand in poker. Although I only need to commit 50 more chips to see the flop from the small blind, the hand's equity and post-flop playability are extremely poor. Given the current stack sizes and the fact that we are nearing the end of the tournament (Hand 140 of 150), I prefer to fold and preserve my 7,850 stack to find a better spot to double up rather than bleeding chips with junk hands.

Heads-up on the button (I’ve already posted the 50 SB) with 6♠4♥ — a marginal hand but very playable in position. In heads-up I want to open very wide to seize initiative and pick up the blind; a standard 2x BB open is appropriate. Raising to 200 (total) costs me 150 more chips and builds a pot I can play in position while keeping the size manageable versus Flash’s ~77bb stack. I’ll c‑bet favorable flops and exercise pot control / fold to heavy resistance on bad textures. Action: raise to 200 (total).

In a heads-up situation, Ace-five offsuit is a strong hand well ahead of a standard button opening range. Minni has been active, and a min-raise from the button represents a wide range of hands. With 77.5 big blinds, I have plenty of room to play. I will 3-bet to 700 to take initiative, define Minni's hand, and potentially win the pot pre-flop. If called, I have an Ace and can play a variety of boards aggressively.

I opened wide to 200 in position with 6♠4♥ which is standard heads‑up, but Flash 3‑bet to 700 (I must call 500 more). Pot ≈900 so a call requires ~36% equity to break even. 6‑4 offsuit has poor raw equity and miserable post‑flop playability against a typical 3‑bet range (lots of value hands and some suited broadways) — it will be a dog in many matchups and rarely realize >36% equity even with position. 4‑betting/isolation with this hand is too high variance and unnecessary given my large stack advantage and the tournament stage. Fold and wait for a better spot in position or a better hand to apply pressure.

In a heads-up situation with 80 big blinds, King-high is a reasonable hand to open from the button. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds immediately. If Minni calls, I'll play the flop with position.

Heads-up vs Flash (button) who opened to 300 — likely a fairly wide stealing range. I’m in the big blind with 4♠6♥, a weak, often-dominated hand but it has some preflop equity against a wide open. The pot is 400 and I must call 200 more (total commit 300), so my break-even equity is about 33%. Against a wide button range 4-6o has roughly enough raw equity to justify a call. I don’t want to 3-bet with such a weak, non-blocking hand and then play a big pot out of position, so I’ll take a cheap flop and re-evaluate. Action: call 200 more (300 total).

Flop: 5♣ 4♥ 4♦ and I hold 4♠6♥ — I’ve flopped trips. Heads-up vs a wide button opener, so Flash’s range contains lots of one-pair hands, single 5s, overcards and some pocket pairs; only hands that beat me here are pocket fives (full house) or the one remaining 4 (quads), both quite rare. I want to extract value while keeping weaker hands in the pot and charging any draws, so I’ll make a half-pot c-bet. Betting 300 into the 600 pot is small enough to get calls from worse (A5, 5x, top pairs, etc.) but large enough to build the pot and deny free cards. If Flash raises reasonably I can re-evaluate/call; if he jams his short stack hard it will usually indicate 55 or quads and I’ll fold. Action: value bet to keep the range wide and control the size of the pot.

I opened King-high from the button and Minni called. The flop is 5-4-4, a very dry board. Minni has led out with a donk bet of 300 into a 600 pot. While K-high has some showdown value in heads-up play, K2o is the weakest possible King and has very little playability on future streets if I don't improve. Minni has a massive chip lead and can easily pressure me on the turn and river. With 77 big blinds remaining and only a few hands left in the tournament, I prefer to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better spot to commit chips rather than floating with air against an aggressive big stack.

Heads-up on the button (I posted the SB 50) with K♠T♠ — a strong, playable hand in position. Pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to see action. Opening is standard here: I want initiative, to narrow Flash’s BB calling/3-bet range, and to build a pot I can play last on postflop. Table norms have been 3x-type opens, so I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB) — big enough to be meaningful but not overcommit given Flash’s ~76.5BB stack. Plan: if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and apply pressure; if he 3-bets small I’ll consider calling to play postflop, if he 3-bets large/all-in I’ll re-evaluate and likely fold to avoid risking a large portion of my stack with KTs out of position preflop.

I am currently in the Big Blind with Jack-Six offsuit. Minni has raised to 3x the blind from the button. While I am getting 2:1 on a call, Jack-Six offsuit is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position, especially against a stack as large as Minni's. With only 8 hands remaining in the tournament and being significantly behind in chips, I need to be selective about the pots I enter. Defending with a marginal hand like this is likely to lead to a difficult post-flop situation where I may be forced to bleed more chips. I will wait for a better opportunity to commit my remaining 76.5 big blinds.

With only 7 hands remaining in the tournament and being significantly out-chipped by Minni, I need to be selective about the pots I enter. Queen-Five offsuit is a weak hand even in heads-up play. While I am on the button and have position, this hand doesn't play well post-flop and I'd rather wait for a stronger opening or a better opportunity to commit my remaining 76 big blinds.

Heads-up on the button with 2♣6♥ — a weak hand but position is huge. With a big stack advantage (32,350 vs 7,500) I should apply low-risk pressure to pick up blinds and antes. A minimum raise to 200 (total) costs me an extra 150 chips to try to take down the 150-pot preflop; if called I keep position and can play postflop cautiously. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold — this hand is dominated and not worth a big confrontation. The small raise maximizes fold equity while risking very little of my lead, so I raise to 200.

In a heads-up situation, Ace-deuce offsuit is a solid hand to defend the Big Blind against a small 2x open from the Small Blind. I am getting 3-to-1 odds to see a flop, and Ace-high often holds significant showdown value or can improve to top pair. Given the stack sizes and the fact that we are nearing the end of the tournament, I will call to see a flop and evaluate my hand's strength based on the board texture.

On this flop, I have flopped trips with an Ace. This is an extremely strong hand, but the board texture is very dry (Ad Ac 4h). If I bet now, I am likely to fold out all of Minni's bluffs and weaker hands. Since I am out of position, the best play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor and allow her to continue her aggression. If she bets, I will most likely just call to keep her range wide and trap, as a raise here would signal extreme strength and likely end the hand.

Check. The flop Ad Ac 4h is extremely wet for my range — any Ace in Flash's calling range (As/Ah) is now trips and will often be check‑behind or trap. My 2♣6♥ has no pair and negligible showdown equity; while a small C‑bet can pick up some folds, it also bloats the pot and gets me into a costly confrontation versus trips or strong Ax hands. With a massive stack advantage and only a few hands left in the tournament, pot control and playing in position is the prudent line: check to see the turn for free and fold to meaningful aggression, preserving chips for better spots and postflop leverage.

On this turn, I still have trip Aces. Minni's check-back on the flop suggests she either has a very weak hand, a small pocket pair, or perhaps a 4. The Jack of Hearts is a card that could potentially have hit her range if she has cards like KJ, QJ, or J10, or it might have given her a flush draw. Since I checked the flop to trap and she didn't bite, I will check again. This continues to disguise the strength of my hand and may induce her to bluff with her air or bet a Jack for value. If she checks back again, I will have to lead the river to ensure I get some value from this strong holding.

Check for pot control. With 2♣6♥ I have no showdown equity against the most likely holdings (an Ace or a medium pair) and Flash’s turn check could be weakness or a trap with an Ace. Betting here only bloats the pot and risks a costly confrontation vs trips or a strong Ax. I have a huge stack advantage, so I’ll preserve chips, see the river for free, and fold to meaningful aggression (only consider calling very small river bets).

On the river, the board is Ad Ac 4h Jh Th. I have trip Aces with a Jack-Ten kicker from the board, as my deuce does not play. Minni checked back on both the flop and the turn, which strongly suggests she does not have an Ace or a strong pocket pair. However, the Jack on the turn or the Ten on the river might have improved her hand to a pair she is willing to call a small value bet with. Since I have checked twice to disguise my hand, leading out now for a small amount (3/4 pot) looks like a thin value bet or a late stab at the pot, which might induce a call from a Jack, a Ten, or even a medium pocket pair like 77-99. If I check, it's highly likely she checks back and I lose out on value.

Call 300. I checked down earlier to control the pot and my plan was to fold to big aggression but to call very small river probes — Flash’s 300 into a 700 pot is exactly that: a cheap bet. Calling 300 costs under 1% of my 32,200 stack and I’m getting roughly 30% pot odds to see showdown. My 2♣6♥ has almost no value versus Ax/trips/quads, but folding to such a small probe is too conservative given my huge chip lead; raising would needlessly risk chips and could induce a shove. I’ll call to gain information and preserve my stack advantage rather than bloat the pot with a bluff or commit to a marginal spot.

I am holding KQo on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a very strong hand in this context. With only 5 hands remaining in the tournament and a significantly smaller stack than my opponent, I need to play aggressively when I have premium holdings to try and bridge the gap. A standard raise to 300 is appropriate to build the pot and take control of the hand.

Heads-up vs Flash (button/SB) who opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with A7o — a hand that fares very well heads-up and is ahead of a large portion of an opener’s range. I’ll 3‑bet to take the initiative and extract value or win the pot preflop: 900 is a sensible size (≈3x the open) — big enough to generate fold equity and isolate weaker holdings without overcommitting deep stacks. If he folds I pick up the pot; if he calls I still have a playable Ace-high with good redraws and can c‑bet favorable boards or pot-control on dangerous ones. Calling would let him keep positional advantage for free; folding is too tight. Raise to 900 to pressure his wide opens and build value.

In a heads-up situation with only 5 hands remaining in the tournament, KQo is an extremely strong hand. I initially raised to 300, and Minni 3-bet to 900. I have 7,800 chips remaining, which is roughly 78 big blinds. While I am the shorter stack, I still have plenty of playability. Calling the 900 allows me to see a flop in position with a strong holding without overcommitting my stack pre-flop against a potential monster. If I hit the flop, I can look to play a big pot, which I need to do to close the gap on Minni's lead before the tournament ends.

I was the preflop aggressor (3‑bet to 900) and we see a very dry Q♦ 2♠ 6♣ flop heads‑up. My A♠7♥ has an overcard and little chance to improve but the board misses a large chunk of Flash’s calling range (broadways, many A‑x, and missed connectors). I should continue to take the initiative and apply pressure — a c‑bet gets value from worse A‑x and denies equity to many of his holdings while folding out pure bluffs and unpaired cards. With the stacks available a ~1/2 pot bet is the right balance of fold equity and economy. If he calls I'll re‑evaluate on the turn (pot/control vs Qx or check back vs safe bricks). Bet 900 to seize the pot now.

I have top pair with a King kicker on a very dry board (Qd-2s-6c). In heads-up play, this is an extremely strong hand. Minni 3-bet pre-flop and is now leading on the flop for 900, which is a standard continuation bet. I have two main options: call to keep her bluffs and dominated hands (like JJ, TT, or a weaker Queen) in the pot, or raise to build the pot immediately. Given that there are only 5 hands remaining in the tournament and I am significantly behind in chips, I need to maximize value. However, calling in position is often the best way to induce further bluffs on the turn from hands like AK or AJ, which I currently crush. I will call the 900 bet, bringing my total commitment for the hand to 1,800 chips, and look to play a large pot on the turn.

Check. The turn (Q♦ 2♠ 6♣ 6♥) materially reduces my fold equity and makes a second barrel much less profitable. Flash called my flop c‑bet, so his continuing range is likely Qx, some pocket pairs and decent one‑pair hands — many of which now have decent showdown value and will frequently continue. My A♠7♥ is primarily showdown value with an ace blocker, not a hand I want to build a big pot with on a paired board. With the pot at 3,600 and his stack only 6,300, a bet here either gets called by most of his range or creates a committed shove situation I don’t want to face with a marginal hand. I’ll check to control the pot: call small turn bets to see the river and fold to large shoves, or if he checks back I’ll re‑evaluate the river and look for a thin value bet or a river‑bluff depending on the runout.

On the turn, the board has paired with a six (6h), and Minni has checked after 3-betting pre-flop and leading the flop. With top pair and a King kicker (KQo), I have an extremely strong hand in this heads-up match. Minni's check could signify a few things: pot control with a marginal hand like a medium pair (JJ-TT) or a weaker Queen, a complete give-up with missed high cards like AK/AJ, or a trap with a monster like AA/KK. Given the tournament situation (Hand 146 of 150) and the fact that I am significantly trailing in chips, I need to maximize the value I get when I have the best of it. Checking behind is safe, but betting allows me to get value from her marginal made hands or draws that might feel priced in to call. A bet of 1,800 (50% of the pot) is large enough to build the pot significantly if called, while still leaving me with some fold equity or room to maneuver on the river if the board becomes more dangerous.

Call 1800. I checked the turn to control the pot with A♠7♥ — it’s mainly showdown value and an ace-blocker, not a hand I want to turn into a bloated pot out of position. Flash called my flop c‑bet, so his continuing range is heavy with Qx, small pocket pairs (including 6x/2x sets), and some Ax or weak made hands — but also some thin bluffs. Facing 1800 into a 5400 pot gives me ~25% pot‑odds (1800 to win ~7200 after a call), and with a wide continuing range A7o has enough equity to justify a call. Raising here would build a pot I don’t want to play out of position with a marginal hand and would commit more chips unnecessarily against his short stack. Plan: call the turn, re‑evaluate on the river — fold to a large shove if he jams, call or extract thin value on a safe river runout, or check‑back when appropriate.

I’m on the river with A♠7♥ on Q♦ 2♠ 6♣ 6♥ T♥. Line: I 3‑bet pre, c‑bet flop, checked the turn and called his 1,800 turn bet. His line is weighted to value (Qx, 6x sets, some 22/TT, and some Ax), and he has only 4,500 behind into a 7,200 pot — a size that allows him to shove for value/pressure. My hand is a marginal ace‑high that beats air and some K/J high hands but loses to most of his natural continuing range. Betting here either folds out only a few hands I beat or induces a shove from hands that already dominate me; I don’t want to inflate the pot out of position with a very marginal hand. Preserve equity and avoid facing a difficult shove — check and take showdown (fold to an over‑aggressive shove if he jams, show down if he checks).

On the river, the board is Qd-2s-6c-6h-Th, and I hold KQo for top pair with a King kicker. Minni 3-bet pre-flop, led the flop, but has since check-called the turn and checked the river. This sequence strongly suggests she has a marginal made hand she is trying to take to showdown, such as a weaker Queen (QJ, QT) or a mid-to-high pocket pair (JJ, 99-77). While there is a small chance she is trapping with a monster like AA, KK, or AQ, the check-check line on the turn and river is more indicative of showdown value or pot control. With only 5 hands remaining in the tournament and being significantly behind in chips, I must maximize value with my strong hands. A shove of my remaining 4,500 chips into a 7,200 pot (roughly a 62% pot-sized bet) is a clear value play in heads-up. She is likely to find a call with any Queen or potentially even a hand like JJ given the pot odds and the aggressive nature of the match.

Pot is 11,700 and he shoved his last 4,500 — I need roughly 4,500/(11,700+4,500) ≈ 28% equity to break even. My A♠7♥ is just Ace‑high on Q♦ 2♠ 6♣ 6♥ T♥. Flash’s line (open, call 3‑bet, call flop c‑bet, bet the paired turn, shove river) is heavily value‑weighted: Qx, 22, 66 (sets/quads), and other medium pocket pairs or tens that beat me. The hands I realistically beat (missed Kx/Jx or tiny A‑x like A3–A5 that didn’t pair) are a small portion of his continuing range. Given the board texture, his short‑stack pot‑commitment and the required ~28% equity, my hand is behind — calling would be a marginal, speculative call out of position. I preserve a big stack and fold to this all‑in.

Heads-up on the button with pocket eights — a very strong hand in this spot. I want to take the initiative and build the pot while using position postflop. A standard 3xBB sizing to 300 (total) balances value extraction and fold equity against Flash’s wide calling/raising tendencies; it forces him to put in 200 more to continue. If he calls I’ll play the flop in position; if he 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold or call depending on sizing and reads).

Holding King-Six offsuit in the big blind, I am facing a standard raise to 300 from Minni. With pot odds of 3:1 (200 to call into a 600 total pot) and a hand that has reasonable high-card value in a heads-up situation, a call is standard to see a flop. I will look to hit a King or favorable board texture, while remaining cautious given Minni's significant chip lead.

On a flop of Jack-Ten-Four with two clubs, I have King-high and the King of clubs, giving me a backdoor flush draw and one overcard to the top pair. As the big blind who called a pre-flop raise, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. I will see how Minni proceeds before deciding whether to continue with my overcard and backdoor equity.

Heads-up on a checked flop (Js Tc 4c) with pocket eights. My hand is a medium-strength pocket pair: ahead of a lot of Flash’s wide calling range (weak pairs, floats, many overcards) but behind any Jx/Tx. I want to take the initiative and extract value while keeping the pot controllable — a small c-bet (~1/3 pot) is best here. It gets value from worse pairs and draws, folds some overcards, and denies free cards. If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (slow down if a club or a jack/ten shows, continue barreling on safe bricks). If he check-raises large I will respect it and likely fold.

On a flop of Jack-Ten-Four with two clubs, Minni makes a small continuation bet of 200 into a 600 pot. I hold King-high with the King of clubs, giving me one overcard to the board and a backdoor nut flush draw. The pot odds are very favorable (4:1), requiring only 20% equity to continue. Folding would be too tight against a wide button range and a small sizing, so I will call to see the turn and potentially realize my backdoor equity or improve to a top pair.

The Ace of hearts on the turn is a significant card that favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Having called the flop with King-high and a backdoor club draw, the appearance of the Ace reduces my equity significantly. I will check to Minni and see how she reacts, though I am likely folding to any further aggression given the board texture and her range advantage.

Flop I c-bet small and was called, so Flash’s range is wide but contains a lot of Jx/Tx, A-x and club draws. The turn A♥ is a big overcard that significantly reduces the equity of my pocket eights — many hands that called the flop now beat me or improved. With position and a 1,000-chip pot I don’t want to bloat the pot with an underpair into a range that has many better hands. A turn bet would be called by most hands that beat me and fold only the worst of his range, so it’s thin for value and exposes me to raises. I’ll check to control the pot, keep the initiative advantage post‑river, and re-evaluate based on the river card and Flash’s action (call reasonable river bets vs bluffs/weak pairs, or fire a small river stab on a safe card).

On a board of Js-Tc-4c-Ah-Jh, I have King-high. Minni checked the turn and now checks the river. While King-high has very little showdown value, it can occasionally win against missed draws (like lower club draws or gutshots) that were part of Minni's small flop c-bet range. Bluffing in this spot is unlikely to be profitable because Minni's double check could indicate a weak showdown hand (like a Ten or a Four) that she might not fold to a single river bet given the small pot size and her significant chip lead. I will take the free showdown and preserve my stack.

Board: Js Tc 4c Ah Jh. I hold 8h8s — river gives me two pair (Jacks and Eights). Opponent (Flash) called my flop c-bet then checked the Ace on the turn and checked the river as well, which narrows his line toward medium-strength hands/missed draws (Kx/Qx, weaker pairs, club floats) rather than clear value hands like Ax, Jx or Tx that often bet for value or would have shown more aggression. I’m ahead of a lot of his calling range but still crushed by any Jack, Ace or Ten. Against a single opponent in position, the correct play is a small value bet to extract calls from worse hands (Kx, Qx, weaker pairs, some rivered second-pair hands and missed draws) while avoiding bloating the pot against the many hands that beat me. I’ll bet roughly half the pot to maximize thin value and make any river bluffs pay. If he raises big I’ll re‑evaluate (most large raises will represent boats/trips or strong two‑pair lines and I’ll give it up).

On a board of J-T-4-A-J, my King-high has almost no showdown value. Minni's check on the turn suggests she might have a medium-strength hand like a Ten or a Four, and her river bet of 500 into 1000 looks like thin value. Since almost any pair in her range beats me, calling with King-high is too risky given the board texture. I will fold and preserve my stack for the final few hands.

We are currently on hand 148 of 150 in a heads-up situation. I am in the Small Blind with 9c 4d, which is a very weak hand. While calling only requires an additional 50 chips, this hand performs poorly post-flop and Minni has shown significant aggression throughout the match. With only three hands remaining, including this one, I prefer to fold this junk hand and preserve my stack for a potentially better spot in the final two hands rather than getting involved in a pot where I am likely behind.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with 9s5d — a marginal hand but very playable in position. Pot is 150 and I only need 50 to see a flop, but open-raising is standard and profitable heads-up because of the wide stealing window and my positional advantage. The table has a history of 300 opens, and a raise to 300 gives good fold equity while still keeping the pot manageable with my deep 28800 stack. Plan: if called, use position to c-bet dry boards, check/float or pot-control on coordinated boards, and fold to a large, committed 3-bet. Raising also continues my usual aggression and pressures Flash’s wide defending range, so I raise to 300.

With only two hands remaining in the tournament and holding 3h 2d, one of the weakest starting hands in poker, there is no reason to defend the Big Blind. I am out of position against Minni, and even with a 2:1 price to call, the hand's equity and playability are too low to justify continuing. I will fold and see if the final hand offers a better opportunity to move chips.
T8o is a solid starting hand in heads-up play. Being on the button, I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and either win the blinds or play the pot with position.

Heads-up preflop vs the button's open to 300. I hold K♥7♠ in the big blind — a marginal but playable hand heads-up. Pot is 400 and I must call 200 more; calling 200 to win 600 (after my call) gives ~33% break-even equity. Versus a typical wide button opening range I have reasonable equity and can play postflop for value or fold when out of line. 3-betting here with K7o is thin and bloats the pot out of position; folding is too weak given the pot odds and heads-up dynamics. Plan: call the 200 (300 total committed), see the flop, and proceed cautiously — look to value bet when I pair my King, fold to heavy action on bad textures, and use position/stack leverage if the spot presents itself.

I have K7o on A♠2♣6♣ — no pair and no club draw. This flop heavily favors the preflop raiser’s range (lots of A‑x and club draws), and I’m out of position, so leading with a marginal hand just bloats the pot and will get me called or raised by most hands that beat me. I’ll check to control the pot size and preserve my large stack; checking lets me see how Flash reacts (I can fold to heavy aggression, call small continuation bets to float and reevaluate, or take a free turn if he checks back). As the chip leader on the final hand, avoiding unnecessary risk with a one‑pair‑out/king‑high is the +EV line.

On this Ace-high board, I have a significant range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor. Since Minni checked, I will follow up with a standard continuation bet of 300 (half pot) to represent the Ace and take the pot down immediately. With only Ten-high, my best chance to win is to fold out her air.

I call to a total of 600. I’m out of position with K♥7♠ on A♠2♣6♣ — no pair and no club draw. Flash was the preflop raiser and c‑bet a standard ~300 into the ~600 pot; folding a small bet here is too weak given heads‑up pot odds and their wide c‑betting range. Raising OOP with no pair/draw just bloats the pot and is -EV. Calling 300 (total committed 600) keeps the pot manageable, lets me float and evaluate the turn, and preserves my large stack in the final hand. Plan: fold to heavy turn aggression, call small turn bets to try to take the pot away on a blank turn, or only value-bet/continue if I pair my King or the action becomes clearly favorable.

Board: A♠ 2♣ 6♣ J♣. I hold K♥7♠ — no pair, no club. Pot 1,200; I’m the chip leader (28,350) vs Flash (10,450). Flash was the preflop raiser and has a range heavily weighted toward A‑x and club combinations. The J♣ turns the board three‑club, which either gives the raiser the nut flush (if he holds two clubs) or a very strong protection/continuation range. Betting out of position with air here is a low‑EV bluff: it will get called by A‑high hands and club draws and will be crushed by any made club flush. Raising is pure risk with virtually no fold equity. I want to control pot size on the final hand and avoid turning a marginal situation into a big pot. Action: check and fold to meaningful aggression on this street — only consider calling a very small probe bet, but otherwise preserve my large stack and see a cheap river if Flash checks back.

The turn Jack of Clubs is a very coordinated card that completes several potential flush draws. I currently hold the Eight of Clubs, giving me a flush draw (four to a flush). Given that Minni called my flop continuation bet on an Ace-high board, she likely has at least a pair or a draw herself. By checking back here, I take a free card to realize my equity and potentially hit my flush on the river without risking a check-raise from a made flush or a strong Ace. Since it is the final hand of the tournament, preserving the stack and taking the high-realization line is a prudent strategy.

Check. I hold K♥7♠ and have missed the entire board (A♠ 2♣ 6♣ J♣ 5♥). Flash was the pre‑flop raiser and bet the flop, then checked the turn — that line is consistent with a slow‑played Ace or a variety of marginal hands and small pairs. The turn made the board three clubs (so only two‑club hole combos have a flush), and the river 5♥ is a blank that doesn’t improve my hand. Pot is only 1,200 while both stacks are deep (I’m the chip leader). Betting as a bluff here has poor fold equity: Flash can profitably call small with A‑high, any pair, or many showdownable hands, and a raise would be a needless gamble out of position. The lowest‑variance, +EV line is to check and take showdown — preserve the big stack and avoid bloating the pot with K‑high. If Flash barrels into me, I’ll fold unless the bet is trivially small and gives excellent pot odds.

On this final hand of the tournament, I hold Ten-high with the Eight of Clubs. The board is Ace-high with three clubs and a Jack. After raising pre-flop and continuation betting the flop, I checked back the turn to take a free card for my flush draw. The river is a blank (Five of Hearts), and I have missed my draw. Minni has checked for the third time, but since she called my flop bet on an Ace-high board, she likely has some showdown value like a weak Ace, a Jack, or a small pair. While Ten-high rarely wins at showdown, my check-back on the turn makes a river bluff represent a very narrow range of value, and it is unlikely she will fold any pair. Therefore, I will check and concede the pot if beaten, preserving my stack for the final count.
